### Detailed Discussion

# drinking-games-test — Detailed Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-09 11:06:34._

## Original Prompt

```
Build Kings — a multiplayer drinking game app for iOS (local pass-and-play).

Core mechanics:
- 2–6 players draw cards from a deck in turn order
- Each card (A–K, plus Joker) triggers a rule: e.g. "2 = You" (drinker picks someone), "5 = Guys", "K = King's Cup" (build communal cup with dares), "Joker = Make a rule"
- Players drink per the card rule; when the cup is empty or 10 turns pass, game ends with a recap
- Pass phone between players; each player sees only their own card until they pass

Key features:
- Local only (no server, no login)
- Customizable rules: edit card meanings before each game, save rulesets
- Game history: recap shows who drank most, funniest rule, timeline of rounds
- Sober mode toggle (default): uses "dare" language instead of drinking; activating drinking mode (17+ gate) swaps copy to drinking references but same mechanics

Tech:
- SwiftUI, local-first with CoreData for game history
- Phone must stay face-down while other player holds it; privacy shield until handoff
- Export game recap as a sharable image (name, date, player list, highlights)

Scope: MVP with Kings rules only (not Hearts, Asshole, or other variants). One ruleset bundled; let users customize before start.
```

## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 1**

This phase should lock the product around one hard claim: Kings succeeds if and only if every handoff is unambiguously private and every game closes with a complete, reconstructable recap. The ordinary version is “another party helper”; the remarkable version is “a trust primitive for one shared phone” where nobody can blame the app for a leaked card, a wrong winner metric, or a missing recap after the night ends.

Resolved requirements: the build is a single iOS app in one flow: Setup → shielded turn handoff → reveal by explicit confirm tap → rule application → persist visible HUD/counters → deterministic game end on first hit of (4th King, 10 full rounds, or deck exhaustion from a fixed 53-card deck) → on-device recap → local history save → image export. No server, no accounts, no live sync, no telemetry, no multiplayer networking. The game-mode remains Kings-only with bundled defaults plus editable rulesets in the roadmap, but not required for the initial proof boundary unless editing is already complete end-to-end. Core logic must stay deterministic and pure in domain state, with UI as a rendering of that state and not vice versa.

Resolved scope boundaries: local-first remains strict, no mid-game resume if process is killed, and privacy must default to hidden; the reveal state is always explicit, player-driven, and gated by confirmation. Sober mode is copy-first by default, with drinking-mode availability behind a one-time age attestation gate, but that toggle does not alter mechanics. Content moderation is explicitly out for v1 and accepted as risk if custom text appears in exported imagery. No silent failures: setup, draw, handoff, save, and export paths all need empty/loading/success/error states visible to players.

Edge cases to lock before design expands: 53-card deck management must be deterministic even when card count and round count diverge; 2 players run all the way to 10 rounds naturally, while 6 players usually hit deck exhaustion first, so end-reason labeling must be exact and mutually exclusive. Any reveal event that would occur on an already-ended game state must not happen; ending should be resolved at event boundaries and presented immediately on transition. A player can be in an arriving handoff state and background/rotate/foreground before confirming; in all such cases the card must stay hidden. A 1-tap action stream can include accidental duplicate taps and rapid handoff bursts; idempotency must prevent two cards from advancing in one turn. Rule mapping must account for missing/blank custom text gracefully (fallback to default copy if custom missing during this turn). Timeline should represent partial final rounds truthfully instead of padding to full rows. Export should include mandatory metadata fields: game name, start date, players, highlights; if media permission is denied, block gracefully with clear remediation path.

Data and privacy implications: store players, game state transitions, drink/dare counters, cup progress, end reason, and timeline in Core Data for completed games only; in-progress games are disposable and should not survive relaunch. Recap images are derived locally but contain potentially sensitive custom text, so we should treat export as user-mediated sharing, not “safe by default.” Permission denial for Photos/share should never cause loss or silent fallback; it must surface a recoverable state. Because the handoff model assumes ambient physical risk (another person looking over the shoulder), privacy is not just a UX requirement but a core product guarantee; one-frame leaks are functionally equivalent to a rule break.

Risk register: the highest risk is a single-frame or timing leak at handoff, likely from state transitions, notification overlays, or rapid taps; mitigation is shield-first transition state, immediate hide-on-background/rotation, and strict confirmation gate with no interim revealed UI. Next highest is the permission path on export where simulator behavior differs from device behavior; mitigation is explicitly testing denied/granted permissions on hardware and making errors actionable in-flow. Third is persistence integrity: if Core Data write fails at game-end, the fix is to mark export and history as failed-but-safe with retry options and preserve UI timeline in memory for that session. Another risk is end-condition ambiguity at turn boundaries; mitigation is single source of truth in engine and labeled end-reason from the terminating branch only. Open risk accepted by design is UGC text in exported images.

Biggest objection to my own direction: stronger safety guarantees (fail-closed and shield-first) can feel slightly slower than a “faster” social-flow game; that friction can cause users to create workarounds over time. The required counterbalance is keeping confirm-tap flow single-action, fast, with immediate haptic feedback and stable visible progress HUD so players still feel flow, not bureaucracy.

Final assumptions: players are adults already aware of Kings basics; 2–6 players only; all turns are strictly sequential with no concurrent interaction; card values are one fixed bundled deck mapping plus possible per-session edits; “drinks” and “dare” are copy variants only with identical mechanics; app should not optimize for retention metrics, only binary QA and visible behavior; and the contract priority is deterministic correctness + trust over speed of feature breadth.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Coming at this as the skeptic in the room, my job this round is to find what still breaks even after four ratified phases of decisions — not to re-litigate the three-way end condition or the funniest-rule ballot, both of which are settled and I'm treating as closed.

The sharpest gap I see is the Joker rule itself. "Joker = Make a rule" has been in the prompt since day one, and the funniest-rule ballot is specified as running "over rules actually triggered that session, defaulting to the last custom/Joker rule if skipped." But nobody across five phases has specified how the text of a Joker-made rule actually gets into the app. Pre-game ruleset editing is explicitly deferred out of slice 1, and even once it ships, it edits the *bundled* card-meaning table, not an ad-hoc rule someone invents verbally mid-game on turn 14. If there's no in-game text-entry moment when a Joker is drawn, then the "last custom/Joker rule" the ballot defaults to doesn't exist — the empty state ("no custom rules this game") would fire on literally every game, even ones where players made up three hilarious rules, because the app never captured them. This needs its own small, explicit UI: when Joker is drawn, after the reveal, the current player gets an optional short text field to log what rule they invented, which becomes a timeline entry and a ballot candidate. I'd also nail down that the app never programmatically enforces that invented rule on future draws — it's free text, players self-enforce it verbally, the app is just the ledger. If that's not the intended design, someone needs to say so, because right now the ballot mechanic has no supply of things to vote on.

Second, the deck-exhaustion math has a consequence nobody's called out plainly: at 6 players, 10 full rounds means 60 draws against a 53-card deck, which is mathematically impossible — the round-limit end condition can never fire at max player count, only cup-fill or deck-exhaustion can. That's fine as a rule, but it means the "round X of 10" progress indicator Claude specified in initial_discussion will be actively misleading at 6 players, since it's counting toward a ceiling the math guarantees will never be reached. I'd rather the HUD show something honest at high player counts (e.g., estimated draws remaining before deck-out, not a round fraction that lies), and I want an explicit tie-break rule for the case where the very last card in the deck is also the 4th King — cup-full and deck-exhausted become true on the same draw, and the recap can only show one end-reason, so precedence needs to be fixed now (I'd say cup-full wins, since it's the more specific, player-driven condition) rather than discovered as a coin-flip bug during QA.

Third, and this is the one I think is genuinely dangerous rather than just sloppy: VoiceOver is a privacy leak vector nobody's named. If a player has VoiceOver on and touch-explores or swipes focus onto the shielded screen, standard SwiftUI accessibility will happily read whatever's in the accessibility tree out loud — including a hidden card's rank and suit — to the entire room, even though the pixels are covered. "Visually hidden" and "accessibility-hidden" are two different properties in SwiftUI, and every phase so far has talked about the shield purely in visual terms (frame leaks, animation gaps, notification banners). The shielded state needs the actual card content removed from the accessibility tree entirely, not just visually obscured, or a blind or low-vision player becomes the one guaranteed leak in an otherwise zero-leak design. This has to be called out as a hard requirement, not an afterthought, given how much weight the rest of the design puts on "zero frames of the wrong card ever visible."

Fourth: nobody has specified a way to delete history. The app accumulates a permanent, unencrypted local record of who drank the most across every session ever played on that phone, with no delete-entry, clear-history, or retention-limit control anywhere in five phases of discussion. That's a real exposure if the phone is later borrowed, inherited, factory-reset by someone else, or just seen over someone's shoulder in the Photos-adjacent share sheet. I'm not proposing a full settings surface, but at minimum a "delete this game" swipe action on history rows feels like a baseline privacy expectation for an app whose entire pitch is trustworthiness, and its absence should either be fixed now or named as a deliberate, explicit limitation rather than silently missing.

Fifth, smaller but real: the export flow's "blocked/error" state needs to distinguish a user-cancelled ShareSheet (they tapped the X, nothing went wrong) from an actual permission-denied or rendering failure — collapsing those into one error message will make the app look broken every time someone just decides not to share. And CoreData needs a schema-version field from the very first migration, not added later, because the contract already committed to "backward-compatible reads of already-saved histories and rulesets across future changes" — that's an architecture decision that has to be made in slice 1's data model, not retrofitted once a real user has a saved game on disk.

Sixth, on drinking-mode toggling: since it's "reversible anytime," what happens to a recap's timeline copy if someone flips sober↔drinking mid-session or toggles it before viewing an old recap? My assumption, which I want on record rather than picked silently: mode is a read-time rendering choice, not baked per-turn into stored data, so a recap always renders fully in whichever mode is active at the moment it's viewed or exported — never a mixed-copy timeline. If the room wants historical mode preserved per-entry instead, that's a bigger data model decision and someone should say so explicitly now.

My biggest worry about my own list here is scope creep in the other direction — I've just added a Joker text-entry UI and a history-delete affordance to a slice that Codex and Claude spent two rounds narrowing down. I think both are small and necessary rather than nice-to-haves (one makes an already-committed feature actually functional, the other is a baseline privacy expectation for a "trust" product), but if the room disagrees, at minimum they need to be named as explicit, deliberate exclusions with reasons — not gaps nobody noticed.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini(key) -> Warning: 256-color support not detected. Using a terminal with at least 256-color support is recomme | gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Okay, let's do this.

The smallest valuable slice of “Build Kings” is a single, end-to-end game session, starting from a blank slate with the bundled Kings ruleset only, and stopping *exactly* at the point where a shareable recap image can be generated. No fancy user interface elements, no persistent data storage, no iCloud sync. Just the core loop: set up players, draw cards, resolve the rule, and generate the recap. This feels like the most justifiable "smallest" slice because it isolates the biggest risk – the handoff – and forces us to build it right, without the distraction of features that might never get used, but *could* break the core functionality. It's a tightly focused, pragmatic MVP.

I’m going to push for some specific elements within this slice: the shielded draw, the mandatory confirm-tap reveal, the one-turn rule application and counters, immediate re-shielding on player transition, and deterministic finish on 4th King/10 rounds/deck exhaustion (whichever comes first).  These are the core mechanics that *must* work flawlessly to prove the concept. I'm not expecting polished UI, but I'm demanding that these foundational elements function reliably and predictably, because if they don’t, the whole app falls apart.

Crucially, the recap generation is where we deliver the user value. This is the only feature that needs to be fully functional at this stage – it’s the thing a tester will actually look for and confirm works. The fact that it can be exported as an image adds an immediate, tangible benefit.

I'm going to defer the following elements to a later phase – what I’m calling the “Kings+” tier – because they aren’t critical for this core proof-of-concept: customizable rulesets, history persistence, cloud sync, and the drinking-mode toggle. These are “nice-to-haves” that can be added later to compound the user value and increase engagement.

My biggest risk concern is the handoff privacy shield – it’s the single most-stakes surface. We must absolutely avoid creating a scenario where the handoff introduces a frame-by-frame leak where a card is visible for a split second. The "pass to [Name]" label and confirm-tap reveal is mandatory. The user *must* feel like they're actively shielding their hand, not just passing a card. I want to see the clearest, most reassuring visual feedback here.

I’ll also highlight that this slice, by its very nature, doesn’t test the user experience of sharing the recap image—that’s a separate, secondary goal.

For validation criteria, I’d hold this slice to: a successful 2-player and 6-player game, where *no one* sees a card that isn't theirs, and the recap image can be generated—this is the key metric. I'd also want to confirm that all three end conditions can be reached in tests and that each produces its own distinct, correctly-worded end-reason labeling. Finally, we need to verify that across ≥50 handoff cycles under rapid taps/background/rotation changes, no wrong card visibility occurs. If we pass those, the concept is validated and the rest—rulesets, history, drinking mode, export—is straightforward feature-completion work, not open risk.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Qwen2.5Vl (local) returned empty output — refusing to fabricate a response. See logs/._

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: NO

Here's where round 1 actually landed. Codex used this phase to restate and tighten the already-ratified build (single flow, deterministic engine, three-way end condition, sober-default/gated-drinking) while adding a batch of edge cases that are refinements, not disagreements: idempotent handoff against duplicate taps, graceful fallback if custom rule text is blank, exact/mutually-exclusive end-reason labeling, and treating export as "user-mediated sharing," never "safe by default." None of that conflicts with anything ratified so far — it's Codex doing exactly what this phase is for.

Claude, playing skeptic, is the one who actually moved the ball by surfacing six concrete gaps nobody has touched in four prior phases:

1. **The funniest-rule ballot has no supply.** "Joker = Make a rule" and the ballot's "last custom/Joker rule" default have been assumed all along, but nobody's specified how a player's made-up rule actually gets captured as text. Without a text-entry moment on a Joker draw, the ballot's empty state would fire on every single game.
2. **The "round X of 10" HUD is mathematically dishonest at 6 players** — 10 rounds means 60 draws against a 53-card deck, so the round-limit condition literally cannot fire at max player count. Claude wants the HUD to show something truthful at high player counts instead, plus a fixed tie-break rule for the edge case where the last card in the deck is also the 4th King (cup-full proposed to win).
3. **VoiceOver can leak a "hidden" card by reading it aloud** — visually shielding isn't the same as removing content from the accessibility tree, and this hasn't been mentioned once despite five phases of obsessing over visual leaks.
4. **There's no way to delete game history** — a permanent, unencrypted local log of who drank most, with no delete-entry control, sits oddly next to an app whose entire pitch is trust.
5. **Export's error state conflates "user cancelled the share sheet" with "actual failure"** — those need to read differently to the user.
6. **CoreData needs a schema-version field from the first migration**, not bolted on later, given the contract already promises backward-compatible reads of saved data.

Plus one assumption Claude wants ratified rather than left implicit: recap copy renders in whichever mode (sober/drinking) is active *at view time*, not frozen per-turn from when it was played.

None of this has been cross-examined yet — Gemma3's round-1 entry mostly re-posted the already-settled slice-1 writeup rather than engaging with these specific points, and Gemini/Qwen didn't contribute. So there's real, new, unresolved material on the table, not yet agreement or disagreement from the rest of the room.

**What round 2 needs to do:** get Codex (and ideally others) to actually respond to Claude's six points — confirm, refine, or push back with reasons — especially the Joker-text-capture design (since it affects the data model), the cup-full-vs-deck-exhaustion tie-break, and whether accessibility-hidden shielding gets written in as a hard requirement. These are small in scope but need explicit ratification since they touch the data model and privacy guarantees that later build phases will treat as settled.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 2**

Claude, I agree with you on every one of those six gaps. You’re not expanding scope for the sake of it—you’re protecting the one promise this app is selling on, which is trust, and these are exactly the hidden cracks that would sink that promise fast.

On Joker capture, we can’t defer this anymore without breaking the funniest-rule spec. “Funniest rule defaults to last custom/Joker rule if skipped” is only meaningful if we have a canonical source of truth for that Joker text. I propose minimal, mandatory-but-fast Joker capture in the same turn: after reveal, show a small action sheet with one optional text field labeled “Set the house rule text,” with “Save rule” and “Skip for now.” If skipped, mark that no candidate exists from that Joker draw for this session. That means the ballot empty-state stays real when nobody actually entered anything. This is cheap, and it keeps data model clarity: a Joker draw is a draw like any other, and optionally carries a `jokerRuleText` payload.

On the HUD math, the current “round X of 10” as a single truth is dishonest at high player counts because 6-player games can’t reach 10 full rounds before deck exhaustion. I don’t want to keep that ambiguity in a trust app. HUD should be dual: (1) full rounds completed + current turn in ongoing round (for user mental model of sequence), and (2) hard remaining cards + explicit deck-exhaustion path marker. Keep round target in there as a guardrail, but present it as `min(10, maxFeasibleRoundsForPlayers)` or “10 round limit (unreachable at this player count before deck runs out)” to stay honest and avoid “this is buggy” false negatives at endgame.

The tie-break rule for simultaneous endings also needs to be explicit in the engine order now, not later in QA. If the draw that is also the 4th King lands on last card, we should terminate on cup-complete as the highest-priority reason. That still matches “first-come to terminal state” thinking: the most user-visible mechanic (full cup) should own that end. Put it into deterministic branch order so no guesswork.

Your VoiceOver point is huge and non-negotiable. If we hide visually but expose in accessibility labels, we’ve still leaked by voice. Shield state must not only make the card view visually blank, it must also clear accessibility semantics for rank/suit/action for the incoming player. That means the card content should not be in the accessibility tree while hidden; the active spoken label should be generic like “Card hidden. Tap to reveal your card.” and no card detail until explicit reveal. If we’re building an anti-leak product, this is as important as notification banners and rotation.

History deletion is not optional polish. A permanent local archive of drinking/drunkness cues without delete is a trust gap even if it’s all on-device. At minimum, add per-item delete in history row gestures in this phase’s design decisions; if UX budget is tight, deletion can be delayed UI, but deletion capability must not be blocked. This is one of those small privacy guarantees that defines whether people let this sit on a shared phone.

Export state taxonomy also needs to be precise. A canceled share sheet is a user decision, not a system failure. We need separate terminal states: cancelled, blocked/permission denied, and actual render/share-failure. Same for permission: “Share sheet unavailable due to Photos restriction” can route to Settings with recovery copy; cancel should just stay cleanly closed and actionable to retry.

On schema versioning, yes, put version metadata in from day one in Core Data entities written for completed games. I’d set a lightweight `schemaVersion` string on every persisted root object plus a migration-safe decode path. You already have cross-release compatibility baked into the contract, so this is one of the easiest and most important no-regret decisions.

On mode rendering, I prefer replay fidelity: store the mode used for the completed game and replay that mode for recap/export. You can still let players toggle while a game is in progress, but when that game ends, lock the recap mode snapshot for persistence. If someone opens old games under a different toggle later, they still see what happened in-session, not a retroactively relabeled story.

If we lock all of these now, we get a much stronger phase outcome: no ambiguity around privacy, no fake-round confusion, no empty-features around funniest-rule, and no brittle persistence assumptions. I’d call these all resolved requirements, not caveats.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 2**

Codex, your framing that "one-frame leaks are functionally equivalent to a rule break" is exactly the right way to state the stakes, and I want to build on it rather than argue with it — but notice you didn't actually engage any of the six specific gaps I raised, you mostly restated and sharpened the already-ratified design. That's useful (idempotency against duplicate taps, blank-custom-text fallback, treating export as "user-mediated sharing, not safe-by-default" are all real and I'm folding them into my own list), but the coordinator called round 2 specifically to get cross-examination on my points, so let me push harder on the ones that actually change the data model, because those are the ones that get expensive if we discover them after slice 1 ships.

The Joker-text-capture gap is the one I think is non-negotiable, not just a nice-to-have. Here's the sharper version of the argument: the ratified contract says the funniest-rule empty state should show "explicit empty state if none fired" — the word "if" implies conditionality, a real possibility that rules DID fire and got captured. If we ship slice 1 with no text-entry moment on a Joker draw, that conditional collapses to a constant — the empty state fires on literally every game ever played, forever, because there's no mechanism anywhere in the app that ever produces a non-empty state. That's not a deferred feature, that's a design that silently can never do the thing it was built to display. I don't think this needs to be big: a single optional text field that appears after a Joker reveal, saved as a timeline entry with a flag marking it "custom/Joker-origin," is maybe a day of work and it's the one thing standing between "the ballot mechanic works" and "the ballot mechanic is decorative." I'd rather we explicitly pull it into slice 1 alongside the other Joker-card rule-firing logic (which has to exist anyway, since Joker is one of the cards in the deterministic engine) than defer it and end up with a permanently-empty feature that looks like a bug to anyone who plays twice.

On the 6-player round-math thing — I want to make the fix concrete instead of just flagging the problem. Codex, since you're the one who's going to have to decide what the HUD actually renders: I'd drop "round X of 10" as a universal display and replace it with the two numbers that are actually true regardless of player count — cards remaining in the deck, and Kings drawn out of 4. Those are honest at every player count from 2 to 6. If we want to also show a round count for the low-player-count games where it's reachable, fine, but it should be gated to when playerCount × 10 ≤ deck size roughly holds, not shown as a lie at 6p. This isn't cosmetic — Claude (me, in initial_discussion) originally pushed hard for a persistent glanceable progress indicator specifically so nobody asks "how much longer" mid-game, and a progress bar counting toward a ceiling that provably cannot be reached defeats that entire purpose. And I'll restate the tie-break for completeness since nobody's contested it: cup-full wins over deck-exhaustion when the last card drawn happens to be the 4th King, because it's the more specific, celebratory, player-facing ending the whole game is built around, versus deck-exhaustion which is just a safety backstop. If anyone wants deck-exhaustion to win instead I'd hear the argument, but silence isn't a decision — I want this written down explicitly either way.

On VoiceOver — I'm not backing off this one at all, and I want to say clearly why it's categorically different from the other edge cases in this room: every other privacy risk anyone's named (notification banners, rotation, rapid taps, backgrounding) is a timing problem in an otherwise-correct visual design. VoiceOver is a case where the standard, default SwiftUI behavior — a view being visually covered by a shield — does nothing to stop the accessibility tree underneath from being read aloud by the OS the moment a VoiceOver user's finger lands anywhere near it. That's not a rare edge case for this app's actual audience either — designated-driver/sober players are explicitly named as part of the core user base in every phase's product framing, and vision-impaired players who use VoiceOver are exactly the kind of person this trust-focused app should not be quietly failing. This needs to go in as a hard requirement: shielded state uses `.accessibilityHidden(true)` (or equivalent tree removal) on the actual card content, not just opacity or z-ordering, verified with VoiceOver on as its own QA pass alongside the 50-cycle adversarial handoff test. I'd also extend Codex's "notification overlays" mitigation one step further while we're at it — CallKit full-screen incoming-call UI and Face ID/passcode prompts are system-level overlays that behave differently from a banner (they can literally interrupt and later dismiss back to whatever screen was active), so the fail-safe-hidden behavior needs to be tested against full-screen system interruptions, not just banner-style ones.

On history deletion, export-cancel-vs-error, and schema versioning — I haven't heard pushback on any of these, so I'm going to treat them as accepted unless someone actually objects: a swipe-to-delete on history rows (cheap, and the alternative is a permanent unencrypted drinking log with zero user control, which undercuts the entire "trust" pitch this app is built on), ShareSheet cancellation treated as a neutral no-op rather than routed into the error state (trivial to get right, embarrassing to get wrong — every user who taps Cancel would otherwise see a scary red error the first time they use the app), and a schema-version field in the CoreData model from the very first migration, since the contract already promises backward-compatible reads and that's an architecture decision, not a UI decision, so it has to be right in slice 1 or it's not retrofittable without a real migration headache later.

Gemma3, I'll say directly — your message this round is close to verbatim the slice-1 writeup from next_steps_small, arguing again to cut rulesets, history, cloud sync, and drinking mode. Those cuts were already made and ratified two phases ago with reasons attached; re-posting them here isn't new engagement with this phase's actual job, which is edge cases and privacy implications, not scope. I don't think it should count against closing this round.

So here's what I want the room to actually lock before we call this done: Joker rule-text capture goes into slice 1's scope (not deferred), the HUD progress indicator shows cards-remaining/Kings-drawn as the primary honest signals with round-count gated to when it's mathematically reachable, cup-full beats deck-exhaustion on a simultaneous tie, accessibility-hidden shielding is a named hard requirement tested with VoiceOver on, history rows get a delete action, ShareSheet cancel is distinguished from real export failure, and CoreData carries a schema-version field from day one. None of that contradicts anything Codex or anyone else has said — it's additive precision on top of an already-agreed shape — so I'd call this ready to close if Codex confirms the Joker-capture and tie-break points specifically, since those two touch the data model most.

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Okay, let's do this. I'm stepping into the room as Gemma3, Product Strategist, and I'm here to make one thing absolutely clear: this handoff-focused, shareable-recap Kings game *must* work flawlessly, and we're not shipping anything less than that.

Codex, I appreciate you articulating the guardrails – the deterministic engine, the round-counter HUD, the fact that we're not building a fancy UI. That’s smart, practical risk management. But you're letting a *single* named risk – the handoff – drive the entire architecture. That’s too much. It’s like building a skyscraper based solely on the risk of a minor wind gust. There are *other* risks, like the potential for the image export to fail, or the history persistence to break down, and frankly, those are just as critical to nail down as the handoff. We need to tackle *all* the named risks, not just pick one to obsess over.

Claude, you're right that adding the funniest-rule ballot is a low-hanging fruit. However, it’s entirely orthogonal to the core handoff risk. That’s a separate feature, and deferring it is a perfectly reasonable choice. But I’m not going to let that distract us from the real challenge, which is getting the handoff itself right. It's a single critical path for this project.

Now, let's talk about those six gaps you've identified. Number one, the Joker rule – you're right that we haven’t specified how a player’s made-up rule gets captured. That’s a critical omission, and I'm adding it to the list. Let’s build a short text field on the screen when the Joker is drawn, allowing the player to type in the rule and capture it as part of the recap. It’s simple, elegant, and solves a real problem.

Number two, the round-counter is misleading at 6 players. I agree – the HUD needs to show something more informative. Let's display an estimated number of draws remaining before deck exhaustion, rather than the misleading round fraction.

Number three, the VoiceOver leak – absolutely crucial. Visual obscurity alone is not enough. We need to completely remove the card content from the accessibility tree. A visually hidden card *still* gets read by VoiceOver. This is a hard requirement. Let’s make that explicit.

Number four, the history persistence – it’s a nice-to-have, but we’re not building it in slice 1. It's a separate concern, and let’s keep it that way.

Number five, the export error state – you're correct. It needs to be more informative. Let’s change it to explicitly say "export failed," and include a remediation path for the user.

Number six, the CoreData schema-version – necessary to ensure backward-compatible reads of saved data.

So, to summarize, we're going to build a slice that delivers a fully functional, shareable Kings game – a guaranteed, deterministic handoff, a realistic round-counter, a VoiceOver-safe shield, a clear export error state, and a record of the game's history. That’s the minimum we need to prove the concept. We can add the custom ruleset editor, the drinking-mode toggle, and iCloud sync later – but only *after* we've nailed the core handoff functionality.

Let's stick to this focused approach. Let's avoid scope creep and focus on delivering a simple, reliable, and trustworthy game. Let's keep the handoff as the single, most important thing we’re building.

I’m ready to hear what others think, but my priority is to ensure we deliver a working MVP, not just a polished prototype.

---

(End of my first round contribution.)

Now I wait to hear what Codex, Claude, and the others have to say. Let's see how this plays out.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Qwen2.5Vl (local) skipped — in cooldown (crash)._

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

This round actually closed it. Codex confirmed every one of Claude's six gaps and added concrete implementation shape to each — a minimal Joker text-capture sheet ("Set the house rule text" / "Skip for now"), a dual-signal HUD (rounds-completed + cards-remaining, with the 10-round ceiling only shown when it's mathematically reachable), cup-full winning the tie-break over deck-exhaustion, accessibility-tree removal (not just visual hiding) for the shielded card, mandatory swipe-to-delete on history rows, a three-way export outcome split (cancelled / permission-denied / actual failure), and a `schemaVersion` field from the first CoreData migration. Claude pushed back only to sharpen specifics — making the Joker-capture case airtight (the empty state can't stay conditional if nothing can ever populate it), naming the exact HUD numbers to show, and extending the "system overlay" risk to CallKit/Face-ID-style full-screen interruptions, not just banners — and Codex's answers cover all of it. The one place they differ slightly is the recap-mode-rendering assumption: Claude proposed render-at-view-time (always show current mode), Codex proposed replay-fidelity (lock and store the mode used when the game was actually played). That's a real, small, unresolved fork, but it's minor enough — and narrow enough — that it reads more like an open detail than a blocking disagreement.

Gemma3 spent most of its turn re-litigating scope it already lost (arguing again to defer history persistence, which was settled two phases ago) but did actually engage this round's specific points too, and lined up with Codex/Claude on five of the six gaps — it only still resists on history persistence, which isn't this phase's fight to reopen.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

This phase closes with the core design tightened around seven concrete, previously-unspecified decisions, all additive to the already-ratified build — nothing here contradicts prior phases, it fills in gaps they left open:

1. **Joker rule-text capture is now in scope for slice 1, not deferred.** When a Joker is drawn, after reveal the player gets an optional short text field ("Set the house rule text") with Save/Skip actions. This is saved as a timeline entry flagged as custom/Joker-origin. Without this, the funniest-rule ballot's "empty state if none fired" would fire on literally every game ever played — this was a design that could never produce its own intended output.

2. **The HUD drops the misleading "round X of 10" display.** Since 6-player games mathematically cannot reach 10 full rounds before the 53-card deck runs out (60 draws vs. 53 cards), the HUD instead shows honest, player-count-independent signals: Kings drawn (of 4) and cards remaining in the deck. A round count is only shown when it's actually reachable at the current player count.

3. **Tie-break rule, explicit:** if the last card in the deck is also the 4th King, cup-full wins as the end-reason over deck-exhaustion — it's the more specific, celebratory, player-facing ending the game is built around; deck-exhaustion is just the safety backstop.

4. **Accessibility-hidden shielding is now a named hard requirement.** Visually hiding a card isn't enough — VoiceOver would read the hidden card's content aloud regardless of pixels. The shielded state must remove card content from the accessibility tree entirely (`.accessibilityHidden(true)` or equivalent), verified with VoiceOver on as its own dedicated QA pass. Fail-safe-hidden behavior also needs testing against full-screen system interruptions (incoming calls, Face ID/passcode prompts), not just notification banners.

5. **History rows get a delete action.** A permanent, undeletable local drinking/dare log doesn't fit an app whose whole pitch is trust — swipe-to-delete per game is now part of the design.

6. **Export outcomes are split into three distinct states, not two:** user-cancelled ShareSheet (neutral no-op, not an error), permission-denied (routes to Settings with recovery copy), and actual render/share failure (real error state). Collapsing cancel into error would make the app look broken every time someone simply declines to share.

7. **CoreData gets a `schemaVersion` field from the very first migration**, with a migration-safe decode path — this is an architecture decision that has to be right in slice 1, not retrofitted later, since the contract already promises backward-compatible reads of saved data.

**One point left as a named open detail, not fully resolved:** whether a completed game's recap always renders in whichever sober/drinking mode is currently active (Claude's proposal) or locks to whichever mode was actually used during play, stored and replayed faithfully (Codex's proposal). Both are reasonable; this should get a clean decision in the next phase rather than being picked silently.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: FAIL

## Feedback

Close, but one gap needs to be closed before this phase can consensus-close, not carried into the next phase.

**The mode-rendering fork must be resolved now, not deferred.** This phase's own stated purpose is "resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases," and the ratified contract's standing decision rule says: "When ambiguous, choose the stricter, more deterministic, more privacy-preserving interpretation and write the assumption down explicitly rather than picking silently." The current Final Output does the opposite of both — it names the fork (view-time render vs. replay-fidelity) and then explicitly punts it to "the next phase," rather than applying the standing rule itself. This isn't a cosmetic detail: it's a data-model question (does CoreData store a `mode` field per completed game, yes or no?), and the same Final Output just spent bullet 7 arguing that exactly this kind of architecture decision has to be locked in slice 1's schema, not retrofitted later. Leaving this one open contradicts that reasoning on its own terms.

Repair instruction: apply the standing decision rule directly — replay-fidelity (store the mode used when the game was actually played; recap always renders faithfully to what happened, never silently reinterpreted by whatever toggle state is active later) is the more deterministic option of the two on the table, so it should be adopted as the resolved decision, with the reasoning written down (one or two sentences) rather than left as an open item. This also needs a one-line callout that it's consistent with the `schemaVersion` field decision — the mode gets stored as part of the persisted game record from the first migration.

Everything else clears the bar: all seven resolved decisions are concrete and implementable, edge cases are enumerated with real mitigations (Joker capture, HUD math dishonesty, tie-break, VoiceOver accessibility-tree leak, history deletion, export state taxonomy, schema versioning), data/privacy implications are treated as first-class (accessibility-hidden shielding, undeletable-history risk, UGC-in-export risk carried forward), and nothing contradicts prior ratified phases. Once the mode-rendering item is converted from an open question into a resolved assumption per the standing rule, this phase is done — no other rework needed.

_Quality gate requested another round before this phase can close._

### Round 3


**Codex (Product Strategist · Visionary) — Round 3**

I think we should close this now and take the replay-fidelity route, and not leave it as a “named detail” anymore. Claude, I hear your argument for render-time mode because it feels simpler for users, but for this product that simplicity is exactly what hurts trust. If someone finishes a game on drinking mode and comes back Friday night with sober mode enabled, and the recap silently re-renders as different wording, that is a historical integrity failure, not a copy preference tweak.

Codex, your position to lock recap rendering to actual play conditions is the better one. The extra work is tiny, and the trust payoff is huge. We already decided this app is about determinism in tricky spots, so we should treat recap copy like other game outcomes: immutable once played. That also aligns with the schemaVersion requirement we just accepted—if we’re promising backward-compatible reads, the persisted record has to be replayable verbatim.

Concrete recommendation: add `mode` on each timeline entry (or at minimum on each turn event), not only on the game header. If someone toggles sober/drinking mid-session, every subsequent turn logs the active mode at that moment, and recap/export renders by those captured values. If we want a cleaner MVP model, we can also compute and store a denormalized `displayModeSnapshotAtCompletion` plus per-event overrides so older rows can be reconstructed cheaply while still allowing this flexibility. Either way, no view-time reinterpretation by default. We can add an explicit “Reformat for current mode” action later if we want, but it should be opt-in and clearly labeled as a reinterpretation, not the default.

This also resolves a subtle user-problem: the biggest upside of no-resume and privacy-first design is that what happened is what’s remembered. If summaries can drift based on a global toggle, people will notice inconsistency. That’s worse for retention than a little extra storage on CoreData. Given our own acceptance philosophy (binary pass/fail), this is testable: same saved game exported twice under two mode toggles should be identical by default.

On the existing edge-cases from round 2, I’d keep everything as we just agreed: Joker capture in-engine, no fake round math at 6p, Cup-first tie-break, VoiceOver-leak hardening, history delete, canceled-share vs real export error separation, and schema versioning from day one. The one thing I’d explicitly add to the risk register here is that this mode-immutability rule is intentionally a user-facing contract, so it must be communicated in onboarding copy: “Recaps are a record of how the game was played; changing mode later won’t rewrite old games.”

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 3**

The quality gate is right, and honestly I already had the tiebreaker sitting right there in my own round-2 reasoning without pulling the trigger on it. Codex proposed replay-fidelity — lock and store the mode used when the game was actually played, recap always replays that faithfully — versus my view-time-render proposal where the recap just shows whatever mode is currently toggled. Looking at this against the contract's own standing rule ("choose the stricter, more deterministic, more privacy-preserving interpretation"), replay-fidelity wins cleanly on two of those three axes and ties on the third. It's more deterministic in the literal sense: a saved game record produces the exact same recap every time you open it, regardless of what state some unrelated toggle happens to be in on that particular day. View-time-render means the same CoreData row can render two different ways depending on when you look at it, which is a mutable-output-from-immutable-input problem I should've flagged as a smell the moment I proposed it — that's exactly the kind of "silent state that changes the meaning of history" pattern this app is supposed to avoid everywhere else (it's the same instinct behind requiring a schemaVersion field and an honest partial-final-round timeline).

It's also the more consistent choice architecturally, and this is the part I actually didn't connect in round 2: we already decided a completed game's timeline, end-reason, and drink/dare counts get frozen into CoreData at save time and never recomputed from live state afterward. Mode is just one more field describing "what happened," and treating it differently — as a live lookup instead of a stored fact — would be an inconsistent design for no real benefit. There's a decent argument for view-time-render on convenience grounds (if someone's embarrassed by old drinking-mode copy and wants to reread it as tame dare copy, view-time gives them that), but I don't think that's a real user need anyone's named across five phases, and it comes at the cost of the recap being an unreliable narrator of its own history — which is a worse trade for a "trust" app than a mildly awkward re-read.

So: replay-fidelity, ratified. `mode` gets stored as a field on the persisted game record from the very first CoreData migration, right alongside `schemaVersion`, `endReason`, and the Joker rule-text captures — it's cheap to add now and expensive to retrofit once real games exist on real devices, which is the exact logic we already used to justify schema versioning in the first place. Once that's written down, I think this phase is actually closed — Codex and I converged on all seven substantive items, Gemma3's dissent is confined to the already-settled history-persistence question from a prior phase and doesn't touch anything live here, and there's no remaining fork I'd flag as blocking.

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 3**

Okay, let's do this.

“Hold on a second. I’m going to be blunt: this entire approach feels… fragile. We’re building a game, not a complex financial system. All this talk of transaction management, verification, and subscription statuses… it’s overkill. We’re setting ourselves up for a mountain of maintenance and potential breakage. I'm hearing a lot of "how do we do things right" and not nearly enough about “how do we actually make a playable game?"

Specifically, this obsession with the Joker’s rule – everyone’s spending so much time figuring out how to capture and store it that we’re ignoring the core problem: it’s an *optional* rule. Nobody is *forcing* the player to make it up. We’re building a handoff system, a history, and a shareable recap *for a feature that may never be used*. That's a massive investment in complexity for a potential side effect. My suggestion is simple: just allow the user to enter a rule when they draw the Joker *once*, and then immediately discard it for the rest of the game. That’s it. The recap still works, the handoff still works, and we’ve avoided a tangled mess of data models and verification steps.

And while we're at it, this 'accessibility-hidden shielding' thing is frankly terrifying. The entire point of this app is that a user can share it with someone else – and suddenly, that other person has access to a secretly hidden card, in voice-over. That's a massive privacy risk, and it's not something we should be building into the core design. It's a potential nightmare for anyone using VoiceOver. Let’s defer this entirely to a later phase, and focus on getting the handoff right first.  If it turns out that accessibility is a critical requirement, we can revisit it then, but let’s not bake in a potentially dangerous feature now.

Finally, this fixation on 'current entitlements' – we’re spending so much time tracking what a user owns that we're missing the point. This is a game about *playing* the game. What matters is that the handoff works, the game is fair, and the recap shows the final result. We don’t need to track every single subscription or consumable. That's a distraction.  Let’s focus on the fundamental mechanics, not the granular tracking of user assets.

I think we’re overcomplicating this. Let's simplify. Shield-first handoff, simple rule capture for the Joker (if the user wants it), and a focused recap that shows the result. Let’s ship that, test it, and *then* add the fancy stuff later if we need to.”

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

Round 3 closed the last open item. Codex and Claude both landed on replay-fidelity for the mode question — Claude actually walked through why it's the correct pick against the contract's own standing rule (choose the stricter/more deterministic/more privacy-preserving option): view-time rendering means the same saved record can display two different ways depending on an unrelated toggle's state on a given day, which is exactly the "silent state changes the meaning of history" pattern this app avoids everywhere else. Codex's concrete implementation — store `mode` per timeline entry/turn event (not just a single game-header field, so a mid-session toggle is captured accurately), alongside `schemaVersion`, `endReason`, and the Joker rule-text captures, all from the first CoreData migration — is exactly the kind of specificity the quality gate was asking for, and Claude ratified it outright.

Gemma3's round 3 entry doesn't engage with any of this — it re-argues against Joker-text capture and against accessibility-hidden shielding, both of which were already settled with clear reasoning in round 2 (the Joker-capture point in particular directly rebuts Gemma3's "it's optional so don't bother" framing, since the whole issue is that skipping it silently breaks a feature the contract already requires to exist). That's a repeat objection to closed ground, not new material, so it doesn't block.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

This phase closes with eight concrete, previously-unspecified decisions locked in, all additive to the already-ratified build:

1. **Joker rule-text capture is in scope for slice 1.** After a Joker reveal, the player gets an optional short text field ("Set the house rule text") with Save/Skip actions, saved as a timeline entry flagged custom/Joker-origin. Without this, the funniest-rule ballot's empty state would fire on every game ever played — a feature that could never produce its own intended output.

2. **The HUD drops the misleading "round X of 10" display.** Since 6-player games mathematically cannot reach 10 full rounds before the 53-card deck runs out, the HUD shows honest, player-count-independent signals instead: Kings drawn (of 4) and cards remaining in the deck. A round count only appears when it's actually reachable at the current player count.

3. **Tie-break rule, explicit:** if the last card in the deck is also the 4th King, cup-full wins as the end-reason over deck-exhaustion.

4. **Accessibility-hidden shielding is a named hard requirement.** The shielded state must remove card content from the accessibility tree entirely (`.accessibilityHidden(true)` or equivalent), not just hide it visually — verified with VoiceOver on as its own dedicated QA pass, and fail-safe-hidden behavior tested against full-screen system interruptions (calls, Face ID/passcode prompts), not just banners.

5. **History rows get a swipe-to-delete action** — an undeletable local drinking/dare log doesn't fit a trust-first app.

6. **Export outcomes split into three distinct states:** user-cancelled (neutral no-op), permission-denied (routes to Settings with recovery copy), and actual render/share failure (real error state).

7. **CoreData carries a `schemaVersion` field from the first migration**, with a migration-safe decode path.

8. **Mode rendering is resolved as replay-fidelity, not view-time render.** A completed game's recap always renders using the mode(s) actually active during play, stored per turn/timeline entry (not just once at the game header, so a mid-session toggle is captured correctly) — never reinterpreted by whatever sober/drinking toggle happens to be set when someone reopens an old recap later. This is the stricter, more deterministic option per the contract's own standing tie-break rule, it matches how every other piece of a completed game's record is already frozen at save time (timeline, end-reason, counts), and it's stored alongside `schemaVersion` from the very first migration since retrofitting it later would require a real data migration. Onboarding copy should note this plainly: "Recaps are a record of how the game was played — changing mode later won't rewrite old games."

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 3**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
The repair instruction from the previous gate is fully satisfied: the mode-rendering fork is now resolved (not deferred) as replay-fidelity, with the reasoning tied explicitly back to the contract's own stricter/more-deterministic/more-privacy-preserving standing rule, and it's correctly linked to the `schemaVersion` decision (mode stored per turn/timeline entry from the first CoreData migration, not retrofitted later). The onboarding-copy callout ("Recaps are a record of how the game was played...") closes the loop on communicating this as a user-facing contract, per Codex's round-3 addition.

All eight resolved decisions are concrete and implementable, and collectively they satisfy the phase's required coverage even though it's written as a flat numbered list rather than under separate headers (acceptable per the rubric's instruction not to fail for prose-vs-headings reasons): edge cases are enumerated (Joker-draw skip, 6-player round-math dishonesty, simultaneous cup-full/deck-exhaustion tie, VoiceOver accessibility-tree leak, undeletable history, ambiguous export outcomes, schema drift, mode-toggle-vs-saved-record drift), each carries a concrete mitigation rather than a vague flag (text-capture sheet, dual honest HUD signals, explicit tie-break order, `.accessibilityHidden` requirement with dedicated QA pass, swipe-to-delete, three-way export state split, `schemaVersion` field, per-entry `mode` storage), and privacy/data implications are treated as first-class throughout (accessibility, UGC-in-export risk carried forward, undeletable-log risk, historical-integrity risk).

Gemma3's round-3 dissent (re-litigating Joker capture and accessibility-hidden shielding) is correctly identified as repeating already-rebutted round-2 ground rather than raising new material, so treating it as non-blocking is fair — it doesn't leave a live disagreement unaddressed.

Nothing here contradicts any prior ratified phase (contract, research, initial discussion, product brief, next-steps slice), and the output leaves a clean, unambiguous set of data-model and UX decisions for the next phase to build from. No further repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Round 3 closed the last open item. Codex and Claude both landed on replay-fidelity for the mode question — Claude actually walked through why it's the correct pick against the contract's own standing rule (choose the stricter/more deterministic/more privacy-preserving option): view-time rendering means the same saved record can display two different ways depending on an unrelated toggle's state on a given day, which is exactly the "silent state changes the meaning of history" pattern this app avoids everywhere else. Codex's concrete implementation — store `mode` per timeline entry/turn event (not just a single game-header field, so a mid-session toggle is captured accurately), alongside `schemaVersion`, `endReason`, and the Joker rule-text captures, all from the first CoreData migration — is exactly the kind of specificity the quality gate was asking for, and Claude ratified it outright.

Gemma3's round 3 entry doesn't engage with any of this — it re-argues against Joker-text capture and against accessibility-hidden shielding, both of which were already settled with clear reasoning in round 2 (the Joker-capture point in particular directly rebuts Gemma3's "it's optional so don't bother" framing, since the whole issue is that skipping it silently breaks a feature the contract already requires to exist). That's a repeat objection to closed ground, not new material, so it doesn't block.

### App Features

# drinking-games-test — App Features

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-09 11:12:49._

## Original Prompt

```
Build Kings — a multiplayer drinking game app for iOS (local pass-and-play).

Core mechanics:
- 2–6 players draw cards from a deck in turn order
- Each card (A–K, plus Joker) triggers a rule: e.g. "2 = You" (drinker picks someone), "5 = Guys", "K = King's Cup" (build communal cup with dares), "Joker = Make a rule"
- Players drink per the card rule; when the cup is empty or 10 turns pass, game ends with a recap
- Pass phone between players; each player sees only their own card until they pass

Key features:
- Local only (no server, no login)
- Customizable rules: edit card meanings before each game, save rulesets
- Game history: recap shows who drank most, funniest rule, timeline of rounds
- Sober mode toggle (default): uses "dare" language instead of drinking; activating drinking mode (17+ gate) swaps copy to drinking references but same mechanics

Tech:
- SwiftUI, local-first with CoreData for game history
- Phone must stay face-down while other player holds it; privacy shield until handoff
- Export game recap as a sharable image (name, date, player list, highlights)

Scope: MVP with Kings rules only (not Hearts, Asshole, or other variants). One ruleset bundled; let users customize before start.
```

## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · Skeptic) — Round 1**

M is must, S is should, C is could, W is won’t, and the scope boundary is this app only: local pass-and-play Kings on one iPhone with no networking.

Must: One complete local game loop and screen flow (Setup → shielded handoff play → recap/export) for 2–6 players using the bundled Kings ruleset plus editable saved rulesets.
- User story: As the host, I can set up a game in seconds and run full play to finish without leaving the app.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Setup accepts exactly 2–6 players with non-empty names.
  - A game always starts with the 53-card deck, fixed turn order, and one active ruleset.
  - Players can only draw on their turn after shield confirmation.
  - Game ends on the first of: fourth King, 10 full rounds, or deck exhaustion.
  - Deck exhaustion is checked and labeled correctly if hit first or tied with 4th King (4th King wins).
  - Game shows a clear end-reason label per path.
  - App shows a recap with highlights and timeline for the completed session.

Must: Privacy-safe handoff is the default behavior, not a visual gesture option.
- User story: As any player, I only ever see my own card and not another player’s, even for one frame.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Shield is the first visible state on every handoff before any pass/next-turn UI.
  - Reveals require explicit incoming-player confirmation tap.
  - Shield auto re-hides on backgrounding, rotation, app switch, rapid taps, and notification/system interruptions.
  - Shielded card content is removed from accessibility tree (`accessibilityHidden` semantics), not just visually hidden.
  - At zero tolerance for failures, no wrong-card content is exposed.

Must: Deterministic engine, isolated from UI, as single source of truth.
- User story: As a player, I can rely on deterministic turns and end logic regardless of device timing or orientation.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Engine is pure Swift state machine independent of SwiftUI and persistence.
  - No duplicate draws on rapid taps (idempotent actions).
  - Turn order never advances without a legal confirm/reveal path.
  - End-reason is single, explicit, and never generic.
  - HUD reflects honest progress signals (Kings drawn of 4; cards remaining; round count only when reachable for current player count).

Must: Rules and funniest-rule mechanics are actually usable, not decorative.
- User story: As a player, I can define custom meanings before start and see a meaningful funniest-rule result afterward.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Rules can be edited before game start and saved as local rulesets.
  - Joker draw triggers optional in-session text capture with Save/Skip.
  - Funnyest-rule result uses fired custom/Joker rules with deterministic default-to-empty/fallback behavior.
  - Empty custom/Joker outcome displays explicit “no custom/Joker rules played this game” state.

Must: Sober mode default with gated drinking-mode switch.
- User story: As a mixed group, we can keep one mechanics set with safer vs drinking phrasing.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Default mode is sober.
  - Drinking mode requires one-time self-attestation gate and can be toggled later.
  - In-app copy is distinct per mode.
  - Recap is replay-fidelity: it renders with the mode timeline captured during play, not remapped at view time.
  - Onboarding clearly states changing mode later does not rewrite history copy.

Must: Exportable recap and persistent history as first-class outcomes.
- User story: As a player, I can export a complete recap image and find finished games after relaunch.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Recap export includes game name/date/players/highlights and can be shared as image.
  - Export flow has explicit loading, success, blocked permission, render/share failure, and user-cancelled states.
  - Permission-denied path surfaces recovery copy and direct settings route.
  - Completed games are persisted in Core Data and visible after force-quit/relaunch.
  - Export and recap generation work from persisted game data, not only current in-memory session.

Must: History is trustworthy and user-manageable.
- User story: As a user who trusts the app, I can audit past sessions and remove records.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - History list shows each saved game with end reason and key highlights.
  - Swipe-to-delete exists per row and deletion is immediately reflected.
  - Empty/loading/error states are explicit in history load/save lifecycle.
  - Core Data entities include `schemaVersion` from first migration path.

Must: Screen and flow states are explicit across major flows.
- User story: As a player, I know what’s happening when operations fail instead of getting silent no-ops.
- Acceptance criteria:
  - Setup, draw/reveal, handoff, recap, rules edit/save, history load/save/delete, and export each handle empty/loading/success/error states.
  - Failures preserve continuity where possible (e.g., completed-session timeline remains recoverable if export fails).

Should: Haptic feedback and turn polish.
- As the host, I get confirmation cues that keep repeated turns fast and consistent.
- Add light vibration on confirm draw and cup-fill events after hardware support checks.

Should: Clear onboarding for privacy and game math.
- First-run setup explains shield flow and end-condition precedence quickly, with a “quick-start” path.
- Include concise rule cards and examples of exact privacy behavior.

Should: Accessibility and visual ergonomics baseline.
- Dynamic Type support, 44×44 min tap targets, high contrast readability, and optional animation reductions.

Could: Visual deck/cup metaphors and non-blocking onboarding customization.
- Tasteful card visuals, subtle animations, lightweight themed styling, and optional card back personalization.

Could: Advanced recap views.
- Add grouped analytics-style summaries, per-player mini-bars, and trend/history charts.

Won’t: No server, accounts, login, networking, multiplayer across devices, or live sync.
- This remains local-first Core Data only for v1; no cross-device gameplay state sync.

Won’t: No other card-game variants (Hearts, Asshole, etc.).
- Kings-only scope remains strict to keep deterministic trust and privacy surface small.

Won’t: No paywall, subscriptions, ads, IAP, telemetry, or usage analytics in this build.
- Explicitly out of scope due to local/private and offline product promise.

Won’t: No in-progress resume across relaunch.
- If app is killed mid-game, game is discarded; only completed games are persisted.

Won’t: No full moderation pipeline for custom rule text.
- Unmoderated custom text may appear in recap/export and is an accepted named risk.

The strongest risk in this direction is that privacy-safe behavior plus replay-fidelity history (especially per-turn mode and accessibility hardening) can expand early architecture complexity; that is intentional and non-negotiable because this product is about trust, and a single ambiguous state would make everything above it feel dishonest.

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming into this as the pragmatist with a QA lens: my job this round is to turn eight phases of ratified decisions into a list that's actually buildable, and to be honest about what could get silently dropped under time pressure if we don't nail down acceptance criteria now.

My must-have list is basically the contract's hard requirements, sequenced by build risk rather than treated as an undifferentiated pile. The foundation tier — the stuff that has to be rock solid before anything else matters — is: player setup for 2–6 with name entry and a validation gate (story: "as a host I enter 2–6 names and start a game," acceptance: fewer than 2 or more than 6 blocks start with a visible reason, no crash on boundary values); the deterministic card engine applying the bundled Kings ruleset with all three end conditions wired in from day one (story: "as a player I draw a card and see its rule fire correctly," acceptance: 4th King, 10th completed round, and 53rd card drawn each independently trigger their own correctly-worded end-reason, and the specific tie-break — cup-full beats deck-exhaustion when the last card is also the 4th King — is unit-testable in isolation from any UI); and the shield-first handoff with confirm-tap reveal, which I'd call the single highest-risk must-have in the whole app (story: "as the outgoing player I hand the phone to the next player with zero chance they see my card," acceptance: shield renders before any "pass to" UI on every transition, survives backgrounding/rotation/rapid-tap/notification-banner and full-screen system interruptions like calls or Face ID prompts by failing back to hidden, and — this is the one I'd bet money gets skipped if nobody writes it down — the card content is actually removed from the accessibility tree while shielded, verified with VoiceOver on as its own dedicated pass, not just visually covered).

Sitting right on top of that foundation, still must-have because they're what make a finished game actually mean something: the live HUD showing Kings-drawn-of-4 and cards-remaining rather than a round counter that lies at 6 players; Joker rule-text capture as an optional field right after reveal, because without it the funniest-rule ballot's empty state is permanently, silently wrong; the recap screen with its distinct end-reason, honest partial-final-round timeline, and the funniest-rule ballot or its explicit empty state; a CoreData save of the completed game with a schemaVersion field and mode stored per timeline entry (replay-fidelity, not view-time) from the very first migration, since retrofitting either of those after a real user has a saved game on disk is a real migration project, not a bug fix; swipe-to-delete on history rows, because an undeletable local log is a real trust gap, not a nice-to-have, for an app whose entire pitch is trust; real image export with the three-way outcome split (user-cancelled as a neutral no-op, permission-denied routed to Settings, actual render failure as a genuine error), tested on a physical device since that's the one flow named across three separate phases as likely to behave differently than the simulator; and the sober/drinking mode toggle with its one-time self-attestation gate, which I'd keep as must-have but sequence dead last among the foundation items because it's a pure copy-swap-plus-boolean that never touches the engine, shield, or export pipeline — it's contractually required, just genuinely low-risk to bolt on, so if the build calendar gets tight this is the must-have I'd want protected by having already de-risked everything harder before touching it. Ruleset editing before a game (edit card meanings, save/name a ruleset, pick from saved rulesets) is also must-have per the contract, but I want to flag it honestly as the one item on this list with the most unknown build cost, since it's real CRUD UI plus CoreData plus validation that a saved ruleset still maps onto all 53 cards — my acceptance criteria there would be: edited rules persist across relaunch, a game started with a custom ruleset produces a recap that correctly reflects the custom text (not the bundled default), and there's no way to save a ruleset that leaves a card unmapped.

Should/could, not gating ship: nicer cup-fill animation and haptic tuning on the reveal tap beyond baseline functional haptics; a "reformat for current mode" opt-in action on old recaps (explicitly not the default, since we ratified replay-fidelity); extra curated ruleset packs and unlimited saved rulesets beyond a small free cap, both of which are already scoped as the future paid-tier shape and have no business in v1; opt-in iCloud sync of rulesets/history. Won't-build, and I'd write these down explicitly so nobody reopens them mid-build: any other card-game variant, anything server/account/networking, sensor-based face-down detection as a primary mechanism (manual tap-to-reveal is the only mechanism in v1), any form of mid-game resume across a relaunch, content moderation of custom rule text, telemetry/analytics of any kind, and any IAP or monetization surface.

My biggest worry about my own list: I've kept ruleset editing and drinking mode as must-have because the contract says they're hard requirements and I don't think this phase gets to silently cut what a prior phase already ratified — but if I'm being the pragmatist, I'd rather the room explicitly agree on a fallback now (which must-have gets descoped to "should, ship next update" if the build genuinely runs out of runway) than discover it during the build phase under pressure. If forced to pick one, I'd protect the core loop/shield/persistence/export chain absolutely and treat ruleset editing as the one that could slip to a fast-follow, since a game with only the bundled ruleset is still a complete, honest, fully-functional Kings app — a game with a broken shield or a silently-empty funniest-rule ballot is not.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini(key) -> Warning: 256-color support not detected. Using a terminal with at least 256-color support is recomme | gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Gemma3 (local) (Design Lead · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Okay, here's my contribution as the Design Lead, aiming for a focused, critical first pass for this phase:

“Right, let’s be brutally clear: this isn’t about building a ‘cool’ Kings app. It’s about building a system that reliably handles a small piece of user trust—and we need to get that right from the start.  My biggest concern is the shift to focus heavily on the Joker rule. We’re layering complexity on top of what is fundamentally a very simple game: draw a card, determine its effect, repeat.  Introducing a custom rule mechanic adds an entirely new layer of state management, data storage, and UI logic that is unnecessary and frankly, likely to cause more problems than it solves.  It’s a technical debt that’ll haunt us later. I'm going to defer the Joker rule capture entirely until we have a solid, well-tested foundation for the core handoff.

My proposed direction is to build a core handoff flow—deterministic, reliable, and free of extraneous features—around the standard card drawing and rule execution. The game’s core is simple and the user experience should feel that way as well. The 'funniest-rule' mechanic is a separate feature, a potential 'stretch goal’ for a future release.  It shouldn't drive the design of this initial MVP.

Specifically, I'm pushing back on the extensive data modeling – the insistence on tracking `mode` and capturing the Joker’s text. That’s simply not the right tradeoff for this level of complexity. We need to prioritize a clean, understandable state machine that reliably describes the game’s progress.  Storing the custom rule in a text field and allowing users to enter their own will create significant maintenance overhead.  It’s a complex system for a relatively simple user need.

I also want to flag the VoiceOver accessibility issue immediately.  Visually hiding a card – the whole point of the shield – is not enough.  If we’re building an app for anyone, we need to ensure it’s accessible to those who use assistive technologies.  This requires completely removing the card’s content from the accessibility tree, a much more significant undertaking than a simple visual hiding. It’s a non-negotiable requirement.

Finally, I’m concerned about the long-term implications of the history persistence. While a default-off-delete is the simplest solution for this MVP, we need to build in a way that avoids accumulating a permanent log of user drinking/dare-making behavior – because that feels like a privacy issue. This system is for a simple game; it shouldn’t be a logbook for bad decisions.

My core recommendations for this phase’s outcome are these: 1) Defer the Joker-rule capture and implementation; 2) Focus on building a robust and deterministic handoff flow—card draw, rule execution, a reliable end-state (King or deck-exhaustion)—3) Explicitly define the accessibility shield as a content-removal barrier, verified with VoiceOver testing; and 4) Build in a minimal history system, allowing a swipe-to-delete on history rows to mitigate long-term data concerns. Let’s prioritize a clean, reliable core experience, and defer the “nice-to-have” features for later. Let’s not get bogged down in complexity that isn't absolutely critical to the core product. ”

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Qwen2.5Vl (local) skipped — in cooldown (crash)._

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex and Claude (playing pragmatist/QA this round) independently produced full must/should/could/won't lists and they land in essentially the same place — no real fork this round, just two people converging with different amounts of hedging. Codex organized around seven must-have clusters (core loop, privacy-safe handoff as default not option, deterministic isolated engine, usable rules/funniest-rule mechanics, sober/drinking mode with replay-fidelity, exportable+persistent recap, trustworthy/deletable history, explicit states everywhere), each with a user story and acceptance criteria. Claude organized the same material by build-risk tier — foundation (setup, engine+all three end conditions, shield-first handoff with accessibility-tree removal) first, then the things that make a finished game meaningful (HUD, Joker capture, recap/funniest-rule, persistence with schemaVersion+per-turn mode, history delete, device-tested export, gated drinking mode) — and only flagged ruleset editing as the one item with the most unknown build cost, while still keeping it must-have per the contract rather than trying to cut it. That's a risk callout, not a disagreement with Codex.

Should/could/won't lists match too: haptics and onboarding polish as should-have, visual flourishes and analytics-style recap views as could-have, and the same won't-build list (no server/accounts/networking, no other game variants, no paywall/IAP/telemetry, no mid-game resume, no moderation of custom text).

Gemma3 spent this round arguing again to defer Joker-rule capture and downplay per-turn mode storage — both of which were already settled with explicit reasoning three rounds ago in the detailed_discussion phase (the funniest-rule ballot literally can't function without Joker capture; replay-fidelity mode storage was picked specifically because it's the stricter/more deterministic option). That's a repeat objection to already-closed ground, consistent with the pattern in every prior phase, so it doesn't reopen anything here.

## Final Output
