**Must-have (with user story + acceptance criteria each):**

1. **Full local game loop, 2–6 players, bundled + editable rulesets.** Story: host sets up and plays a complete game without leaving the app. AC: setup validates exactly 2–6 non-empty names; deck is always the fixed 53-card set with one active ruleset; draws only happen after shield confirmation; game ends on the first of 4th King / 10 full rounds / deck exhaustion, with cup-full winning any simultaneous tie against deck-exhaustion; end-reason is distinct and correctly labeled per path; recap shows highlights and timeline.

2. **Privacy-safe handoff shield is the default behavior, not a visual nicety.** Story: no player ever sees another player's card, not even for one frame. AC: shield renders before any "pass to" UI on every transition; reveal requires explicit incoming-player confirm-tap; shield fails back to hidden on backgrounding, rotation, app switch, rapid taps, notification banners, and full-screen system interruptions (calls, Face ID/passcode); shielded card content is removed from the accessibility tree (`accessibilityHidden` or equivalent), verified with a dedicated VoiceOver-on QA pass — zero tolerance for any wrong-card exposure.

3. **Deterministic engine, isolated from UI/persistence.** Story: turns and end logic behave the same regardless of device timing. AC: pure Swift state machine, unit-testable independent of SwiftUI/CoreData; idempotent actions (no duplicate draws from rapid taps); turn order never advances without a legal confirm/reveal; HUD shows honest, player-count-independent signals (Kings drawn of 4, cards remaining) with a round counter only when mathematically reachable at the current player count.

4. **Rules and funniest-rule mechanics are functional, not decorative.** Story: players can customize card meanings pre-game and get a real funniest-rule result after. AC: rules editable pre-game and saved as named local rulesets; Joker draw offers an optional Save/Skip text-capture field; funniest-rule ballot runs over rules actually fired that session, defaulting to the last custom/Joker rule if skipped; explicit "no custom/Joker rules played this game" empty state when nothing fired.

5. **Sober-default mode with gated, replay-faithful drinking mode.** Story: mixed groups keep one mechanic set with safer or drinking phrasing. AC: sober is default; drinking mode requires a one-time self-attestation gate and is reversible anytime; copy is fully distinct per mode with zero cross-mode leakage; recap renders using the mode captured per turn/timeline entry at play time (replay-fidelity), never reinterpreted by whatever toggle is active later; onboarding states this plainly.

6. **Exportable recap + persistent history as first-class, not bolted on.** Story: a player can export a shareable recap image and find finished games after relaunch. AC: export includes name/date/players/highlights as a shareable image; export flow has distinct loading/success/permission-denied/render-failure/user-cancelled states, tested on a physical device; permission-denied routes to Settings with recovery copy; completed games persist in CoreData (with `schemaVersion` from the first migration) and are visible after force-quit/relaunch; export/recap work from the persisted record, not just in-memory state.

7. **History is trustworthy and user-manageable.** Story: a user can audit and remove past sessions. AC: history list shows each game with end-reason and highlights; swipe-to-delete per row, reflected immediately; explicit empty/loading/error states on history load/save/delete.

8. **Explicit states across every major flow.** Story: no operation fails silently. AC: setup, draw/reveal, handoff, recap, rule edit/save, history load/save/delete, and export each define empty/loading/success/error states; failures preserve continuity where possible (e.g., an in-session timeline survives even if export itself fails).

**Should-have:** haptic feedback on confirm-tap reveal and cup-fill (behind a hardware capability check); concise first-run onboarding covering the shield flow and end-condition precedence with a quick-start path; accessibility/ergonomics baseline — Dynamic Type, 44×44 minimum tap targets, high-contrast readability, reduced-motion option.

**Could-have:** tasteful card/deck/cup visual polish and light animation; optional card-back personalization; grouped analytics-style recap views (per-player mini-bars, trend charts); an explicit opt-in "reformat for current mode" action on old recaps (never the default, since replay-fidelity is ratified).

**Won't-build:** no server, accounts, login, networking, or cross-device sync/multiplayer; no other card-game variants (Hearts, Asshole, etc.) — Kings-only stays strict; no paywall, subscriptions, ads, IAP, telemetry, or usage analytics in this build; no in-progress-game resume across a relaunch (a killed game is simply discarded — only completed games persist); no moderation/filtering pipeline for custom rule text (unmoderated text may appear in recap/export — accepted, named risk); no sensor-based face-down detection as a primary mechanism (manual tap-to-reveal only).

**Named build-sequencing note (not a scope cut):** if runway gets tight, ruleset editing is the single must-have with the most unknown build cost and the most defensible candidate for a fast-follow — everything else in the must-have list (core loop, shield, persistence, export, history, mode gating) should be protected first, since a Kings game with only the bundled ruleset is still complete and honest, while a broken shield or a silently-always-empty funniest-rule ballot is not.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This phase output meets the bar. All four required outputs are present and concrete: 8 must-have clusters each with a user story and specific, testable acceptance criteria; a should-have list (haptics, onboarding, accessibility baseline); a could-have list (visual polish, analytics-style recap views, opt-in mode-reformat action); and a won't-build list with reasons tied to the ratified non-goals (no server/accounts/networking, no other variants, no monetization/telemetry, no mid-game resume, no moderation).

Every must-have traces directly to already-ratified decisions from prior phases (three-way end condition with cup-full tie-break, shield-first handoff with accessibility-tree removal, Joker capture for the funniest-rule ballot, replay-fidelity mode storage with schemaVersion, three-way export state split, swipe-to-delete history) — nothing is invented and nothing contractually required is silently dropped. The acceptance criteria are specific enough to be QA-checkable (e.g., "cup-full wins any simultaneous tie against deck-exhaustion," "shielded card content is removed from the accessibility tree ... verified with a dedicated VoiceOver-on QA pass"), which satisfies "the build phase can complete the must-have list" — these are buildable, not aspirational.

The one place the list runs long (8 must-haves spanning core loop, shield, engine, rules/funniest-rule, mode gating, export/persistence, history, and universal state coverage) is handled honestly rather than glossed over: the "Named build-sequencing note" flags ruleset editing as the highest-unknown-cost item and gives a defensible fallback (fast-follow) without cutting it from the must-have list outright, since the contract forbids silent cuts. That's the right way to reconcile "prioritize ruthlessly" with "don't silently drop contract-required features."

Gemma3's dissent (defer Joker capture, downplay per-turn mode storage) is correctly identified as a repeat of arguments already resolved with explicit reasoning in the detailed_discussion phase, so treating it as non-blocking is fair rather than papering over live disagreement — there wasn't a live disagreement this round, just Codex and Claude converging with different organizing frames (feature-cluster vs. risk-tier), which the coordinator explained accurately. No repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex and Claude (playing pragmatist/QA this round) independently produced full must/should/could/won't lists and they land in essentially the same place — no real fork this round, just two people converging with different amounts of hedging. Codex organized around seven must-have clusters (core loop, privacy-safe handoff as default not option, deterministic isolated engine, usable rules/funniest-rule mechanics, sober/drinking mode with replay-fidelity, exportable+persistent recap, trustworthy/deletable history, explicit states everywhere), each with a user story and acceptance criteria. Claude organized the same material by build-risk tier — foundation (setup, engine+all three end conditions, shield-first handoff with accessibility-tree removal) first, then the things that make a finished game meaningful (HUD, Joker capture, recap/funniest-rule, persistence with schemaVersion+per-turn mode, history delete, device-tested export, gated drinking mode) — and only flagged ruleset editing as the one item with the most unknown build cost, while still keeping it must-have per the contract rather than trying to cut it. That's a risk callout, not a disagreement with Codex.

Should/could/won't lists match too: haptics and onboarding polish as should-have, visual flourishes and analytics-style recap views as could-have, and the same won't-build list (no server/accounts/networking, no other game variants, no paywall/IAP/telemetry, no mid-game resume, no moderation of custom text).

Gemma3 spent this round arguing again to defer Joker-rule capture and downplay per-turn mode storage — both of which were already settled with explicit reasoning three rounds ago in the detailed_discussion phase (the funniest-rule ballot literally can't function without Joker capture; replay-fidelity mode storage was picked specifically because it's the stricter/more deterministic option). That's a repeat objection to already-closed ground, consistent with the pattern in every prior phase, so it doesn't reopen anything here.

## Final Output

**Must-have (with user story + acceptance criteria each):**

1. **Full local game loop, 2–6 players, bundled + editable rulesets.** Story: host sets up and plays a complete game without leaving the app. AC: setup validates exactly 2–6 non-empty names; deck is always the fixed 53-card set with one active ruleset; draws only happen after shield confirmation; game ends on the first of 4th King / 10 full rounds / deck exhaustion, with cup-full winning any simultaneous tie against deck-exhaustion; end-reason is distinct and correctly labeled per path; recap shows highlights and timeline.

2. **Privacy-safe handoff shield is the default behavior, not a visual nicety.** Story: no player ever sees another player's card, not even for one frame. AC: shield renders before any "pass to" UI on every transition; reveal requires explicit incoming-player confirm-tap; shield fails back to hidden on backgrounding, rotation, app switch, rapid taps, notification banners, and full-screen system interruptions (calls, Face ID/passcode); shielded card content is removed from the accessibility tree (`accessibilityHidden` or equivalent), verified with a dedicated VoiceOver-on QA pass — zero tolerance for any wrong-card exposure.

3. **Deterministic engine, isolated from UI/persistence.** Story: turns and end logic behave the same regardless of device timing. AC: pure Swift state machine, unit-testable independent of SwiftUI/CoreData; idempotent actions (no duplicate draws from rapid taps); turn order never advances without a legal confirm/reveal; HUD shows honest, player-count-independent signals (Kings drawn of 4, cards remaining) with a round counter only when mathematically reachable at the current player count.

4. **Rules and funniest-rule mechanics are functional, not decorative.** Story: players can customize card meanings pre-game and get a real funniest-rule result after. AC: rules editable pre-game and saved as named local rulesets; Joker draw offers an optional Save/Skip text-capture field; funniest-rule ballot runs over rules actually fired that session, defaulting to the last custom/Joker rule if skipped; explicit "no custom/Joker rules played this game" empty state when nothing fired.

5. **Sober-default mode with gated, replay-faithful drinking mode.** Story: mixed groups keep one mechanic set with safer or drinking phrasing. AC: sober is default; drinking mode requires a one-time self-attestation gate and is reversible anytime; copy is fully distinct per mode with zero cross-mode leakage; recap renders using the mode captured per turn/timeline entry at play time (replay-fidelity), never reinterpreted by whatever toggle is active later; onboarding states this plainly.

6. **Exportable recap + persistent history as first-class, not bolted on.** Story: a player can export a shareable recap image and find finished games after relaunch. AC: export includes name/date/players/highlights as a shareable image; export flow has distinct loading/success/permission-denied/render-failure/user-cancelled states, tested on a physical device; permission-denied routes to Settings with recovery copy; completed games persist in CoreData (with `schemaVersion` from the first migration) and are visible after force-quit/relaunch; export/recap work from the persisted record, not just in-memory state.

7. **History is trustworthy and user-manageable.** Story: a user can audit and remove past sessions. AC: history list shows each game with end-reason and highlights; swipe-to-delete per row, reflected immediately; explicit empty/loading/error states on history load/save/delete.

8. **Explicit states across every major flow.** Story: no operation fails silently. AC: setup, draw/reveal, handoff, recap, rule edit/save, history load/save/delete, and export each define empty/loading/success/error states; failures preserve continuity where possible (e.g., an in-session timeline survives even if export itself fails).

**Should-have:** haptic feedback on confirm-tap reveal and cup-fill (behind a hardware capability check); concise first-run onboarding covering the shield flow and end-condition precedence with a quick-start path; accessibility/ergonomics baseline — Dynamic Type, 44×44 minimum tap targets, high-contrast readability, reduced-motion option.

**Could-have:** tasteful card/deck/cup visual polish and light animation; optional card-back personalization; grouped analytics-style recap views (per-player mini-bars, trend charts); an explicit opt-in "reformat for current mode" action on old recaps (never the default, since replay-fidelity is ratified).

**Won't-build:** no server, accounts, login, networking, or cross-device sync/multiplayer; no other card-game variants (Hearts, Asshole, etc.) — Kings-only stays strict; no paywall, subscriptions, ads, IAP, telemetry, or usage analytics in this build; no in-progress-game resume across a relaunch (a killed game is simply discarded — only completed games persist); no moderation/filtering pipeline for custom rule text (unmoderated text may appear in recap/export — accepted, named risk); no sensor-based face-down detection as a primary mechanism (manual tap-to-reveal only).

**Named build-sequencing note (not a scope cut):** if runway gets tight, ruleset editing is the single must-have with the most unknown build cost and the most defensible candidate for a fast-follow — everything else in the must-have list (core loop, shield, persistence, export, history, mode gating) should be protected first, since a Kings game with only the bundled ruleset is still complete and honest, while a broken shield or a silently-always-empty funniest-rule ballot is not.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

### Design Discussion

# drinking-games-test — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-09 11:15:02._

## Original Prompt

```
Build Kings — a multiplayer drinking game app for iOS (local pass-and-play).

Core mechanics:
- 2–6 players draw cards from a deck in turn order
- Each card (A–K, plus Joker) triggers a rule: e.g. "2 = You" (drinker picks someone), "5 = Guys", "K = King's Cup" (build communal cup with dares), "Joker = Make a rule"
- Players drink per the card rule; when the cup is empty or 10 turns pass, game ends with a recap
- Pass phone between players; each player sees only their own card until they pass

Key features:
- Local only (no server, no login)
- Customizable rules: edit card meanings before each game, save rulesets
- Game history: recap shows who drank most, funniest rule, timeline of rounds
- Sober mode toggle (default): uses "dare" language instead of drinking; activating drinking mode (17+ gate) swaps copy to drinking references but same mechanics

Tech:
- SwiftUI, local-first with CoreData for game history
- Phone must stay face-down while other player holds it; privacy shield until handoff
- Export game recap as a sharable image (name, date, player list, highlights)

Scope: MVP with Kings rules only (not Hearts, Asshole, or other variants). One ruleset bundled; let users customize before start.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

The direction is to lock this as a trust-first utility flow: Setup → Shielded turn engine → Finish/end → Recap/export → History, with no extra navigation branches for a playable session. The product value is in confidence that nothing leaks between hands and that the game always produces a complete, auditable recap.

Primary user flow:
Home, or first-run empty state, opens straight into setup because players want play, not menus. On Setup, 2–6 names are entered, one ruleset is selected/created, mode and attestations are set, and the game starts. In-game is a strict loop: shield state first, confirm-tap reveal, card/rule render, HUD update, handoff re-shields, next player. The game ends on first terminal condition (4th King, 10 rounds, or deck exhaustion, with 4th King winning if deck ends on that draw). Then Recap becomes the only next required screen, with export and timeline from immutable completed-game data, then History for retention and cleanup.

Screen inventory:
Home/Hub shows start game, rulesets, history, and settings; this screen also hosts first-run onboarding if needed and can show load-state errors from Core Data; key screen states are empty (no prior games), loading, success (menu available), error (retry with messaging).
Setup is split into Game Setup and Player Entry plus Ruleset+Mode panel; this is where the exact 2–6 gate, default mode, and ruleset choice happen; state model is invalid input error (name/rules count), creating-state loading, proceed-to-game success, and blocked if mode gate/permissions are missing.
Game Play is the single active session screen, with shield-first handoff as the canonical state; states are shielded (privacy lock, no card semantics in a11y tree), awaiting confirmation, revealed (card and rule visible), joker-text capture optional flow, apply-rule/hud update, and terminating transition.
Recap is a persisted-game detail screen, not an ephemeral live view; states are computing from timeline, success summary, empty-but-defensive state (“no custom/Joker rules played this game”), and recap load failure.
Export Overlay/Share path is separate enough to own failures clearly; states are permission-check, rendering/loading, cancelled (neutral), permission-denied with Settings recovery action, and hard render/share failure with explicit retry text.
History List uses swipe-to-delete as a trust primitive; states are loading, empty (“no games yet”), success list with end-reason badges, and delete error with undo/ retry action.
Ruleset Editor is pre-game only; players can edit/create/select card meanings and save snapshots; states are listing/loading, unsaved-changes warning, invalid-mapping error, save-in-progress, save success, and save failure with preserved edit state.
Sober/Drinking Mode Settings appears as a lightweight policy screen: default sober mode, gated activation gate for drinking mode, and a permanent note that recap copy is replay-fidelity from play time; states are normal, gate required, attestation failed, and mode-change success.
Onboarding state exists as an optional compact sheet tied to first launch and first rule conflict; states are unopened, viewed-and-accepted, skipped, and reopenable from Settings.

State model per screen, in implementation terms:
Home state is a snapshot state machine over persisted game/rules/history loading and error recovery.
Setup state holds player roster validation, selected ruleset pointer, mode and attestation intent, and a deterministic transition to Game Ready only when roster and required guards pass.
Game Play state is a pure state-machine host view with view-only derivations: hiddenCard, confirmArmed, activeCardShown, jokerCapturePending, turnResolved, handoffPending, gameOverReason. All UI states are projections, never sources of truth.
Recap state is read-only from persisted turn timeline and highlights model: computes winner metrics, end-reason label, funny-rule outcome candidate set with empty-state fallback preserved.
Export state records three-way outcome split and a rendered image artifact path for share/retry.
History state manages list data and delete operations with immediate optimistic UI update and reconciliation for Core Data failure.
Ruleset state manages card-to-copy map edits, validation completeness across all supported cards, and saves with schema-aware compatibility assumptions.

Visual direction:
Use a restrained, bold utility aesthetic: matte warm gradient background with subtle card/cup geometry, not flat monochrome, no ad-like ornamentation, and no purple-driven palette. Use one expressive display font for headings and a high-legibility sans for controls, with clear hierarchy and wide spacing; avoid visual noise during turns. The card region should have heavy contrast in both normal and low-light conditions, and the shield should be unmistakable (full-surface mask, distinct edges, clear prompt text). Minimal but intentional motion only: fade/shield transition and a brief tap-confirm pulse for confidence; no spinning loaders beyond short shimmer for async states.

Accessibility notes:
Every tappable control is >=44×44 points, labels are explicit, and state changes are announced with VoiceOver-friendly live regions. The hidden-card state must remove card content from the accessibility tree (`accessibilityHidden` or equivalent), not just visual blur/cover. During shielded handoff, no rank/suit/verb should be discoverable by screen reader. Dynamic Type must be supported without clipping, with a high-contrast option and reduced-motion compatibility for animations. Orientation and backgrounding paths must always force shielded state and explicit hidden semantics, and recap/share actions must be discoverable in VoiceOver with separate semantics for “cancel”, “blocked permission”, and “failed export.”

Biggest risk to this design is over-adding pre-session features into the first play loop and degrading the one-screen handoff loop speed; keeping the active-game path to a single state machine with deterministic transitions is non-negotiable. The detail that must survive is this: recap history is immutable to post-game mode changes and a “no custom/Joker rules this game” empty state is a valid outcome, so the schema must already assume persisted turn snapshots and per-turn mode capture from day one.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Let me lay out my strongest independent read on this, thinking about the actual person holding the phone at a party, half paying attention, a little tipsy by round six.

The screen list I'd build from is deliberately narrow, and I want to resist the urge to give this app a tab bar or a home screen with cards and sections — it's a single-session utility, not a hub. Setup is effectively home. From Setup you can reach a Ruleset picker/editor as a sheet, and there's a small, unobtrusive History entry point (top-corner icon, not a tab) since the app is opened fresh every time anyway — no persistent nav chrome competing with the one thing that matters, which is "get to the first card fast." So: Setup, Ruleset Editor (sheet), Active Game (which is really three states of one screen, not three screens — shield, reveal, and the Joker-capture moment layered on reveal), Recap, History List, History Detail (a saved recap reopened read-only), and the one-time Drinking Mode attestation dialog reachable from a settings affordance on Setup. That's it. If I find myself wanting a ninth screen I should ask whether it's actually a state of one of these seven.

The primary flow: launch always lands on Setup (never mid-game — that's already decided and I'm holding the line on it, because a "continue game?" prompt after a kill is a false promise given the app deliberately can't resume). Setup: enter 2–6 names, pick bundled or a saved ruleset, tap Start — deck locks at 53 cards. Then the loop: shield is the *first pixel* rendered on every turn transition, showing only "Pass to [Name]" and a big, unambiguous "Reveal" tap target — nothing about the card is in view or in the accessibility tree yet. Tap reveals card + rule text (sober or drinking copy) + any counter/cup-fill animation, and if it's a Joker, an inline "Set the house rule (optional)" field appears right there before the player moves on, not as a separate detour. HUD (Kings drawn of 4, cards remaining, round count only shown when mathematically reachable) is a slim persistent strip that never gets covered by the shield or reveal content — players should be able to glance at "how much game is left" without waiting for a lull. Player taps "Pass" (a distinct, deliberate action, not auto-advance on a timer) which immediately re-shields for the next player before any transition animation plays. That repeats until an end condition fires, which cuts straight to Recap — no "game over, tap to continue" middle step, the recap screen just *is* what you see. Recap shows the end-reason sentence, the honest timeline, the funniest-rule ballot (or its explicit empty state), and two actions: Export/Share and Play Again (which routes back to Setup, not a rematch-with-same-players shortcut — keeping it simple). The completed game is saved the moment the engine reaches its end state, before the player even looks at Recap, so a crash on the recap screen itself can't lose the record.

On states per screen, the one I care about most is the shield/reveal turn screen because it's repeated dozens of times a session and any ambiguity there is the whole ballgame. Shielded is the only default state — there's no "loading" state visible to the user because the shield itself covers any setup latency. Revealed state includes its own sub-state for Joker (optional text field, Save/Skip, defaulting silently to skip if they just tap Pass without engaging it — no forced modal blocking the flow). Setup needs a real inline validation error state for player count out of range and for duplicate/empty names, shown before Start is even tappable rather than after a failed attempt. Ruleset editor needs empty (first time, shows bundled defaults pre-filled), a saved-confirmation success state, and a blocking validation error if a card is left unmapped. Recap needs to handle the local save itself failing — I'd rather the recap still renders fully from in-memory state with a small non-blocking "couldn't save to history" banner than lose the recap the player is standing there waiting to screenshot. Export needs all four states named in the contract as genuinely distinct visuals, not just distinct strings: a quiet in-place spinner for loading, a success state that's just the native share sheet appearing (no extra "success!" screen needed, the share sheet itself is the confirmation), a permission-denied state with a real "Open Settings" button, and cancelled which should visually do nothing more than close the sheet — no toast, no error color, because punishing a cancel with red text is the fastest way to make the app feel broken on someone's very first share attempt. History list needs empty (first launch, "no games yet, play one!"), loaded-with-rows, per-row swipe-delete with a confirm on the destructive action, and a load-error state with retry.

Visual direction: I want this to feel like a single object being handed around, not a multi-section app — one card, centered, large, with the shield as an intentionally heavy, opaque full-bleed treatment (not a translucent blur that could hint at content underneath) using a lock glyph and the incoming player's name in large type, because that name is the one piece of information everyone in the group needs to see instantly to know whose turn it is. Calm, low-saturation palette by default (this has to work equally well for the sober-mode framing and not look like a party-flyer clip-art app), with the cup-fill visualization as the one place I'd spend real visual budget, since watching it fill is the game's built-in tension arc. Reduced-motion users get a static fill-level indicator instead of an animated pour.

Accessibility notes, beyond what's already ratified (accessibilityHidden on shielded card content, verified with VoiceOver): the "Pass to [Name]" label and the Reveal button must remain fully in the accessibility tree and clearly announced even while the card itself is hidden, otherwise a blind player has no way to know it's their turn at all — hiding the wrong layer is as bad as hiding nothing. Dynamic Type needs to be tested specifically on the reveal screen where card rank/suit/rule text and the Joker text field are all competing for space at accessibility text sizes — I'd rather the rule text wrap and scroll than truncate, since a truncated house rule defeats the entire funniest-rule feature. All primary actions (Reveal, Pass, Start, Export, Delete) need to be 44×44 minimum and vertically stacked, never side-by-side small buttons, because these are the exact taps happening under party conditions — slightly drunk, low light, one-handed.

The biggest risk to my own direction is that folding Joker-capture into the reveal screen instead of giving it a dedicated screen could make that one screen feel cluttered on the turn it matters most, and the biggest thing I don't want lost in synthesis is that the shield is not a screen you "navigate to" — it has to be the literal first frame rendered on any state transition into a new turn, with the pass/reveal button, not a separate animated hand-off screen layered in front of it.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini(key) -> Warning: 256-color support not detected. Using a terminal with at least 256-color support is recomme | gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · Closer) — Round 1**

Okay, here's my first-pass contribution as the Design Lead, aiming for a focused, critical first pass for this phase:

“Right, let’s be brutally clear: this isn’t about building a ‘cool’ Kings app. It’s about building a system that reliably handles a small piece of user trust—and we need to get that right from the start. My biggest concern is the shift to focus heavily on the Joker rule. We’re layering complexity on top of what is fundamentally a very simple game: draw a card, determine its effect, repeat. Introducing a custom rule mechanic adds an entirely new layer of state management, data storage, and UI logic that is unnecessary and frankly, likely to cause more problems than it solves. It’s a technical debt that’ll haunt us later. I’m going to defer the Joker-rule capture entirely until we have a solid, well-tested foundation for the core handoff.

My proposed direction is to build a core handoff flow—deterministic, reliable, and free of extraneous features—around the standard card drawing and rule execution. The game’s core is simple and the user experience should feel that way as well. The 'funniest-rule' mechanic is a separate feature, a potential ‘stretch goal’ for a future release. It shouldn’t drive the design of this initial MVP.

Specifically, I’m pushing back on the extensive data modeling – the insistence on tracking `mode` and capturing the Joker’s text. That’s simply not the right tradeoff for this level of complexity. We need to prioritize a clean, understandable state machine that reliably describes the game’s progress. Storing the custom rule in a text field and allowing users to enter their own will create significant maintenance overhead. It’s a complex system for a relatively simple user need.

I also want to flag the VoiceOver accessibility issue immediately.  If we’re building an app for anyone, we need to ensure it’s accessible to those who use assistive technologies. This requires completely removing the card’s content from the accessibility tree, a much more significant undertaking than a simple visual hiding. It’s a non-negotiable requirement.

Finally, I’m concerned about the long-term implications of the history persistence. While a default-off-delete is the simplest solution for this MVP, we need to build in a way that avoids accumulating a permanent log of user drinking/dare-making behavior – because that feels like a privacy issue. This system is for a simple game; it shouldn’t be a logbook for bad decisions.

My core recommendations for this phase’s outcome are these: 1) Defer the Joker-rule capture and implementation; 2) Focus on building a robust and deterministic handoff flow—card draw, rule execution, a reliable end-state (King or deck-exhaustion)—3) Explicitly define the accessibility shield as a content-removal barrier, verified with VoiceOver testing; and 4) Build in a minimal history system, allowing a swipe-to-delete on history rows to mitigate long-term data concerns. Let’s prioritize a clean, reliable core experience, not a complex feature set. Don’t make it look like a Swiss watch trying to track a single card—that’s not what people want.”

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Qwen2.5Vl (local) skipped — in cooldown (crash)._

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round 1 actually landed, and where it didn't quite close yet.

Codex and Claude agree on basically everything that matters for the actual gameplay loop — and this is just restating what's already been locked in over eight prior phases, not new debate: shield renders first on every turn with nothing about the card touchable by VoiceOver until reveal, confirm-tap is the only way a card shows, Joker capture happens inline on the reveal screen (not a separate detour), the HUD shows honest signals (Kings-of-4, cards remaining, round count only when reachable), recap is computed from the persisted/immutable turn timeline with replay-fidelity mode storage, export has the three distinct outcome states (cancel/permission-denied/failure) with cancel treated as a quiet no-op rather than an error, and history gets swipe-to-delete. Both also independently landed on the same instinct for the shield's accessibility floor: hide the card content from the tree entirely, but keep "Pass to [Name]" and the Reveal button fully readable by VoiceOver — Claude made that second half explicit and Codex didn't contradict it.

Where they actually diverge is navigation shape, and it's a real fork, not just phrasing. Codex wants a Home/Hub screen as the front door — start game, rulesets, history, settings all live there, with onboarding hosted on it too. Claude is pushing back hard on that: no hub, no tab bar, Setup *is* the first screen every launch (since a killed game never resumes anyway, there's nothing for a hub to triage), with History tucked behind a small corner icon and the Ruleset Editor as a sheet off Setup rather than its own top-level destination. Claude also adds a screen Codex's list doesn't have — a read-only History Detail view for reopening a saved recap — and treats Export as an action/state on the Recap screen rather than Codex's separate "Export Overlay" screen. These aren't contradictions so much as two different screen inventories that need to get reconciled into one.

Gemma3's entry this round is the same objection it's made in three previous phases now — defer Joker capture, don't bother tracking mode per-turn — both of which are settled decisions from detailed_discussion with explicit reasoning already on record. Not new material, doesn't move this round.

CONSENSUS: NO

Round 2 needs Codex and Claude to actually resolve the navigation question directly: is there a Home/Hub screen or does the app open straight to Setup with History/Rulesets as secondary entry points off it? And they should reconcile into one final screen inventory — settle whether History Detail is its own screen, whether Export is its own screen/overlay or a state of Recap, and where the one-time drinking-mode attestation and onboarding actually live in that structure.

### Round 2


**Codex (Product Strategist · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

I think we should close this fork now and adopt Claude’s shape, with a small tweak so it doesn’t actually contradict Codex’s concerns. For this app, “Setup is the first screen every time” is the stronger ship-fast choice because there’s no in-progress resume and no value in an extra triage screen before game start. A separate Hub only adds one more tap pattern for the core path and dilutes the privacy-first speed goal. At the same time, we can still satisfy your “home-like affordances” point by making the Setup screen explicitly function as the home surface: it’s where all global actions live, but the user still lands directly into setup controls by default.

So I want to collapse this into one root `Setup/Home` screen with a compact header/toolbar for optional paths. Example: Launch on fresh start shows player inputs + rule set + mode row + Start; top-right/history icon opens history (list), bottom toolbar or info button opens ruleset management/history/settings. Same screen reused after a game ends via Play Again so users can start another game in one tap if desired. This keeps the app in one mental model and avoids a “where am I now?” hop.

I disagree with introducing an explicit Export overlay screen. The recap screen already has export as a bounded flow with distinct outcomes, and the engine contract says we should avoid decorative screens. Keep `Recap` as primary completed-game artifact, and put share states there: preflight permission check, render/loading, cancelled/no-op, denied with Settings route, and hard failure with retry. That gives us the exact 3/4 export outcomes without adding another route.

For state paths, here’s the concrete split I’m proposing as the final inventory: `Setup/Home`, `Gameplay`, `Recap`, `History List`, `History Detail` (read-only), `Ruleset Editor`, and a lightweight `Mode Attestation` sheet. The recap/read-only history path is enough to satisfy “open past runs,” and it can be a pushed screen from history list so it’s discoverable but not part of the active game loop. Importantly, there is no separate “pass-to-next-screen” state; gameplay is a single screen with sub-states (`shielded`, `revealed`, `joker capture`, `handoff-locked`, `gameOver transition`) and `Ruleset Editor`, `Mode`, onboarding all launch as sheets from setup, not as extra top-level destinations.

Onscreen states are straightforward and buildable: Setup shows empty/default (first run), validation error (2–6 + names), loading (saving ruleset / starting), success-to-game, and blocked (attestation required if trying to enable drinking mode). Gameplay shows `hidden` fail-safe at all interruptions, `confirm to reveal`, `revealed`, optional `joker text` entry, `applying rule`, `handoff`, `ended`. Recap shows `from persisted game` loading/success with “no custom/Joker rules this game” as explicit empty state and save-error banner fallback. History list shows loading/empty/error/loading-retry/success. Ruleset editor shows list/unsaved/error/saving/saved. This maps directly to the phase checks and makes a SwiftUI view hierarchy obvious.

On visuals and accessibility, this structure still supports the trust-first requirement cleanly: shield-first handoff always and immediately, no visible card semantics in a11y tree while shielded, but keep turn label (“Pass to Alex”) and reveal control still discoverable in VoiceOver so accessibility doesn’t become a new leak vector. The whole point of a narrow flow is that every repeated action is fast and predictable — and this one-surface entry model does that best. Let's lock this as consensus and stop reopening screen-count debates.

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Codex, I want to zero in on the hub-vs-no-hub thing because I think it's the one real fork left and I don't think it's actually as far apart as the coordinator framed it — but I also don't want to just wave it away, because the difference matters for the exact moment this app lives or dies in: someone at a table saying "okay let's play" and unlocking their phone. Your hub gives Start Game, Rulesets, History, and Settings equal visual weight as four tiles on a landing screen. I think that's one tap too many for the case that matters 95% of the time. Nobody opens Kings to browse their rulesets library — they open it to play, right now, with the people sitting across from them. If the very first thing they see is a menu asking them to choose among four options before they can even type a name, that's friction on the golden path to save friction on paths almost nobody takes. So here's my concrete counter-proposal, which I think actually absorbs what you're going for without the tile-menu cost: Setup screen leads with name entry front and center — that's the primary content, not a tile among tiles — and History/Rulesets/Settings live as small icon affordances in the corners of that same screen, one tap away but visually subordinate. Functionally that gives you everything your hub gives you, just with an obvious visual hierarchy that says "this is what you're here to do" instead of "pick a category first."

On History Detail — you're right that my screen list had a gap, I said "History Detail (a saved recap reopened read-only)" like it's obviously a new screen, but thinking about it more, it shouldn't be a new screen at all, it should be the same Recap view with a different data source and a different bottom-action set. A just-finished game's Recap has "Export" and "Play Again" as its primary actions and maybe a transient "saved to history" confirmation; a reopened History row is exactly the same layout — end-reason, timeline, funniest-rule outcome, highlights — just with "Export" as the only action and no "Play Again" framing, since you're not mid-flow anymore. That's one SwiftUI view parameterized by context, not two screens to design, build, and keep visually consistent by hand. I think that's a real simplification worth locking in rather than treating as two items on the inventory.

Same instinct pushes me to disagree with your "Export Overlay/Share path is separate enough to own failures clearly" framing — I get the instinct, distinct failure modes deserve distinct clarity, but "separate enough to own failures clearly" doesn't require a separate screen, it requires a well-designed state on the screen that's already there. If Export is its own pushed screen, you've added a navigation transition and a back button to an action that should feel like "tap button, see share sheet" — one beat, not two. I'd rather the loading/permission-denied/failure states render as an inline status area right on the Recap screen near the Export button (small spinner replacing the button while the image renders, then the system share sheet takes over, and if something goes wrong an inline banner appears with the retry or Settings-deeplink action) than push the user to a whole new screen just to watch a spinner. The four states still get the clarity your framing wants — I just don't think clarity requires a new route.

Where I do want to concede ground to you: your "Sober/Drinking Mode Settings" as its own small screen is better than what I had, which was just "a settings affordance on Setup." Bundling the mode toggle, the one-time attestation gate, and the replay-fidelity explainer copy into one dedicated screen, reachable from a corner icon on Setup, is cleaner than me trying to cram a toggle into Setup's main view. I'll take that. And onboarding — I'd anchor it as a one-time sheet that fires automatically on first launch from Setup, reopenable from that same Mode/Settings screen, rather than a hub tile; that matches what you said about it being "reopenable from Settings" almost exactly, so I think we're already aligned there, just describing it from different starting screens.

So here's where I'd land the final inventory, and I think this actually reconciles both our lists rather than picking a winner: Setup is the single front door every launch, with name entry as the hero content and three small corner affordances (History, Rulesets, Mode/Settings); Ruleset Editor opens as a sheet from Setup; Mode/Settings is a small pushed screen with the attestation gate baked in; Active Game is one screen with the shield/reveal/joker-capture states we both already agree on cold; Recap is one reusable view for both the just-finished game and any reopened History row, with Export as an in-place state on that screen rather than a separate destination; and History List sits behind its corner icon with swipe-to-delete. That's six real screens plus one reusable dual-context view, which is tighter than either of our round-1 lists and I think closes the exact gap the coordinator flagged without either of us just conceding to the other's structure wholesale.

On Gemma3 — same pattern as every phase so far, re-arguing to cut Joker capture and mode-per-turn tracking, both settled with explicit reasoning three phases ago. I don't think it needs a fresh rebuttal from me this round, it's not new material and it's not about navigation, which is what round 2 was actually supposed to resolve.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini(key) -> Warning: 256-color support not detected. Using a terminal with at least 256-color support is recomme | gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Gemma3 (local) (Product Strategist · Closer) — Round 2**

Okay, let's do this. Here’s my contribution as Gemma3, aiming for that pragmatic, skeptical Product Strategist voice:

“Alright, hold on a second. I’m seeing a lot of hand-wringing about complexity here, and frankly, I’m not entirely convinced it’s the *right* complexity to address. This isn't about building a ‘trust-first’ app – that’s a noble goal, but it's also a trap if you build it with a feature set that’s more suited to a banking app than a casual card game. We're talking about Kings, not a financial institution.

I’m hearing a lot of talk about deferring the Joker rule capture. Look, I get it – it’s a complex UI element. But it’s *optional*. It’s a ‘stretch goal.’ It's not core to the game's fundamental design. If the core handoff flow – the Shield, Confirm-Tap, Reveal – works flawlessly, *nobody* will notice the Joker. If it doesn’t, that’s a separate issue to address. Pushing this to a future release is a classic ‘analysis paralysis’ move—let’s focus on shipping the damn thing, and iterate later. We can add the optional Joker feature, and if players love it, fantastic. But we’re not building this app to be the ultimate Joker-rule engine; we're building a fun, reliable game.

And let’s talk about this ‘replay-fidelity mode’ – storing the per-turn mode state. This is where I’m seeing the most serious architectural problems. We’re talking about persisting a *state* that's inherently unstable – a game is constantly changing. We don't want a scenario where someone’s play session is wiped out because the app ran out of memory, and then they have to start over, remembering all the last details.  Storing the mode *adds* complexity, not simplifies it.  It's an unnecessary layer of overhead, and I'm concerned it will make the codebase brittle and harder to maintain. Let's just stick to the simple truth: the game ends when the deck is exhausted, and the recap reflects that.

I also disagree with the insistence on removing the card content from the accessibility tree.  Yes, it’s a potential challenge, but it’s a solvable one. We’re building an iOS app—accessibility is a core part of the OS. There are well-established patterns for this. It’s not a fundamental barrier; it’s a detail that needs to be addressed – and let’s address it head-on, not bury it under layers of abstraction.  It's not about building an accessibility-perfect app; it's about building an app that *works* for everyone.

Finally, I think this obsession with the history list is misplaced. A brief, easily-accessible summary of the previous game is fine. But a full, searchable history of every single game played? That’s a maintenance nightmare.  Let’s keep it simple—one, maybe two, easily-accessible saved recaps.

I’m proposing we drop the ‘should’ and ‘could’ from our list, and focus *solely* on the ‘must’ – a robust, deterministic handoff flow, a clear end-state, and a basic history. We can add the Joker feature, the per-turn mode capture, and the detailed history list later – if we choose to. But right now, we need to ship a working game, not a feature-rich, overly-complex system. Let’s focus on the core mechanics, not the technical bells and whistles. I'm going to push for a single, streamlined view for the active game state— Shield, Reveal, Card, and the confirm tap—and leave the rest for a second iteration."

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Qwen2.5Vl (local) skipped — in cooldown (crash)._

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Round 2 actually closed the real fork. Codex read Claude's no-hub argument and just agreed with it outright — "I think we should close this fork now and adopt Claude's shape" — collapsing the Home/Hub idea into Setup itself acting as the front door, with History/Rulesets/Settings as corner affordances rather than a menu of tiles someone has to pick through before they can even type a name. Codex also independently dropped his own "Export Overlay" screen idea in this same round, landing on exactly what Claude wanted: export lives as an inline state right on the Recap screen (spinner → native share sheet → banner for denied/failed), not a separate route. Claude then took that same "don't add a screen when a state will do" logic one step further and proposed folding History Detail into the same Recap view too — one view, parameterized by whether it's a just-finished game (with Play Again) or a reopened history row (without it) — which nobody's pushed back on and it's a direct extension of the principle Codex had already applied to Export. The one place they swapped: Claude conceded that Codex's dedicated Mode/Settings screen (toggle + attestation + replay-fidelity explainer, reachable from a corner icon) beats her original plan of cramming that into Setup directly, and took it as-is.

So the two design leads ended up in the same place from opposite starting points, which is a real convergence, not a compromise where something got smoothed over.

Gemma3's round 2 is the same objections as every prior round — defer Joker capture, drop per-turn mode storage, and now also pushing back on accessibility-tree removal and trimming history down to "one or two recaps." All four were already settled with explicit reasoning several phases ago (the funniest-rule ballot literally can't function without Joker capture; replay-fidelity was chosen specifically for being the more deterministic option; accessibility-hidden shielding is a named hard requirement precisely because VoiceOver would otherwise read a "hidden" card aloud). Nothing here is new engagement with this phase's actual navigation question, so it doesn't reopen anything.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory (7 screens, one reused in two contexts):**
1. **Setup/Home** — the app's single front door on every launch (no mid-game resume, so there's never anything to triage). Name entry for 2–6 players is the hero content; ruleset selector and Start button sit with it; small corner icons open History, Ruleset Editor, and Mode/Settings.
2. **Ruleset Editor** — sheet launched from Setup. Edit card-to-copy mappings, save/name rulesets, pick from saved ones.
3. **Mode/Settings** — small pushed screen from Setup's corner icon. Sober-default toggle, drinking-mode one-time self-attestation gate, plain-language replay-fidelity explainer ("changing mode later won't rewrite old games"), and the entry point to reopen onboarding.
4. **Active Game** — one screen, several sub-states, never a separate "pass to next player" screen: shielded (default/fail-safe state), confirm-to-reveal, revealed (card + rule + HUD update), optional inline Joker text capture, immediate re-shield on handoff, end-of-game transition.
5. **Recap** — one reusable view for two contexts: a just-finished game (Export + Play Again, transient "saved" confirmation) and a reopened History row (Export only, read-only, no Play Again). Shows end-reason, honest partial-round timeline, funniest-rule ballot or its explicit empty state, highlights. Export is an inline state on this screen — status area/spinner, then the system share sheet, then a banner for permission-denied/failure, silent close on user-cancel — not a separate route.
6. **History List** — behind its corner icon, swipe-to-delete per row, tapping a row pushes Recap in its read-only context.
7. **Onboarding** — one-time sheet firing automatically on first launch, reopenable anytime from Mode/Settings.

**Primary user flow:** Launch always lands on Setup/Home (first-run also surfaces the onboarding sheet) → enter 2–6 names, pick/confirm ruleset and mode → Start locks the 53-card deck → Active Game loop: shield renders first on every turn, confirm-tap reveals card + rule (sober/drinking copy), Joker draws offer optional inline text capture, HUD updates, immediate re-shield before any handoff UI → repeats until the first of 4th King / 10 rounds / deck exhaustion (cup-full wins a simultaneous tie) → completed game is persisted to CoreData the instant the engine ends, before Recap even renders → Recap shows the finished game with Export and Play Again → Play Again returns cleanly to Setup/Home. Independently, History List is reachable anytime from Setup and opens past games into the same Recap view, read-only.

**State model per screen:** Setup — default/first-run, inline validation error (player count/names), loading (starting/saving ruleset), blocked (attestation required for drinking mode), success-to-game. Active Game — shielded (fails safe on backgrounding/rotation/rapid taps/notifications/system interruptions), confirm-armed, revealed, joker-capture-pending (Save/Skip), applying-rule, handoff-locked, game-over transition. Recap — loading from persisted record, success, explicit "no custom/Joker rules this game" empty state, non-blocking "couldn't save to history" banner if the local save itself fails, and the four export sub-states (loading, success/share-sheet, cancelled-as-no-op, permission-denied-with-Settings-link, hard-failure-with-retry). History List — loading, empty ("no games yet"), success list with end-reason badges, delete confirmation, load-error with retry. Ruleset Editor — loading/list, unsaved-changes warning, invalid-mapping error (unmapped card), saving, save success, save failure with edits preserved. Mode/Settings — normal, attestation-required gate, attestation-failed, mode-change success.

**Visual direction:** A restrained, bold utility aesthetic — matte warm gradient background with subtle card/cup geometry, no ad-like ornamentation, no flat monochrome, no purple-driven palette. One expressive display font for headings, a high-legibility sans for controls, generous spacing, minimal visual noise during turns. One large centered card as the focal object; the shield is a fully opaque, unmistakable full-bleed mask (not a translucent blur that could hint at content underneath), showing a lock glyph and the incoming player's name in large type — that name is the one thing everyone at the table needs to read instantly. Cup-fill visualization gets the real visual/animation budget as the game's built-in tension arc; reduced-motion users get a static fill-level indicator instead of an animated pour. Calm, low-saturation default palette that works equally well under sober-mode framing. Minimal, purposeful motion only: shield transition fade and a brief tap-confirm pulse.

**Accessibility notes:** All primary actions (Reveal, Pass, Start, Export, Delete) are ≥44×44pt and stacked vertically, not side-by-side, for one-handed party-condition use. Shielded card content is removed from the accessibility tree entirely (`accessibilityHidden` or equivalent) — visual hiding alone is not sufficient — verified with a dedicated VoiceOver-on QA pass; but the "Pass to [Name]" label and Reveal control stay fully in the tree and clearly announced, since hiding that layer too would leave a blind player unable to tell it's their turn. Dynamic Type is tested specifically on the reveal screen where rank/suit/rule text and the Joker field compete for space — rule text wraps and scrolls rather than truncates, since a truncated house rule breaks the funniest-rule feature outright. High-contrast and reduced-motion options are supported; orientation changes and backgrounding always force the shielded state and its hidden accessibility semantics, with fail-safe-hidden behavior also verified against full-screen system interruptions like calls and Face ID prompts, not just banners.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This closes the phase well. The one genuine fork from round 1 (Home/Hub vs. Setup-as-front-door) is resolved with real convergence, not a smoothed-over compromise: Codex explicitly adopted Claude's no-hub structure, then independently applied the same "state not a screen" logic to drop his own Export Overlay screen, and Claude extended that same logic one step further by folding History Detail into the reusable Recap view. The one swap (Claude conceding Codex's dedicated Mode/Settings screen) is a specific, traceable concession, not hand-waving.

All five required outputs are present and concrete: a 7-screen inventory (with one screen correctly reused across two contexts rather than needlessly duplicated) with clear purpose and entry points for each; a primary user flow that traces start-to-finish and matches the ratified engine rules (three-way end condition, cup-full tie-break, persist-before-recap-renders ordering); a per-screen state model detailed enough to build from (Setup's inline validation and blocked/attestation state, Active Game's full sub-state list including joker-capture-pending and fail-safe shielded, Recap's four distinct export sub-states matching the ratified cancel/denied/failure split, History List's empty/loading/error/delete-confirm states, Ruleset Editor's unsaved/invalid-mapping/save-failure states); a specific, non-generic visual direction (opaque full-bleed shield with lock glyph and player name as the one must-read element, cup-fill as the dedicated animation budget, reduced-motion fallback); and accessibility notes that go beyond boilerplate (explicitly distinguishing what stays in the accessibility tree — the "Pass to [Name]" label and Reveal control — from what's removed, and calling out Dynamic Type contention specifically on the reveal screen where truncation would silently break the funniest-rule feature).

Nothing here contradicts prior ratified decisions (Joker capture, replay-fidelity mode storage, accessibility-hidden shielding, swipe-to-delete history, three-way export states, schemaVersion-backed persistence) — it's all consistently referenced and built into the concrete screen/state design. Gemma3's round 2 objections (defer Joker capture, drop per-turn mode storage, weaken accessibility-tree removal, trim history) are correctly identified as repeat objections to already-settled ground rather than new engagement with this phase's navigation question, so treating them as non-blocking is fair.

A SwiftUI developer could build the view hierarchy directly from this output, and every required user workflow (setup, play, ruleset editing, mode/attestation, recap, export, history browse/delete, onboarding) has a clear screen/state path. No further repair needed.

See the coordinator's message above.

**Screen inventory (7 screens, one reused in two contexts):**
1. **Setup/Home** — the app's single front door on every launch (no mid-game resume, so there's never anything to triage). Name entry for 2–6 players is the hero content; ruleset selector and Start button sit with it; small corner icons open History, Ruleset Editor, and Mode/Settings.

### Design Handoff

# drinking-games-test — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-09 11:19:13._

## Original Prompt

```
Build Kings — a multiplayer drinking game app for iOS (local pass-and-play).

Core mechanics:
- 2–6 players draw cards from a deck in turn order
- Each card (A–K, plus Joker) triggers a rule: e.g. "2 = You" (drinker picks someone), "5 = Guys", "K = King's Cup" (build communal cup with dares), "Joker = Make a rule"
- Players drink per the card rule; when the cup is empty or 10 turns pass, game ends with a recap
- Pass phone between players; each player sees only their own card until they pass

Key features:
- Local only (no server, no login)
- Customizable rules: edit card meanings before each game, save rulesets
- Game history: recap shows who drank most, funniest rule, timeline of rounds
- Sober mode toggle (default): uses "dare" language instead of drinking; activating drinking mode (17+ gate) swaps copy to drinking references but same mechanics

Tech:
- SwiftUI, local-first with CoreData for game history
- Phone must stay face-down while other player holds it; privacy shield until handoff
- Export game recap as a sharable image (name, date, player list, highlights)

Scope: MVP with Kings rules only (not Hearts, Asshole, or other variants). One ruleset bundled; let users customize before start.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce a complete design handoff: screen-by-screen specs, interaction states, motion notes, design tokens, accessibility expectations, and a professional Claude Design prompt or import plan if the run is configured to pause for external design.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

This is the version I’d ship as the first handoff pass: a fast, trust-first, premium utility flow with Setup as the home, a single active-game screen, and recap/history behavior that is always consistent and recoverable.

I’m anchoring the whole design on one rule: the shield is never optional and is the first paint before every handoff, then every other element is just utility around that certainty. The biggest risk is a one-frame or one-accessibility-tree leak at transition; everything below is shaped to remove that failure mode before we optimize visuals.

Setup/Home is the first screen on every cold launch and the only normal landing point. It is a setup-first layout: hero title, 2–6 player name fields, ruleset selector chip, Start button, and unobtrusive top-corner controls for History, Rulesets, and Mode/Settings. In a normal happy path a group can reach first draw in under 10 taps.
Screen layout: app bar with "Build Kings", hero card icon, vertical player rows with inline add/remove controls capped at 2–6, a ruleset row that shows active set and “edit,” a sober/drinking mode status label with lock icon for 17+ attestation when needed, a large Start action, and one tiny row of small utility footnote links (privacy reminder and onboarding reopen).  
State variants: first-run with one empty baseline field visible, validation-error when player count/name invalid, loading while game service primes deck/ruleset, blocked when drinking mode needs attestation, and success transition into active game. For load failures that can surface here (like ruleset fetch/save), show inline non-blocking banner and keep inputs editable.  
Accessibility: each control is ≥44×44pt, error labels announced, focus order from name fields to Start, and mode status announced as a single sentence so one-handed users can recover quickly.  
Motion: no screen-flip on first render; subtle fade-in and focused field highlight only.  

Active Game is a strict state machine screen, not a navigation route.  
Layout: full-screen matte card surface with one centered card area; top HUD strip with Kings drawn, cards remaining, and round indicator only when mathematically meaningful for current player count; bottom action strip with large Confirm and Pass controls; right rail with optional drink/dare count micro-summary; optional inline Joker field appears only when Joker is revealed.  
State variants: shielded fail-safe (default), reveal-armed with pass target label, revealed card and rule text, Joker entry (Save/Skip), applying rule + haptic cue, pass-locked handoff, and game-over transition.  
Critical interaction: shielded state must always render before any pass-to-next UI on transition; if app backgrounds, rotates, receives notification banner, call, Face ID/pin overlay, or rapid repeated taps occur, immediately drop back to shielded state.  
State persistence in engine terms must be deterministic and idempotent: one draw per confirm; duplicate taps are ignored while confirm is processing.  
Accessibility: shielded card content is removed from the accessibility tree entirely (`accessibilityHidden` for the card content subtree). The “Pass to [Next Player]” label and Reveal button stay discoverable.  
Motion: shield fade/slide at 120–180ms + a short confirm pulse; cup-fill fill-beat only when a King occurs (or equivalent event), with reduced-motion fallback to static update.

Recap is a reusable view with two modes: immediate completed-game and read-only historical game.  
Layout: recap header (game name/date/mode label), end-reason ribbon, compact timeline list showing rounds/events (including partial final round), highlight panel (who drank most), funniest rule result area with explicit fallback text when none were played, and action row with Export and Play Again when in just-finished context. In historical context hide Play Again and keep Export only.  
State variants: loading from persisted record, success, no-custom-or-Joker-empty-state, export-loading/rendering, permission-denied with Settings deep-link action, export-failure with retry, cancelled no-op, and local save warning banner if history persistence fails while timeline remains visible.  
Interaction: share is inline on this screen, not a separate route; after tapping Export, show spinner, invoke share sheet, recover gracefully on permission-denied and hard errors.  
Accessibility: timeline and highlights are in a readable stack order; long card/rule text wraps and scrolls (never truncates) at large text sizes.  

History List is a quick-access utility screen behind its icon, not a hub.  
Layout: list of sessions with end-reason chip, players, and date; each row supports swipe-to-delete and confirmation dialog; row tap opens Recap in read-only mode.  
State variants: loading, empty (“no games yet”), success list, delete-confirmation in progress, load/save error with retry.  
Motion: delete interaction uses quick destructive row reveal; no celebratory animation or toasts.

Ruleset Editor is pre-game only and always launched from Setup.  
Layout: full card list for all values A–K plus Joker with editable text fields and default-value reset actions; ruleset name field, save/cancel actions, and selector for using a saved set.  
State variants: list/loading, unsaved-changes warning, validation failure if any card is unmapped, saving, save-success, save-failed.  
Interaction: save blocks until every card has a value; duplicate names for rulesets prompt overwrite confirmation.  
Motion: single fade + list diffing, no heavy transitions.

Mode/Settings is a small dedicated screen from Setup corner control.  
Layout: sober-mode status at top, drinking-mode toggle with required one-time self-attestation flow, replay-fidelity statement block (“changing mode later does not rewrite old recaps”), and action button to reopen onboarding.  
State variants: normal, gate requested, attestation failed/undoable explanation, mode toggle success.  
Accessibility: explicit semantics for toggle, clear confirmation copy before lock-in.  

Onboarding is a one-time sheet and reopenable from Mode/Settings.  
Layout: 3-step visual walkthrough cards: shield-first handoff, mandatory confirm flow, recap integrity rules (three end reasons + deterministic behavior). One “Understood” CTA and “Show Again” action in settings.  
State variants: first-run forced, reviewed, skipped, and reopen success/close.  
Motion: no tutorial carousel auto-scroll; one card at a time with simple progress dots.

Screen-to-screen flow is explicit and tight:
Launch to Setup/Home → player setup and optional mode/ruleset selection → Start → Active Game loop (shield, reveal, apply, pass/re-shield) → end transition → Recap (save-once to persistence before render) → Export or Play Again (back to Setup).  
Parallel read-only path: Setup icon to History List → row tap → Recap read-only; Setup icon to Ruleset Editor; Setup icon to Mode/Settings.

Component inventory:
Primary action button style used for Start, Reveal, Pass, Export, Play Again.
Shield overlay component with lock icon, “Pass to [name]” line, and confirm CTA.
Card surface component with explicit front/back rendering and masked-a11y behavior.
HUD strip component with Kings-of-4, remaining cards, optional rounds.
Timeline row component with timestamp-like turn index + rule text.
Mode badge token for sober/drinking per-turn rendering snapshot.
Joker capture form with optional short text field, Save/Skip actions.
History row component with end-reason token and swipe-delete.
Export status banner component with four outcomes.

Design tokens:
palette warm and non-purple: gradientStart #1E130A, gradientMid #3D2614, gradientEnd #6C5037, surface #F5EFE6, surfaceElevated #FFF8EE, primaryText #16100B, secondaryText #4E4238, accentGold #DFAE70, positive #2E8B6D, warning #B06C2D, danger #AF3F3F, border #D8C6B1.  
Typography: display heading family "Playfair Display" with heavy/bold weight, body family "Nunito Sans" for controls and hierarchy; button text uses semibold body family, note text uses small regular. If unavailable, fallback to SF Rounded plus SF Pro with no functional loss.  
Spacing scale: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 32, 48.
Corner radii: 12 for controls, 18 for overlays, 24 for card surface.
Shadows: subtle depth token, elevation-low 0,2,8 and elevation-mid 0,4,12 only.
Motion durations: 120ms for shield reveal/hide, 180ms for action state transitions, 260ms for list inserts.  
Duration reductions: when Reduce Motion is enabled, only state changes occur without transform/opacity choreography.

Accessibility requirements are not optional:
Every required action control is 44×44pt min and vertically discoverable.
High-contrast variants preserve contrast in low-light and bright ambient conditions.
Dynamic Type on reveal + Joker capture can scroll; no truncation on rule text.
Shielded card content is removed from VoiceOver entirely while active; pass ownership and turn cues remain spoken.
App lifecycle interruption and rotation both force shielded state and hidden card semantics.
Export/share and history operations expose explicit error copy with recovery paths and avoid ambiguous silent states.

Privacy/security interaction rules that must stay fixed:
Fail-safe hidden state on every interruption.
No history detail reinterpretation by current mode; recap rendering is replay-fidelity from persisted turn timeline snapshot per event/mode.
If permissions are blocked, explicit blocked-state actions appear with Recovery to Settings.
No background mid-game resume; if the app is killed, relaunch goes to fresh Setup.

The detail this handoff must preserve above all is that the experience is boringly predictable rather than feature-rich: one tap to reveal per player, no frame where the wrong user can see card semantics, and recaps are immutable historical artifacts.

If you want an external Claude Design pass instead of implementing manually, here is a direct prompt:
“Design a production-grade SwiftUI interface for a local pass-and-play Kings card app called Build Kings in iOS 17+. Prioritize trust-first privacy (mandatory shield-first handoff), deterministic state flow, and party-condition usability. Use these screens: Setup/Home, Active Game, Recap (dual-mode completed vs historical), History List, Ruleset Editor, Mode/Settings, Onboarding. Include exact empty/loading/success/error states per screen, a11y constraints, motion tokens, and component states for shielded, revealed, joker-capture, export-cancel/denied/failure, and swipe-to-delete history. Keep palette warm, premium, non-purple, non-dark default with reduced-motion and Dynamic Type support, and provide spacing/typography/component-level specs ready for SwiftUI implementation.”

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Coming at this as the frontend engineer who actually has to build the NavigationStack and the state machine — my job this round is to turn the seven-screen inventory into something a SwiftUI builder can start typing into Xcode without guessing, and to flag the places where the architecture as currently described has gaps nobody's caught yet.

The first thing I want to name before anything else: there's a real inconsistency sitting between two ratified decisions from different phases that nobody's reconciled. The data model was explicitly built to support toggling sober/drinking mode mid-session — Codex's round-3 detailed_discussion language is "if someone toggles sober/drinking mid-session, every subsequent turn logs the active mode at that moment" — but the design_discussion screen inventory only gives you a way to reach Mode/Settings from a corner icon on Setup, and Setup is never visited again once a game starts (there's no route back to it mid-game, by design, since the whole point of the no-hub structure is that Active Game is a closed loop until it ends). So either the per-turn mode storage is solving for a scenario the UI can never actually produce — nobody can toggle mode mid-game because there's no path to the toggle — or Active Game needs a small settings affordance I don't see specified anywhere. I'd rather we decide this explicitly than have it fall out by accident: my proposal is a small unobtrusive icon in the Active Game HUD strip (not competing visually with Reveal/Pass) that pushes Mode/Settings as a sheet without leaving the active session, so the per-turn storage decision actually has a UI that can exercise it. If the room decides mid-game mode switching isn't actually a real use case worth a UI affordance, that's fine too — but then we should say so explicitly and treat per-turn mode storage as "technically correct schema design for a scenario nobody triggers in v1," not leave it ambiguous.

On architecture: this whole app should be one `@Observable` `GameEngine` (pure Swift per the contract) held at the App root and injected via environment, with a thin `PersistenceController` that only gets touched at defined boundaries — loading the active ruleset once at Setup, writing the completed game once at game-end, and history list/delete operations — never inside the per-turn hot path. That matters for the shield: the moment CoreData work sneaks into the reveal/handoff loop, you introduce exactly the kind of unpredictable latency that turns a deterministic 150ms transition into a variable one, and variable latency around the shield is where leak bugs live (a frame where the fetch hasn't returned yet but the shield has already started to lift). Navigation should be a single `NavigationStack` off Setup/Home, with Ruleset Editor and Mode/Settings as `.sheet`, Active Game pushed as a value-type route, and Recap parameterized by an explicit `RecapContext` enum (`.justFinished(GameID)` vs `.historyRow(GameID)`) rather than two initializers with default params — that enum is the thing that keeps Play-Again-vs-read-only from drifting apart as two people touch the same view over time. One more state-restoration gotcha worth writing down now: SwiftUI scene state restoration will happily try to resurrect the NavigationStack path on relaunch, including an in-flight Active Game route — that directly contradicts the ratified "a killed game is simply gone" decision, so Active Game's route needs to be explicitly excluded from restoration (don't persist it in the restorable path, or wipe path-to-ActiveGame on `scenePhase == .active` after a cold launch).

On the shield itself, since it's still the highest-stakes surface: `scenePhase` alone isn't tight enough — its transitions can lag a runloop tick behind the actual interruption. I'd pair it with `UIApplication.willResignActiveNotification`/`didBecomeActiveNotification` observers wrapped in a single `.privacyShielded()` view modifier applied at the root of Active Game, so shielding is enforced structurally rather than something each screen state has to remember to check. And I want to flag a transition-design risk directly: don't build the reveal as a 3D card-flip animation. It's tempting visually, but a flip implies two faces existing in the same view hierarchy during the transition, and that's precisely the kind of transitional-frame risk research already named as a top concern — a hard cross-fade (shield out, card in, no shared transform where both are simultaneously composited) is the only transition that can't leak a frame by construction.

For the screen-by-screen build spec: Setup/Home is a `Form`-adjacent single scroll view — name entry rows first (each a `PlayerNameEntryRow` with inline validation, live-updating a count badge that goes red/blocks Start outside 2–6), ruleset picker below, corner toolbar items for History/Rulesets/Settings using SF Symbols (`clock.arrow.circlepath`, `slider.horizontal.3`, `gearshape`) sized at the 44×44 minimum tap target even though the glyph itself is smaller. Ruleset Editor is a sheet with `.interactiveDismissDisabled(hasUnsavedChanges)` plus a confirmation dialog on attempted swipe-dismiss, since "unsaved-changes warning" isn't a real state unless dismissal is actually interceptable.
