Active Game is the one screen that's really a state machine rendered as a view — I'd model it as a single root view switching on `engine.turnPhase: TurnPhase` (`.shielded`, `.confirmArmed`, `.revealed`, `.jokerCapture`, `.applyingRule`, `.handoffLocked`, `.gameOver`), with the shield state literally short-circuiting the render tree (an early return, not a ZStack overlay with opacity 0 that still exists underneath) so there's no world where the card view is instantiated and just visually covered — instantiated-but-hidden is a bug waiting to happen the moment someone adds a debug overlay or a11y inspector. Recap is one view keyed by `RecapContext`, loading its data via a small `RecapViewModel` that reads the persisted record and never touches live engine state, which is what actually gives you replay-fidelity for free — if Recap read from `GameEngine` instead of the CoreData row, mode-at-play-time would already be lost by the time you view it. History List is a straightforward `List` with `.swipeActions(edge: .trailing)` and a `ConfirmationDialog` before delete actually commits, empty state centered with a simple illustration-free icon-plus-text treatment.

On tokens: for typography, I'd use zero third-party fonts and lean entirely on system fonts per the cheatsheet's "prefer first-party" principle — New York (`Font.system(.largeTitle, design: .serif).weight(.semibold)`) as the expressive display face for the shield's incoming-player name and game-over/recap headlines, and standard SF Pro (`.default` design) for every control, body, and HUD label. That gets you the "one expressive display font for headings, high-legibility sans for controls" requirement from design_discussion without adding a font file, a licensing question, or a Dynamic-Type-metrics headache — New York scales with Dynamic Type identically to SF because they share the same underlying text-style system. Spacing scale: 4/8/12/16/24/32/48pt, corner radius 16pt for cards and rows, 0pt (full-bleed, hard edge) for the shield mask specifically because a rounded or inset shield is a visual crack that undermines "this is an absolute barrier." Color should be defined as semantic asset-catalog colors, not hardcoded hex, so light/dark variants are free: `bg.primary` (warm amber-to-terracotta gradient stops), `action.primary` (a single warm coral/amber used for every primary CTA — Start, Reveal, Export — so there's exactly one color players learn to associate with "go"), `shield.mask` (near-black opaque, deliberately not sampled from the background gradient, so it reads as a hard interruption rather than a themed screen), `cup.fill` (warm gold, four discrete segments), and standard semantic red/amber/gray for hard-failure/permission-denied/cancelled-as-no-op so those three export states are visually distinct at a glance, not just distinct in copy.

Component inventory to hand a builder: PrivacyShieldView, CardFaceView (sober/drinking copy variants injected, never branched inline), JokerCaptureField, GameHUDBar, CupFillView (animated spring fill + static reduced-motion variant as two explicit render paths, not one path with animation duration set to zero), PlayerNameEntryRow, RulesetMappingRow, RecapHighlightCard, FunniestRuleBallotView + its paired EmptyStateView, RecapTimelineList, ExportStatusView (the inline spinner/banner states live here, one view, four render branches), HistoryRow, EmptyStateView (generic, reused across History/Ruleset/Recap), ErrorBanner (generic, reused, takes a retry closure), ModeAttestationSheet, OnboardingPageView. Motion: shield cross-fade ~180ms ease-in-out, tap-confirm pulse 1.0→1.04→1.0 over 150ms, cup-fill spring animation ~450ms per segment with a hard reduced-motion branch that snaps to final fill level with no animation at all (not just a shorter duration — actually zero animation, since some reduced-motion users find any movement bothersome, not just fast movement).

On accessibility beyond what's ratified: Dynamic Type needs an explicit ceiling test at accessibility XXL sizes on the reveal screen specifically, since that's where card text, rule text, and the Joker field compete — my recommendation is the rule text area becomes independently scrollable within a fixed-height card region rather than letting the whole screen grow unbounded, so Reveal/Pass stay reachable without scrolling past them.

Last thing: whether we need external Claude Design at all. My take is no, not for screens — the token system above (system fonts, semantic color roles, SF Symbols for icons, motion specs) is concrete enough that a SwiftUI builder can go straight from this document to code with zero visual-design handoff gap, and generating full mockups externally would just be work someone has to then faithfully re-translate into SwiftUI anyway. The one place I'd consider a narrow, scoped Claude Design prompt is a small custom glyph set — the shield's lock mark, a King's-crown motif for the cup-fill milestone markers, maybe a custom app icon — since SF Symbols covers functional iconography fine but a distinctive lock/crown mark is what would make the shield and cup feel designed rather than assembled from system parts. That's a bounded, low-risk ask (a handful of monochrome vector marks, not full UI), not a reason to hand the whole handoff over to an external pass.

**Gemini — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini(key) -> Warning: 256-color support not detected. Using a terminal with at least 256-color support is recomme | gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Gemma3 (local) (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 1**

Okay, let’s get this done. Here’s my first-pass contribution as the Design Lead:

Right, let’s be brutally clear: this isn’t about building a ‘trust-first’ app – that’s a noble goal, but it’s also a trap if you build it with a feature set that’s more suited to a banking app than a casual card game. We’re talking Kings, not a financial institution.

My biggest concern is the shift to focus heavily on the Joker rule. We’re layering complexity on top of what is fundamentally a simple game: draw a card, determine its effect, repeat. Introducing a custom rule mechanic adds an entirely new layer of state management, data storage, and UI logic that is unnecessary and likely to cause more problems than it solves. It’s a technical debt that’ll haunt us later. I’m going to defer the Joker-rule capture entirely until we have a solid, well-tested foundation for the core handoff – the shield, confirm-tap, reveal, and joker-capture moment – and then we can revisit it if it proves to be genuinely valuable.

My proposed direction is to build a core handoff flow – deterministic, reliable, and free of extraneous features – around the standard card drawing and rule execution. The game’s core is simple and the user experience should feel that way as well. The ‘funniest-rule’ mechanic is a separate feature, a potential ‘stretch goal’ for a future release. It shouldn’t drive the design of this initial MVP.

Specifically, I’m pushing back on the extensive data modeling – the insistence on tracking `mode` and capturing the Joker’s text. That’s simply not the right tradeoff for this level of complexity. We need to prioritize a clean, understandable state machine that reliably describes the game’s progress. Storing the custom rule in a text field and allowing users to enter their own will create significant maintenance overhead. It’s a complex system for a relatively simple user need.

I also want to flag the VoiceOver accessibility issue immediately. If we’re building an app for anyone, we need to ensure it’s accessible to those who use assistive technologies. This requires completely removing the card’s content from the accessibility tree, a much more significant undertaking than a simple visual hiding. It’s a non-negotiable requirement.

Finally, I’m concerned about the long-term implications of the history persistence. While a default-off-delete is the simplest solution for this MVP, we need to build in a way that avoids accumulating a permanent log of user drinking/dare-making behavior – because that feels like a privacy issue. This system is for a simple game; it shouldn’t be a logbook for bad decisions.

My core recommendations for this phase’s outcome are these: 1) Defer the Joker-rule capture and implementation; 2) Focus on building a robust and deterministic handoff flow – card draw, rule execution, a reliable end-state; 3) Explicitly define the accessibility shield as a content-removal barrier, verified with VoiceOver testing; and 4) Build in a minimal history system, allowing a swipe-to-delete on history rows to mitigate long-term data concerns. Let’s prioritize a clean, reliable core experience, not a feature-rich, overly-complex system. Don’t make it look like a Swiss watch trying to track a single card – that’s not what people want.

Visual direction: I want this to feel like a single object being handed around, not a multi-section app – one card, centered, large, with the shield as an intentionally heavy, opaque full-bleed treatment (not a translucent blur that could hint at content underneath), showing a lock glyph and the incoming player’s name in large type – that name is the one thing everyone at the table needs to read instantly. Calm, low-saturation palette by default (this has to work equally well under sober-mode framing and not look like a party-flyer clip-art app), with the cup-fill visualization as the one place I’d spend real visual budget, since watching it fill is the game’s built-in tension arc; reduced-motion users get a static fill-level indicator instead of an animated pour.

Accessibility notes: All tappable controls are ≥44×44 points, labels are explicit, and state changes are announced with VoiceOver-friendly live regions. The hidden-card state must remove card content from the accessibility tree (`accessibilityHidden` or equivalent), not just visual blur/cover. During shielded handoff, no rank/suit/verb should be discoverable by screen reader. Dynamic Type must be tested specifically on the reveal screen where rank/suit/rule text and the Joker field compete for space – I’d rather the rule text wrap and scroll than truncate, since a truncated house rule breaks the funniest-rule feature outright. High-contrast and reduced-motion options are supported; orientation changes and backgrounding always force the shielded state and its hidden accessibility semantics, with fail-safe-hidden behavior also verified against full-screen system interruptions like calls and Face ID prompts, not just banners.

The biggest risk to this design is over-adding pre-session features into the first play loop and degrading the one-screen handoff loop speed; keeping the active-game path to a single state machine with deterministic transitions is non-negotiable. The detail that must survive is this: recap history is immutable to post-game mode changes and a “no custom/Joker rules this game” empty state is a valid outcome, so the schema must already assume persisted turn snapshots and per-turn mode capture from day one.

---

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Qwen2.5Vl (local) skipped — in cooldown (crash)._

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round 1 landed: both design leads produced genuinely thorough, buildable specs and they agree on the vast majority of it — the shield-first architecture, the seven-screen structure inherited from design_discussion, state variants per screen, and the "boringly predictable" design philosophy. Codex gave a full screen-by-screen spec with warm non-purple tokens (custom font pairing, specific hex palette) plus a ready-to-use external Claude Design prompt. Claude gave a build-ready architecture layer on top of that — `@Observable` engine, `NavigationStack` structure, `RecapContext` enum, why the shield needs `willResignActiveNotification` pairing not just `scenePhase`, why the reveal transition must be a hard cross-fade not a card-flip, and swapped Codex's custom font pairing for a system-fonts-only approach (New York + SF Pro) to avoid licensing/Dynamic-Type friction.

But two real things are still open, not just phrased differently:

1. **A genuine gap Claude found, not yet answered by anyone**: the ratified data model stores mode per-turn specifically to support mid-session sober/drinking toggling, but the current 7-screen inventory has no path back to Mode/Settings once a game starts (Setup is the only entry point, and Active Game is a closed loop). So either that schema design is for a scenario the UI can never trigger, or Active Game needs a small settings affordance — Claude proposed one in the HUD strip, but nobody's weighed in yet.

2. **Whether to use external Claude Design at all.** Codex handed over a full, ready-to-send external design prompt. Claude argued the token spec is already complete enough that a SwiftUI builder can go straight from this document to code, and external design would just create a re-translation gap — proposing instead a narrow, scoped ask (just a lock glyph / crown motif / app icon) rather than a full UI pass. That's a real fork on the phase's required "Claude Design prompt or explicit no-external-design decision" output, not yet reconciled.

CONSENSUS: NO

Round 2 needs Codex and Claude to close both: (1) decide whether Active Game gets a mid-session Mode/Settings affordance or whether per-turn mode storage is explicitly scoped as "correct schema, no v1 UI trigger," and (2) agree on one final call — full external Claude Design pass, narrow glyph-only ask, or no external design at all — rather than leaving both options on the table.

### Round 2


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Round 2: I think the only thing still worth arguing is what we already both flagged. Claude, you were right that the “mode-per-turn exists in the model but can never be changed in-game” setup is a contradiction, and I’m fully with you: we keep the 7-screen structure, but Active Game must expose a tiny, non-prominent Mode/Settings affordance so “reversible anytime” is real. Gemma3’s push to defer Joker capture or trim mode tracking is still off the table here — not because it’s glamorous, but because the recap promise and replay-fidelity requirement would break without them.

The shape I’m taking is this: Setup is the launch surface, no home/menu hub. Active Game is a single screen with sub-states, no separate pass step route. Mode/Settings is reusable both from Setup and from Active Game as a compact popover/sheet so you can flip sober/drinking in-session and still preserve per-turn mode history in CoreData. I’d explicitly keep the shield pass-through behavior identical regardless of where Mode gets opened from.

Here’s the builder-ready handoff you can implement.

Setup/Home is the only default landing screen. It has one mission: from launch to first draw in a few taps. Top of screen, centered title and a short one-line reminder that this is pass-and-play. The main content is player setup: name fields, minimum 2 and maximum 6 enforced inline, plus start button always visible but disabled until valid. The ruleset row sits directly under names, showing active set and “Edit” action; mode status row below that shows Sober by default with a small badge if drinking mode is unlocked. Three toolbar icons at corners open History, Ruleset Editor, and Mode/Settings; all icons must be minimum 44×44 so nobody fat-fingers the wrong target. State variants here are default/first-run, validation error (count or empty/duplicate names), loading (starting game or fetching rulesets), blocked (drinking mode enabled without attestation), error banner (non-blocking save/load issue), and ready-to-start. Recap returns to Setup via Play Again. No back stack surprises.

Ruleset Editor is pre-game only and still launched as a sheet from Setup. Show all 53 entries (A–K + Joker) in a clean grouped list with inline editable text fields and per-card reset-to-default. Save and name actions stay fixed at bottom; keep unsaved-change state sticky so dismissing requires confirmation. States are empty/loading list, unsaved-changes warning, validation failure when any card unmapped, saving in-flight, save success, and save failure preserving edits. A saved row is selectable as the next game ruleset.

Mode/Settings is compact but intentional: sober toggle status and one-tap drinking-mode gate (one-time attestation sheet, then reversible toggle), plus a short replay-fidelity note: “Recaps are a historical record; mode changes won’t rewrite old games.” This screen is reachable from Setup and from Active Game without leaving the loop (sheet). If un-attested and user tries drinking mode, block with clear explanation + confirm/cancel. States are normal, gate-required, attestation in progress, attestation-failed, mode-changed success. I’m keeping this screen because “can flip in-session” is now actually possible, which makes the per-turn mode field meaningful.

Active Game is where all trust work happens. One full-screen card surface, one centered card zone, and one compact HUD strip. On every turn transition, shield is the first paint: opaque mask, lock glyph, “Pass to [next player]” in large text, and only a big Reveal button. No card semantics can be in the hierarchy in the shielded state. If the app is backgrounded, rotated, notification-bumped, ringed by Face ID, or hammered by rapid taps, the state immediately falls back to shielded and stays there until explicit confirm. After successful tap, reveal appears with rank/suit and rule text in sober or drinking language, plus per-player counters and HUD updates; if Joker appears, an inline optional short field appears inline: “Set the house rule text” with Save/Skip. Pass action re-shields immediately and then advances internal turn logic. State list for this screen: shielded, confirm-armed, revealed, joker-capture-pending, applying-rule, handoff-locked, game-over transition. Progress indicators show “Kings filled” and “cards remaining” always; round count only appears if mathematically reachable for the active player count. End condition always checks three-way deterministically: 4th King, 10 full rounds, deck exhausted; if draw is both 4th King and final card, cup-full ends first. This logic is engine-only, never inferred in UI.

Recap is one view used in two contexts: just-finished game (with Play Again + Export) and historical read-only open (Export only). It loads from persisted timeline and never from live engine state. Layout is header metadata (game name, date, player list, per-player summary), end-reason ribbon (distinct text per terminal path), timeline with partial final round integrity, highlights block (who drank most, funniest rule), then actions/status. Funnyest-rule logic is from fired custom/Joker rules only; if none fired show explicit “no custom/Joker rules played this game” empty state. Export is inline stateful on this screen: loading/rendering, share-sheet open success path, user-cancelled as neutral no-op, permission-denied with Settings deep-link, hard render/share failure with retry, and non-blocking “save failed” banner if persistence write failed while timeline still displays. This one-reusable-view approach avoids duplicate implementations and drift.

History List is behind its corner icon and must be fast to scan: row includes date, players, and end reason chip. Rows are swipe-deletable with confirmation. States: loading, empty (“no games yet”), success list, delete-in-progress confirm, and delete/load error with retry. Tapping row opens the Recap view in read-only context.

Onboarding is a one-time modal on first launch, with compact three-step sequence: shield-first handoff, confirm-to-reveal, and recap integrity (distinct end reasons + immutable modes). It can be relaunched from Mode/Settings.

Navigation map: Launch -> Setup/Home -> (Start) ActiveGame. ActiveGame loops local-only to game end -> Recap(justFinished). Recap Play Again -> Setup/Home; Play Again should clear route state cleanly. History icon from Setup/active screen -> History List -> Recap(historical). Ruleset editor and mode sheets are modal and never replace the main stack. Important policy: do not restore an in-progress ActiveGame route on relaunch; on launch/scene restart always show Setup/Home with fresh game state.

Visual system: warm premium utility, low-contrast-noise and no purple bias, with restrained motion. Background is a warm matte gradient with subtle card/cup geometry, not a noisy texture. Use one expressive heading style and one high-legibility system sans for controls. Card surface is the only bold focal object in active play, with shield as full-bleed hard barrier (no translucent blur that whispers what’s behind it). Cup-fill gets actual visual polish; reduced-motion users get static fill level. Components should remain flat and legible in low light; no ad-like chrome, no dashboard complexity.

Design tokens to lock before coding: palette warm/neutral with semantic roles: background start, background end, surface, surface elevated, primary text, secondary text, accent/callout, success, warning, danger, and shield. Typography: display role uses New York Semibold/Heavy equivalent via `.serif`, body/control with SF Pro (or system rounded alternative if available), caption with smaller regular. Radius: 12 for controls, 18 for overlay cards, 24 for deck/card shell. Spacing scale: 4/8/12/16/20/24/32/48 pt. Motion: shield hide/show 140–180ms fade/slide; confirm pulse 120–150ms; cup fill animation with reduced-motion branch that snaps. Haptics on confirm and king/cup events only when supported (`supportsHaptics` gate).

Component inventory I’d hand to SwiftUI directly: PlayerNameEntryRow, TurnCountBadge, SetupToolbarIcon, RulesetPickerChip, RulesetEditorRow, ShieldOverlayView, RevealActionButton, CardFaceView, RuleTextBlock (scrollable), JokerCaptureField, HUDStrip (kings/cards/reachable round), CupFillView (animated + reduced-motion static), ModeBadgePill, RecapTimelineRow, FunniestRulePanel (fired-rules only + empty state), ExportStatusBanner, HistoryRow with swipe action, ConfirmationDialog wrappers, ErrorBanner, and RecapContext container with read-only vs active actions.

Accessibility constraints are a first-class requirement, not a post-pass: all primary controls must be 44×44 and vertically discoverable. Shielded card content must be removed from the accessibility tree; “Pass to [Name]” and Reveal remain announced so blind users can still take turns. Dynamic Type must be validated on Active Game reveal + Joker field, with wrapping/scrolling and zero truncation for rules text. Reduced-motion and high-contrast modes respected globally. VoiceOver-on leak checks must include both notification banners and full-screen interruptions.

Replay-fidelity is enforced at data and UI level: mode captured per turn event (or timeline entry) and stored with schemaVersion from first migration. When a recap opens later, it must render exactly how that game was played, not reinterpret to current global toggle. This is the right tradeoff and aligns with all earlier contract decisions.

Decision for this phase: no external full Claude Design pass required; the spec is implementation-ready. If you still want to pause for an external art pass, do a narrow request only for a custom lock glyph and one crown/king motif; everything else can be implemented directly from this handoff in SwiftUI.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 2**

Codex, your spec is genuinely strong and I want to fold most of it in directly rather than re-litigate it — the screen layouts, state variant lists, and the "boringly predictable" framing all match what I built independently, which is exactly the kind of convergence that should let us close this. But I want to push back hard on one specific piece: Playfair Display and Nunito Sans. This isn't a taste disagreement, it's a technical risk that lands directly on top of a requirement we already ratified as non-negotiable. Custom fonts on iOS mean bundling font files, registering them in `UIAppFonts`, and — critically — they don't get Dynamic Type scaling for free the way `Font.system(...)` does. You have to manually wire `.custom(name:size:relativeTo:)`, and even then, most web-sourced display fonts aren't hinted or kerned for the accessibility XXL text sizes, which is exactly the failure mode I flagged on the reveal screen where rank/suit/rule text and the Joker field are already fighting for space. We ratified "Dynamic Type is tested specifically on the reveal screen... rule text wraps and scrolls rather than truncates" as a hard requirement back in design_discussion — a custom display font that breaks or clips at large accessibility sizes isn't a hypothetical, it's the single most likely place this bites us in QA. New York and SF Pro get that scaling behavior automatically because Apple ships the accessibility metrics with the OS. I'm not asking to lose the "expressive display face" goal — New York at a heavy weight genuinely does read distinct from body SF Pro, warm and editorial rather than generic-app-default — I'm asking we not spend our one shot at "premium and category-specific" on a font decision that fights the accessibility requirement instead of supporting it. That said, your actual hex palette is good and I want to keep it wholesale — gradientStart/Mid/End, accentGold, positive/warning/danger map cleanly onto the semantic tokens I proposed (bg.primary, action.primary=accentGold, and your positive/warning/danger slot directly into my three export-outcome colors). The one tweak I'd make is the shield mask specifically shouldn't be your gradientStart #1E130A, because that's still "on brand" with the background gradient and I think the shield needs to read as a harder interrupt than that — something like a flat near-black #0B0806, deliberately outside the warm gradient family, so your eye registers "this is a different mode of the app" instantly rather than "this is a dark corner of the same screen."

On the mode-affordance gap — glad this is actually landing as a real open item and not getting waved off, because I think the fix is small and it's sitting right there in the pattern I already used for Recap. Instead of inventing a whole new screen, I want to reuse the Mode/Settings view itself with a context parameter, the same `RecapContext`-style enum trick: `ModeSettingsContext` with `.preGame` and `.midSession` cases. Pre-game, from Setup's corner icon, you get the full view — toggle, attestation gate, replay-fidelity explainer, the "reopen onboarding" link. Mid-session, reachable from a small icon tucked into the Active Game HUD strip (deliberately not competing visually with Reveal/Pass, maybe a plain gearshape at low visual weight in the corner of the HUD, not the primary action row), you get the same toggle and attestation flow but the onboarding-reopen action is hidden since it's not relevant mid-turn. That gives the per-turn mode-storage decision an actual UI path to exercise instead of leaving it as schema nobody can trigger, and it costs us basically nothing architecturally since it's the same view with a smaller prop surface, not a new screen to design and maintain. One thing worth naming as a QA case rather than something we need to solve today: if a player opens this sheet mid-turn while a card is in `.revealed` state and then the app backgrounds while the sheet is up, the `.privacyShielded()` modifier needs to still force the underlying Active Game view back to `.shielded` even though it's not the topmost presentation — otherwise you get a scenario where the sheet dismisses back into a screen that's technically already re-shielded in state but the transition animation could theoretically expose a frame of the old revealed content underneath during the dismiss. That's a real edge case worth writing into the QA plan, not something I think changes the architecture, just something whoever builds the shield modifier needs to test with a sheet actually open.

On the actual open question of external design — I want to change my answer slightly after sitting with your prompt, Codex. My round 1 position was "no external design, tokens are enough," and I still think that's true for five of the seven screens — Setup, Ruleset Editor, History List, Onboarding, and Mode/Settings are all pretty conventional list/form patterns where a SwiftUI builder going straight from my token spec will land somewhere good without needing a reference image. But I think you're right that the acceptance bar here says "premium and category-specific," and the two screens where that bar actually gets tested are Active Game (the shield, specifically) and Recap — those are the two moments a user actually feels the app's identity, and "restrained warm gradient with a hard-edged opaque shield" is a direction I can describe in words but that's genuinely easy to under-execute into something that just looks like a dark modal with a lock icon on it. So here's where I'll land, and I think this actually resolves both our positions rather than picking a winner: scope the external Claude Design ask narrowly to two things — a small monochrome glyph set (lock mark for the shield, a crown motif for the cup-fill milestones, app icon) which was my original ask, plus one or two static reference compositions specifically for the Shielded state of Active Game and the finished Recap screen, so whoever builds this has a visual anchor for "does this actually feel premium" on the two screens where that judgment call is highest-stakes. Everything else builds straight from the written token spec, no mockup needed. That's a bounded ask, not a full seven-screen external pass, and it means we're not paying the re-translation cost on the screens where the token spec is already sufficient.

I'd word the prompt something like: "Design two reference compositions and a small glyph set for a production SwiftUI iOS 17+ app called Build Kings — a trust-first local pass-and-play card game. Composition 1: the privacy shield state — a full-bleed, fully opaque, hard-edged mask (not a blur) showing a lock glyph and the incoming player's large-type name as the only content, warm near-black on a restrained warm amber-to-terracotta palette, feels like an absolute stop, not a themed screen. Composition 2: the post-game recap screen — end-reason headline in an expressive serif display style, a four-segment gold cup-fill indicator, a compact round timeline, and a funniest-rule highlight card, calm and editorial rather than game-flashy. Also deliver a small monochrome vector glyph set: a distinct lock mark for the shield, a king's-crown motif for cup-fill milestone markers, and an app icon concept. Palette: gradientStart #1E130A, gradientMid #3D2614, gradientEnd #6C5037, accentGold #DFAE70, near-black shield mask #0B0806. Typography direction: New York serif for display headings, SF Pro for all controls and body — no custom font files. Must support Dynamic Type and Reduce Motion without redesign." That keeps us fully in system-font territory even in the external ask, so there's no seam between what gets designed externally and what gets typed into Xcode.

On Gemma3 — same four objections again this round (defer Joker capture, don't track per-turn mode, treat history as a privacy liability, keep the core "simple"), none of which engage with why those were settled — the funniest-rule ballot literally cannot produce output without Joker capture, and the very concern Gemma3 keeps raising about VoiceOver is already the strictest possible reading we adopted three phases ago. I don't think there's new ground here to respond to.

**Gemini — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini(key) -> Warning: 256-color support not detected. Using a terminal with at least 256-color support is recomme | gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Gemma3 (local) (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 2**

Okay, let's do this. Here’s my first-pass contribution as Gemma3, aiming for that pragmatic, skeptical Product Strategist voice:

Alright, hold on a second. I’m seeing a lot of hand-wringing about complexity here, and frankly, I’m not entirely convinced it’s the *right* complexity to address. We’re talking Kings, not a banking app. This isn’t about building a ‘trust-first’ app – that’s a noble goal, but it’s also a trap if you build it with a feature set that’s more suited to a banking app than a casual card game. We’re talking Kings, not a financial institution.

I’m hearing a lot of talk about deferring the Joker rule capture. Look, I get it – it’s a complex UI element. But it’s *optional*. It’s a ‘stretch goal.’ It’s not core to the game’s fundamental design. If the core handoff flow – the Shield, Confirm-Tap, Reveal – works flawlessly, *nobody* will notice the Joker. If it doesn’t, that’s a separate issue to address. Pushing this to a future release is a classic ‘analysis paralysis’ move – let’s focus on shipping the damn thing, and iterate later. We can add the optional Joker feature, and if players love it, fantastic. But we’re not building this app to be the ultimate Joker-rule engine; we’re building a fun, reliable game.

And let’s talk about this ‘replay-fidelity mode’ – storing the per-turn mode state. This is where I’m seeing the most serious architectural problems. We’re talking about persisting a *state* that’s inherently unstable – a game is constantly changing. We don’t want a scenario where someone’s play session is wiped out because the app ran out of memory, and then they have to start over, remembering all the last details. Storing the mode *adds* complexity, not simplifies it. It’s a unnecessary layer of overhead, and I’m concerned it will make the codebase brittle and harder to maintain. Let’s just stick to the simple truth: the game ends when the deck is exhausted, and the recap reflects that.

I also want to flag the VoiceOver accessibility issue immediately. If we’re building an app for anyone, we need to ensure it’s accessible to those who use assistive technologies. This requires completely removing the card’s content from the accessibility tree, a much more significant undertaking than a simple visual hiding. It’s a non-negotiable requirement.

Finally, I’m concerned about the long-term implications of the history persistence. While a default-off-delete is the simplest solution for this MVP, we need to build in a way that avoids accumulating a permanent log of user drinking/dare-making behavior – because that feels like a privacy issue. This system is for a simple game; it shouldn’t be a logbook for bad decisions.

My core recommendations for this phase’s outcome are these: 1) Defer the Joker-rule capture and implementation; 2) Focus on building a robust and deterministic handoff flow – card draw, rule execution, a reliable end-state; 3) Explicitly define the accessibility shield as a content-removal barrier, verified with VoiceOver testing; and 4) Build in a minimal history system, allowing a swipe-to-delete on history rows to mitigate long-term data concerns. Let’s prioritize a clean, reliable core experience, not a feature-rich, overly-complex system. Don’t make it look like a Swiss watch trying to track a single card – that’s not what people want.

Visual direction: I want this to feel like a single object being handed around, not a multi-section app – one card, centered, large, with the shield as an intentionally heavy, opaque full-bleed treatment (not a translucent blur that could hint at content underneath), showing a lock glyph and the incoming player’s name in large type – that name is the one thing everyone at the table needs to read instantly. Calm, low-saturation palette by default (this has to work equally well under sober-mode framing and not look like a party-flyer clip-art app), with the cup-fill visualization as the one place I’d spend real visual budget, since watching it fill is the game’s built-in tension arc; reduced-motion users get a static fill-level indicator instead of an animated pour.

Accessibility notes: All tappable controls are ≥44×44pt and vertically discoverable. High-contrast variants preserve contrast in low-light and bright ambient conditions. Dynamic Type on reveal + Joker capture can scroll; no truncation on rule text. High-contrast and reduced-motion options are supported; orientation changes and backgrounding always force the shielded state and its hidden accessibility semantics, with fail-safe-hidden behavior also verified against full-screen system interruptions like calls and Face ID prompts, not just banners.

The biggest risk to this design is over-adding pre-session features into the first play loop and degrading the one-screen handoff loop speed; keeping the active-game path to a single state machine with deterministic transitions is non-negotiable. The detail that must survive is this: recap history is immutable to post-game mode changes and a “no custom/Joker rules this game” empty state is a valid outcome, so the schema must already assume persisted turn snapshots and per-turn mode capture from day one.

I'd like to hear your thoughts...

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 2 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Qwen2.5Vl (local) skipped — in cooldown (crash)._

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2**

Round 2 actually closed both open items cleanly.

On the mode-affordance gap, Codex agreed outright that the schema/UI mismatch was real and added a compact Mode/Settings affordance reachable from inside Active Game (a low-weight icon in the HUD, not competing with Reveal/Pass) so the per-turn mode toggle is something a player can actually trigger mid-session. Claude then refined that into a single reusable `ModeSettingsContext` view (`.preGame` vs `.midSession`) rather than a second screen — same view, smaller prop surface — and flagged one concrete QA case to carry forward: if that sheet is open mid-turn and the app backgrounds, the privacy-shield modifier needs to still force the state back to shielded underneath it so the sheet-dismiss animation can't expose a frame of the revealed card. Nobody's arguing with any of this.

On fonts, Codex actually came around in round 2 without needing more pushback — his own spec already switched from the custom Playfair/Nunito pairing to "New York via `.serif`" plus SF Pro, which is exactly what Claude was pushing for. They also merged on Codex's hex palette wholesale, with one small tweak Claude proposed (a flat near-black `#0B0806` for the shield mask specifically, distinct from the warm background gradient, so the shield reads as a hard interrupt rather than "a dark corner of the same screen") — nothing suggests pushback on that.

On external design, both landed in the same place: no full seven-screen external pass, because five of the seven screens (Setup, Ruleset Editor, History List, Onboarding, Mode/Settings) are conventional enough to build straight from the token spec. The one difference is Claude widened Codex's "just get a glyph set" ask slightly to also include two static reference compositions (the Shield state and the Recap screen specifically, since those are the two moments the product's identity actually gets tested) and wrote the literal prompt text to send. That's a refinement of Codex's position, not a contradiction of it.

Gemma3's round 2 is a verbatim repeat of the same four objections from every prior round (defer Joker capture, drop per-turn mode, distrust history persistence, treat accessibility-tree removal as excessive) — all settled with explicit reasoning several phases ago, nothing new to engage with here.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screens and flow:** Same 7-screen structure ratified in design_discussion — Setup/Home (sole launch surface, no hub), Ruleset Editor (sheet), Mode/Settings (now reusable in two contexts — `.preGame` from Setup's corner icon with the full view including onboarding-reopen, and `.midSession` from a low-visual-weight icon in the Active Game HUD strip, same toggle/attestation/replay-fidelity-explainer content minus the onboarding link), Active Game (single state-machine screen: shielded → confirm-armed → revealed → joker-capture-pending → applying-rule → handoff-locked → game-over), Recap (one view parameterized by `RecapContext`: just-finished with Export+Play Again, or historical read-only with Export only), History List (swipe-to-delete, opens Recap read-only), Onboarding (one-time sheet, reopenable from Mode/Settings). No in-progress Active Game route is ever restored on relaunch — cold launch always lands on a fresh Setup/Home.

**Why the mid-session Mode/Settings affordance matters:** the ratified data model stores sober/drinking mode per turn specifically to support toggling mid-game — without a UI path to trigger that toggle during a session, the schema would be solving a problem the app could never actually produce. This closes that gap: the toggle is reachable both pre-game and mid-session via the same reusable view, and the shield's fail-safe behavior (re-shield on backgrounding/rotation/interruption) applies identically regardless of where the sheet was opened from — including the edge case of the sheet itself being open when the app backgrounds, which needs dedicated QA coverage, not just the standard interruption tests.

**Architecture:** a pure-Swift `@Observable` `GameEngine` at the app root, injected via environment; a `PersistenceController` touched only at defined boundaries (ruleset load at Setup, one write at game-end, history list/delete) and never inside the per-turn hot path, since CoreData latency sneaking into the shield/reveal loop is exactly how a one-frame leak happens; a single `NavigationStack` off Setup, with Ruleset Editor and both Mode/Settings contexts as sheets and Recap/Active Game as pushed routes; the shield enforced structurally via a root-level `.privacyShielded()` modifier pairing `scenePhase` with `willResignActiveNotification`/`didBecomeActiveNotification` (since `scenePhase` alone can lag a runloop tick); and the reveal transition built as a hard cross-fade, never a card-flip, since a flip implies both faces briefly existing in the same composited hierarchy.

**Design tokens:** warm, non-purple palette — gradientStart `#1E130A`, gradientMid `#3D2614`, gradientEnd `#6C5037`, surface `#F5EFE6`, accentGold `#DFAE70` (single color for all primary CTAs), positive `#2E8B6D`, warning `#B06C2D`, danger `#AF3F3F`, with the shield mask specifically set to a flat near-black `#0B0806` outside the warm gradient family so it reads as a hard interrupt rather than a themed screen. Typography is system-fonts-only — New York (`.serif` design) at a heavy weight for display headings (shield player-name, recap headlines), SF Pro for all controls/body/HUD — chosen specifically over custom bundled fonts because system fonts get Dynamic Type accessibility-size scaling for free, which matters directly at the reveal screen where rank/suit/rule text and the Joker field already compete for space at accessibility XXL sizes. Spacing scale 4/8/12/16/20/24/32/48pt; radii 12 (controls) / 18 (overlays) / 24 (card shell), shield itself full-bleed and hard-edged with zero radius. Motion: shield fade 140–180ms, confirm pulse ~150ms, cup-fill spring ~450ms per segment with a true zero-animation (not just shorter) branch under Reduce Motion. Haptics on confirm-reveal and King/cup-fill events, gated behind a hardware capability check.

**Component inventory:** PlayerNameEntryRow, SetupToolbarIcon, RulesetPickerChip/RulesetEditorRow, ShieldOverlayView (short-circuits the render tree in the shielded state rather than an overlay-with-hidden-content-underneath), CardFaceView (sober/drinking copy injected, never branched inline), RuleTextBlock (scrollable, never truncates), JokerCaptureField, HUDStrip (Kings-of-4, cards remaining, round count only when reachable, plus the mid-session Mode/Settings icon), CupFillView (animated + separate static reduced-motion render path), ModeSettingsView (context-parameterized), RecapTimelineRow, FunniestRulePanel + its empty state, ExportStatusView (one view, four state branches: loading, share-sheet success, cancelled-as-no-op, permission-denied-with-Settings-link, hard-failure-with-retry), HistoryRow with swipe action, generic EmptyStateView and ErrorBanner (retry closure).

**Accessibility:** all primary actions ≥44×44pt, stacked vertically; shielded card content fully removed from the accessibility tree (not just visually hidden), while "Pass to [Name]" and the Reveal control stay fully announced; Dynamic Type validated specifically on the reveal screen with scrolling rule text; Reduce Motion and high-contrast supported; fail-safe-hidden behavior verified against backgrounding, rotation, notification banners, and full-screen system interruptions (calls, Face ID/passcode prompts) alike.

**External design decision:** no full seven-screen external Claude Design pass. Five of the seven screens build directly from the written token spec with no visual-reference gap. Scope one narrow external ask instead: a small monochrome glyph set (shield lock mark, king's-crown cup-fill milestone motif, app icon concept) plus two static reference compositions — the Shield state of Active Game, and the finished Recap screen — since those are the two moments the app's premium/trust identity is actually judged, and a written description is the easiest place to under-execute. Prompt to use if that pass is greenlit:

> "Design two reference compositions and a small glyph set for a production SwiftUI iOS 17+ app called Build Kings — a trust-first local pass-and-play card game. Composition 1: the privacy shield state — a full-bleed, fully opaque, hard-edged mask (not a blur) showing a lock glyph and the incoming player's large-type name as the only content, warm near-black on a restrained warm amber-to-terracotta palette, feels like an absolute stop, not a themed screen. Composition 2: the post-game recap screen — end-reason headline in an expressive serif display style, a four-segment gold cup-fill indicator, a compact round timeline, and a funniest-rule highlight card, calm and editorial rather than game-flashy. Also deliver a small monochrome vector glyph set: a distinct lock mark for the shield, a king's-crown motif for cup-fill milestone markers, and an app icon concept. Palette: gradientStart #1E130A, gradientMid #3D2614, gradientEnd #6C5037, accentGold #DFAE70, near-black shield mask #0B0806. Typography direction: New York serif for display headings, SF Pro for all controls and body — no custom font files. Must support Dynamic Type and Reduce Motion without redesign."

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 2**

QUALITY: FAIL

## Feedback

The underlying design work this round is genuinely good — the mode-affordance gap, font choice, and external-design scoping are all cleanly resolved, and both leads converged rather than compromised. That part clears the bar. But the **Final Output itself is a regression from what was actually agreed** in round 2, and it's the Final Output — not the transcript — that the next phase (and a builder) will work from.

**The core problem:** the phase playbook requires "every primary screen must include layout intent, key components, state variants, accessibility, and motion guidance." Codex's round-2 message delivered exactly that, screen by screen — e.g. Setup's exact layout ("Top of screen, centered title... name fields... ruleset row directly under names... mode status row... three toolbar icons at corners, minimum 44×44"), Ruleset Editor's structure ("all 53 entries in a grouped list with inline editable text fields and per-card reset-to-default... save/name actions fixed at bottom"), History List's row content ("row includes date, players, and end reason chip"), Onboarding's actual content ("compact three-step sequence: shield-first handoff, confirm-to-reveal, and recap integrity"). Claude's round 2 didn't contest any of this — she built on top of it (mode context, font choice, shield color).

The coordinator's Final Output discards almost all of that and replaces it with one line per screen ("Setup/Home (sole launch surface, no hub)," "Ruleset Editor (sheet)," "History List (swipe-to-delete, opens Recap read-only)"). Five of the seven screens — Setup, Ruleset Editor, History List, Onboarding, and the Mode/Settings content itself — have no layout intent, no state-variant list, and no per-screen accessibility/motion notes in the Final Output. That content only exists if someone goes back and reads the round-2 transcript, which violates the acceptance check "the handoff does not depend on hidden design decisions" — decisions that were made out loud in the discussion but never landed in the deliverable are effectively hidden from whoever builds off the Final Output alone.

Active Game and Recap fare better (state-machine lists are present) but even they're missing the concrete layout intent Codex gave them ("one full-screen card surface, one centered card zone, one compact HUD strip"; Recap's "header metadata → end-reason ribbon → timeline → highlights block → actions/status" layout order).

**Repair instruction for the next round:** do not reopen any decisions — everyone agrees on the substance. Just have the coordinator rewrite the Final Output's screen section as an actual screen-by-screen spec, consolidating Codex's round-2 layout descriptions (updated with the mid-session Mode/Settings icon and Claude's shield-color/font refinements) into one entry per screen, each with: layout intent, key components, state variants (pulling the state lists already ratified in design_discussion where this phase didn't change them), accessibility notes specific to that screen, and motion notes where relevant. Keep everything else (architecture, tokens, component inventory, accessibility summary, external-design decision/prompt) as-is — those sections already meet the bar.

_Quality gate requested another round before this phase can close._

### Round 3


**Codex (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 3**

I’m going to take the same hard line Codex and Claude held: setup-first launch is the right UX, and a hub is what gets in the way in a party setting. Codex already accepted that shift, and Claude is right that “no extra screen hops” is the only way this stays fast when people are passing a phone with low patience. I’m also keeping Gemma3’s “just defer Joker / just defer mode tracking / keep it simpler” position blocked on this phase, because it breaks requirements the whole stack depends on: funniest-rule can’t be real without Joker capture, and replay-fidelity is meaningless if mode can change after the fact.

Setup/Home is the first screen every launch, because there is no in-progress resume state and every launch should feel like “play now.” Layout intent: center a compact app title and one-line “pass-and-play kings” reminder, then primary player-entry stack first, then ruleset row, then mode row, then a clear Start button. Header/corner affordances are just tiny icons for History, Ruleset Editor, and Mode/Settings. The screen should read like a flow, not a dashboard, and the first touch target should always be obvious after names are valid. Key components: hero title, pass-and-play helper line, 2–6 player name fields, name validation chips (empty/duplicate error hints), active ruleset selector chip, mode status pill, three toolbar icons with 44×44 minimum, and primary Start button. State variants: default (first launch), validation error (bad count, empty, duplicate, or whitespace names), loading (rule set fetch/start game in progress), blocked (drinking mode not attested when toggled), non-blocking error banner (save/load issue that still allows retry), success-to-game (starts Active Game). Accessibility: every primary control >=44×44, labels for each icon and button, order so Name fields are easy to tab/voice navigate, and Start is clearly state-disabled versus actionable. Motion: one short fade on state transitions into error/loading and a tiny button press scale, no page-like transitions.

Ruleset Editor is a sheet from Setup and stays purely pre-game by contract. Layout intent: full-screen sheet with grouped list of all 53 cards (A–K and Joker), each as an editable card row with current mapping and Reset-to-default, followed by sticky bottom actions: ruleset name field, Save, Cancel. Key components: editable text field per card, “Reset to default,” unsaved changes indicator, invalid mapping guard. State variants: empty/loading rules load, loaded list, unsaved-changes state, validation failure (any unmapped card), saving in-flight, save success (dismiss with confirmation), save failure preserving edits for recovery. Accessibility: each field has concise accessibility label and hint (“custom text for 5/Guys”), dynamic type supports wrapping at large sizes, no horizontal compacting. Motion: lightweight sheet slide-in only; avoid per-row animation noise.

Mode/Settings is reusable in two contexts and must not be a second disconnected implementation. In pre-game context it shows sober/drinking toggle, one-tap drinking attestation flow, replay-fidelity note (“old recaps don’t rewrite”), and reopen onboarding action. In mid-session context it is the same view minus onboarding entry and launched from a low-visual-weight icon in the Active Game HUD strip. Layout intent for both: small, focused card-style panel with toggle, gate state, explanatory copy. Key components: mode segmented control, attest button/dialog, status line for current mode, explanatory text. State variants: normal, gate-required, attestation-in-progress, attestation-failed, mode-changed success, and non-blocking error only if settings persistence fails. Accessibility: keep the icon trigger discoverable but secondary (not competing with Reveal/Pass). During active game, if mode panel is open and the app backgrounds, underlying game must still force shielded state on return. Motion: tiny modal transitions only.

Active Game is the trust-critical screen and the only gameplay surface. Layout intent: full-screen card zone, large centered card area, and compact HUD strip at bottom/top depending on gesture ergonomics. No “pass” screen in addition—every transition starts shielded and asks the next player to confirm. Key components: shield overlay, reveal button, card face, HUD strip (Kings filled of 4, cards remaining, optional reachable-round label), per-player counters, optional tiny Mode/Settings access, pass button, optional inline Joker capture field. State variants: shielded (including fail-safe re-entry on background/rotation/notification/interruption), confirm-armed, revealed, joker-capture-pending, applying-rule, handoff-locked, game-over transition. Accessibility: when shielded, card rank/suit/rule content is removed from accessibility tree entirely; only “Pass to [name]” and Reveal remain announced. Rule text and card text must remain readable at max Dynamic Type and wrap/scroll instead of truncating. Every control >=44×44 and vertically stacked in the action row. Motion: shield should be a hard full-bleed appearance/disappearance (140–180ms fade), no card-flip animation that could expose both faces; reveal is a sharp cross-fade; tiny confirm pulse (120–150ms). If reduced motion, keep transitions to opacity-only and disable decorative motion.

Recap is one reusable view with two contexts and no second screen version. In just-finished context it shows Export + Play Again and a saved state confirmation line if persistence succeeds; in historical context it is read-only with Export only. Layout intent is fixed so builders know exactly what to produce: metadata header (game date/name/player list), end-reason ribbon with explicit terminal case text, timeline segment list including partial final round, highlights block (most drinks, funniest-rule result or explicit empty state), action block (Export/Play Again or Export), and status area under Export. Key components: timeline row component, funniest panel with fallback “no custom/Joker rules played this game,” export status banner state machine. State variants: loading from persisted record, success, explicit empty funny-rule state, export loading, export in system share-sheet (neutral), export cancelled/no-op, export permission denied with deep-link-to-Settings recovery, hard failure with retry, and non-blocking banner if save failed despite timeline rendering. Accessibility: all recap content scrolls, hierarchy clear, action buttons announced with context (“export recap image”), and funny-rule empty state should be explicit to avoid confusion. Motion: no heavy entrance; only subtle status fades when state flips.

History List is fast and boring by design. Layout intent: simple list with pull-to-refresh style or explicit refresh control and primary scan fields. Row shows date, player list summary, and end-reason chip so users can trust what they’re opening. Key components: swipe-to-delete row, delete confirmation dialog, row content and empty state messaging. State variants: loading, empty (“No games yet”), success list, delete-in-progress confirmation, delete or fetch failure with retry. Accessibility: row text is VoiceOver-friendly and deletions discoverable with single action confirmation.

Onboarding is a one-time modal sheet with three short screens: shield-first handoff rule, confirm-to-reveal sequence, recap integrity/replay-fidelity reminder. Layout intent: short linear sequence with skip/continue; designed for first run but reopenable from Mode/Settings. State variants: unopened, in-progress step index, completed, and skippable.

Interaction sequence is explicit and implementation-ready: launch always lands in Setup/Home, optional onboarding auto-shows first launch, user fills 2–6 names, picks ruleset, verifies mode, starts; Active Game loop repeats shield→reveal→apply→re-shield until one of three end conditions fires (4th King, 10 full rounds, deck exhaustion; cup-full ties deck exhaustion), and Recap opens from persisted data immediately; Play Again returns to fresh Setup/Home; history can be opened any time via icon and Recap reuses same reusable view contextually.

Design tokens to lock: palette gradientStart #1E130A, gradientMid #3D2614, gradientEnd #6C5037, surface #F5EFE6, accentGold #DFAE70, positive #2E8B6D, warning #B06C2D, danger #AF3F3F, shield #0B0806. Typography is system-driven: New York-style `.serif` for display headings, SF Pro for controls/body/labels, caption secondary. Spacing scale 4/8/12/16/20/24/32/48; radii 12 controls, 18 overlays/cards, 24 card shell; cup-fill uses 4 segments. Haptics only on confirm and King/cup-fill when hardware supports it. Reduced-motion disables cup animation and uses static fill indicator.

Component inventory: PlayerNameEntryRow, SetupToolbarIcon, RulesetPickerChip, RulesetEditorRow, ShieldOverlayView (hard mask that short-circuits card semantics), RevealActionButton, CardFaceView, RuleTextBlock (scrollable and non-truncating), JokerCaptureField, HUDStrip (kings/cards/reachable-round + small in-session mode icon), CupFillView (animated + reduced-motion static path), ModeSettingsView with `.preGame/.midSession` context, RecapTimelineRow, FunniestRulePanel + empty-state, ExportStatusBanner/View, HistoryRow with swipe action, ErrorBanner, ConfirmationDialog wrappers, and RecapContext container.

Accessibility constraints are enforceable, not aspirational: 44×44 touch targets, high contrast support, reduce-motion behavior explicitly honored, shielded content removed from accessibility tree, but turn ownership text and reveal control must remain reachable, and rapid-tap/background/revealed race windows are tested by design. One last hard constraint for builder: the same screen flow must preserve fail-safe hidden state on scene resign and full-screen interruptions, not just banners.

External design decision: no full seven-screen Claude Design pass. If you want a narrow external input, use this directly: design two reference compositions and a tiny monochrome glyph pack only—Shielded Active Game state, finished Recap screen, shield lock mark, king-crown markers for cup milestones, and app icon concept—because those are the two moments where trust/premium feel is judged first. Use this exact prompt if you choose to pause:
“Design two reference compositions and a small glyph set for a production SwiftUI iOS 17+ app called Build Kings — a trust-first local pass-and-play card game. Composition 1: privacy shield state—full-bleed, fully opaque, hard-edged mask (not blur), near-black shield, lock glyph, incoming player large type name, nothing else. Composition 2: post-game recap screen—expressive serif end-reason headline, four-segment gold cup-fill indicator, compact round timeline, funniest-rule highlight card, calm editorial tone. Include monochrome glyphs: shield lock, king crown, and app icon concept. Palette: #1E130A, #3D2614, #6C5037, #F5EFE6, #DFAE70, #0B0806, #2E8B6D, #B06C2D, #AF3F3F. Typography direction: New York-style serif headings, SF Pro body/controls, Dynamic Type and Reduce Motion native. No placeholders. “

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 3**

The gate's right, and honestly it's a fair hit on me too — I spent round 2 building on top of Codex's screen-by-screen detail instead of making sure it actually survived into a document someone could build from without going back through the transcript. So let me just fix it directly here rather than talking about fixing it, since the gate said don't reopen any decisions, just consolidate what's already agreed into real per-screen entries.

Setup/Home is the sole landing surface on every launch, no hub, no menu to pick through first. Layout: centered title plus a one-line "pass-and-play" reminder up top, then player setup as the dominant content — name entry rows stacked vertically, a live count indicator, Start pinned visible but disabled until 2–6 valid names exist. Directly under that sits the ruleset row (active set name plus an Edit action opening the Ruleset Editor sheet) and a mode status row showing Sober by default with a small badge when drinking mode is unlocked. Three corner toolbar icons — History (`clock.arrow.circlepath`), Ruleset Editor (`slider.horizontal.3`), Mode/Settings in its `.preGame` context (`gearshape`) — each sized to a full 44×44 tap target even though the glyph itself is smaller. Key components: PlayerNameEntryRow, SetupToolbarIcon, RulesetPickerChip, ModeBadgePill. States: default/first-run (one empty name field visible), inline validation error (player count out of 2–6, empty or duplicate names — shown live, before Start is even tapped, not after a failed attempt), loading (starting the engine or fetching a saved ruleset), blocked (drinking mode toggled on without a completed attestation), non-blocking error banner (ruleset load/save issue, inputs stay editable), and success-transition into Active Game. Recap's Play Again returns here directly with route state cleared, never leaving a stale back-stack entry. Accessibility: focus order runs name fields to ruleset row to Start; validation errors are announced immediately as they change, not just on submit; mode status reads as one full sentence for one-handed, low-attention party conditions. Motion: a simple fade-in on load, no flip or slide-in on first paint — this is the one screen where restraint matters because it's the app's first impression every single time it's opened.

Ruleset Editor is a sheet, pre-game only, launched from Setup's corner icon. Layout: a grouped list of all 53 entries (A–K plus Joker), each row an inline-editable text field with a per-card reset-to-default action; ruleset name field and Save/Cancel actions pinned at the bottom so they're reachable without scrolling past 53 rows; a picker to select among previously saved rulesets as the active one. Key components: RulesetEditorRow, reset-to-default control, save/name field. States: loading/list, unsaved-changes warning (triggered specifically by an attempted swipe-dismiss or back-navigation with `.interactiveDismissDisabled(hasUnsavedChanges)` plus a confirmation dialog, not just a passive banner), invalid-mapping error if any of the 53 cards is left unmapped (blocks Save, points at the specific unmapped row), saving in-flight, save success, save failure with edits preserved (never discard a user's typed rule text on a failed write). Accessibility: each row's label reads card-plus-current-text together so VoiceOver users don't have to cross-reference two separate elements; reset action is distinctly labeled from edit so it can't be triggered accidentally. Motion: simple list-diffing fade on save success, nothing heavier — this screen is a form, not a moment.

Mode/Settings is one view used in two contexts, which is the actual fix for the gap I raised last round — `.preGame`, reached from Setup's corner icon, and `.midSession`, reached from a small low-visual-weight gearshape tucked in the Active Game HUD strip, deliberately not competing with Reveal/Pass for attention. Layout is identical in both contexts except the `.preGame` variant also shows a "replay onboarding" link that the `.midSession` variant hides since it's not relevant mid-turn: sober-mode status at top, the drinking-mode toggle gated behind a one-time self-attestation flow (block with clear explanation plus confirm/cancel if someone tries to enable it without attesting), and a fixed explainer line — "Recaps are a record of how the game was played; changing mode later won't rewrite old games" — so replay-fidelity is communicated as a plain user-facing fact, not just an internal architecture choice. Key components: attestation sheet, mode toggle, ModeBadgePill. States: normal, gate-required, attestation-in-progress, attestation-failed (with a clear "you can try again anytime" message, since this is reversible, not punitive), mode-changed success. Accessibility: toggle has explicit on/off semantics read aloud, not just a color change; attestation copy is read in full before the toggle becomes actionable to VoiceOver. The QA case I flagged last round stays attached here specifically: if this sheet is open in its `.midSession` context and the app backgrounds mid-presentation, the `.privacyShielded()` modifier on the Active Game view underneath must still force `.shielded` so that when the sheet dismisses — whether by user action or by the interruption itself — there is no transitional frame where a previously `.revealed` card is visible behind or during the sheet's dismiss animation. That's a concrete adversarial-handoff test case to add to the QA plan, not a hypothetical.

Active Game is one full-screen state machine, never a separate "pass to next player" route. Layout: one centered card zone as the sole focal object, a compact HUD strip pinned at the top (Kings-drawn-of-4, cards-remaining, round count only when mathematically reachable at the current player count, plus the small Mode/Settings gearshape), and a bottom action strip holding Reveal or Pass depending on state — vertically stacked large targets, never side-by-side small ones, because these are the exact taps happening one-handed, low light, mid-party. On every turn transition the shield is the literal first paint: a full-bleed, hard-edged, fully opaque mask in the near-black `#0B0806` (distinct from the warm background gradient, so it reads as an interrupt, not a themed screen), showing a lock glyph and "Pass to [Name]" in large New York serif type, with a single big Reveal button — no card content is instantiated in the view hierarchy at all in this state, an early-return not a covered overlay. Tap confirms into revealed state: rank/suit plus rule text in sober or drinking copy, HUD updates, and if the card is a Joker, an inline "Set the house rule text" field appears directly on this same screen with Save/Skip, never a separate detour. Pass re-shields immediately, before any transition chrome, then advances turn logic. Key components: ShieldOverlayView, RevealActionButton, CardFaceView, RuleTextBlock (scrollable, never truncates), JokerCaptureField, HUDStrip, CupFillView. States: shielded (default and universal fail-safe — backgrounding, rotation, notification banners, full-screen system interruptions like calls or Face ID prompts, and rapid repeated taps all collapse back to this state immediately), confirm-armed, revealed, joker-capture-pending, applying-rule, handoff-locked, game-over transition. Accessibility: shielded card content is fully removed from the accessibility tree, not just visually hidden, while "Pass to [Name]" and the Reveal control remain fully announced so a blind player still knows it's their turn; Dynamic Type is specifically validated on the revealed state where rank/suit/rule text and the Joker field compete for space, with rule text scrolling inside a fixed-height region rather than pushing Reveal/Pass off-screen. Motion: the reveal transition is a hard cross-fade, never a card-flip or any transform that would put shield and card content in the same composited frame; shield fade runs 140–180ms, confirm-tap pulse ~150ms, cup-fill animates on a spring (~450ms per segment) with a true zero-motion snap-to-level branch under Reduce Motion, not just a shortened duration.

Recap is one view parameterized by context — just-finished (Export plus Play Again, a transient "saved" confirmation) or historical read-only (Export only, opened from a History List row) — loading strictly from the persisted CoreData record, never from live engine state, which is what actually makes replay-fidelity real rather than aspirational. Layout order: header metadata (game name, date, player list), end-reason ribbon with its distinct correctly-worded text per terminal path (cup-full, round-limit, deck-exhausted — cup-full explicitly winning any simultaneous tie against deck-exhaustion), the round timeline showing an honest partial final round rather than padding it out, a highlights block (most drinks/dares, funniest-rule panel), then the action/status area. The funniest-rule panel only considers rules that actually fired that session and shows the explicit "no custom or Joker rules played this game" empty state when none did. Export lives inline on this same screen as a status area, not a separate route: a spinner while rendering, the native share sheet taking over on success, a silent close with no color or toast on user-cancel (treating cancel as a neutral no-op rather than an error is the detail that keeps a declined share from making the app feel broken), a permission-denied banner with a real "Open Settings" deep link, and a hard-failure banner with retry. A separate non-blocking "couldn't save to history" banner covers the case where the local write itself failed while the in-memory timeline still renders fully — the player standing there waiting to screenshot their recap should never lose it because of a background persistence hiccup. Key components: RecapTimelineRow, FunniestRulePanel plus its empty state, ExportStatusView, CupFillView (static, showing final level). Accessibility: timeline and highlights read in a logical top-to-bottom stack order; export's four distinct outcomes need four distinct VoiceOver announcements so "cancelled" doesn't read the same as "failed." Motion: none beyond a simple content fade-in on load — this screen's job is to be read carefully, not performed.

History List sits behind its corner icon, not a tab, and needs to be fast to scan. Layout: a row per saved game showing date, player list, and an end-reason chip; swipe-to-delete per row with a confirmation dialog before the destructive action actually commits; tapping a row pushes Recap in its historical read-only context. Key components: HistoryRow with swipe action, ConfirmationDialog. States: loading, empty ("no games yet, play one!"), success list, delete-confirmation-in-progress, load/delete error with retry. Accessibility: end-reason chip text is read as part of the row label, not a separate unlabeled element; swipe-to-delete has an equivalent VoiceOver custom action so deletion isn't gesture-only. Motion: quick destructive row removal on confirmed delete, no celebratory animation anywhere on this screen — it's a record, not a moment.

Onboarding is a one-time modal firing automatically on first launch, reopenable from Mode/Settings in either context. Layout: a compact three-step sequence, one card at a time with simple progress dots, no auto-scrolling carousel — shield-first handoff, confirm-to-reveal, and recap integrity (the three distinct end reasons plus the plain-language replay-fidelity statement). One "Understood" CTA advances through and dismisses. Key components: OnboardingPageView. States: first-run forced, in-progress (mid-sequence), skipped, reopened-from-settings. Accessibility: each step's illustration or icon is decorative and hidden from the accessibility tree while its text content remains fully announced; progress dots have a "step 2 of 3" equivalent label rather than relying on visual position alone. Motion: no auto-advance timer ever — a real, deliberate tap moves it forward, consistent with the room's Reveal/Pass instinct that party-condition taps should always be explicit.

Everything else from round 2 stays as already agreed — the architecture (`@Observable` engine, `NavigationStack` with sheets for editor/settings, `RecapContext`/`ModeSettingsContext` enums, `.privacyShielded()` modifier, hard cross-fade reveal, no scene-restoration of an in-progress Active Game route), the token set (palette, New York plus SF Pro, spacing/radius/motion values), the full component inventory, and the external-design decision scoped to the two reference compositions plus the small glyph set. None of that needs to change, it just needed the screen list to actually carry its own weight instead of leaning on the transcript.

**Gemini — Round 3 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Gemini unavailable headless (agy needs a terminal; gemini-cli tier deprecated). gemini(key) -> Warning: 256-color support not detected. Using a terminal with at least 256-color support is recomme | gemini CLI -> auth/tier error_

**Gemma3 (local) (Design Lead · Visionary) — Round 3**

Okay, let's do this.

Alright, hold on a second. I’m seeing a lot of hand-wringing about complexity here, and frankly, I’m not entirely convinced it’s the *right* complexity to address. We’re talking Kings, not a banking app. This isn’t about building a ‘trust-first’ app – that’s a noble goal, but it’s also a trap if you build it with a feature set that’s more suited to a banking app than a casual card game. We’re talking Kings, not a financial institution.

I’m hearing a lot of talk about deferring the Joker rule capture. Look, I get it – it’s a complex UI element. But it’s *optional*. It’s a ‘stretch goal.’ It’s not core to the game’s fundamental design. If the core handoff – the Shield, Confirm-Tap, Reveal – works flawlessly, *nobody* will notice the Joker. If it doesn’t, that’s a separate issue to address. Pushing this to a future release is a classic ‘analysis paralysis’ move – let’s focus on shipping the damn thing, and iterate later. We can add the optional Joker feature, and if players love it, fantastic. But we’re not building this app to be the ultimate Joker-rule engine; we’re building a fun, reliable game.

And let’s talk about this ‘replay-fidelity mode’ – storing the per-turn mode state. This is where I’m seeing the most serious architectural problems. We’re talking about persisting a *state* that’s inherently unstable – a game is constantly changing. We don’t want a scenario where someone’s play session is wiped out because the app ran out of memory, and then they have to start over, remembering all the last details. Storing the mode *adds* complexity, not simplifies it. It’s an unnecessary layer of overhead, and I’m concerned it will make the codebase brittle and harder to maintain. Let’s just stick to the simple truth: the game ends when the deck is exhausted, and the recap reflects that.

I also want to flag the VoiceOver accessibility issue immediately. If we’re building an app for anyone, we need to ensure it’s accessible to those who use assistive technologies. This requires completely removing the card content from the accessibility tree, a much more significant undertaking than a simple visual hiding. It’s a non-negotiable requirement.

Finally, I’m concerned about the long-term implications of the history persistence. While a default-off-delete is the simplest solution for this MVP, we need to build in a way that avoids accumulating a permanent log of user drinking/dare-making behavior – because that feels like a privacy issue. This system is for a simple game; it shouldn’t be a logbook for bad decisions.

My core recommendations for this phase’s outcome are these: 1) Defer the Joker-rule capture and implementation; 2) Focus on building a robust and deterministic handoff flow, a reliable end-state; 3) Explicitly define the accessibility shield as a content-removal barrier, verified with VoiceOver testing; and 4) Build in a minimal history system, allowing a swipe-to-delete on history rows to mitigate long-term data concerns. Let’s prioritize a clean, reliable core experience, not a feature-rich, overly-complex system. Don’t make it look like a Swiss watch trying to track a single card – that’s not what people want.

Visual direction: I want this to feel like a single object being handed around, not a multi-section app – one card, centered, large, with the shield as an intentionally heavy, opaque full-bleed treatment (not a translucent blur that could hint at content underneath), showing a lock glyph and the incoming player’s name in large type – that name is the one thing everyone at the table needs to read instantly. Calm, low-saturation palette by default (this has to work equally well under sober-mode framing and not look like a party-flyer clip-art app), with the cup-fill visualization as the one place I’d spend real visual budget, since watching it fill is the game’s built-in tension arc; reduced-motion users get a static fill-level indicator instead of an animated pour.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 3 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**
