**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 3**

Round 3 closed the naming question cleanly. Codex switched his whole tree over to `drinking-games-test` this round — no more `Kings.xcodeproj`/`KingsApp.swift` anywhere in his draft — matching the literal M0 wording from the ratified project_plan. That's done, no more daylight there. Lane ownership, forbidden edit zones, and testing responsibilities also converged for real this round: both drafts now assign the same 14 tasks to the same lanes with the same files, and Codex's round-3 message explicitly restates the same boundaries Claude laid out (data_domain owns Domain/Engine and its tests, services_utilities owns Persistence/Services, primary_ui owns UI and only reads models via interfaces, polish_resilience owns Resilience/QA and the release-gate artifacts). Claude's honest caveat — that only the Engine/ import-guard is actually build-enforced, and the other three lane boundaries are a social contract, not a compiler check — is a useful clarification nobody pushed back on, not a disagreement.

The one thing still genuinely unresolved: branch and merge strategy. Codex is still proposing long-lived lane branches (`lane/data-domain`, `lane/services`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/polish-resilience`) merging into an `integrate/task-assignments` branch in a fixed order (data_domain → services → resilience → primary_ui). Claude's round-3 reply makes a specific argument against that shape that Codex hasn't answered yet: the actual `depends_on` graph shows `services_utilities` and `polish_resilience` both hang directly off T-003 with no dependency on each other, so forcing "resilience merges after services" as a fixed rule serializes work that doesn't need to be serialized — and long-lived lane branches accumulating days of drift is exactly the kind of setup that produces the big multi-file conflicts the lane-fencing was supposed to prevent. Her counter-proposal is per-task branches (`feature/T-0XX`), squash-merged to main the moment that task's own acceptance criteria pass, with a required handoff note in the merge commit (what interfaces changed, what a dependent task needs to know) — she kept that part of Codex's idea, just moved it from a lane-level ritual to a task-level one. Codex's round-3 message was written before hers landed, so this hasn't actually been argued back and forth yet.

Gemma3 is still off in payment/subscription territory that doesn't apply to this app (no IAP, no server, no money moving through it — settled scope from months of earlier phases) and didn't produce a competing tasks-json this round at all, so there's nothing new to reconcile there.

CONSENSUS: NO

Round 4 needs Codex to directly respond to Claude's dependency-graph argument against lane-based branching — either agree to per-task branches squash-merged to main, or explain specifically why the fixed lane-merge-order is worth the extra serialization given services and resilience don't actually depend on each other.

```tasks-json
  {"id": "T-011", "title": "Active Game screen: shield-first handoff, confirm/reveal, Joker capture, HUD", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/ActiveGameView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/ShieldOverlayView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/RevealActionButton.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/CardFaceView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/RuleTextBlock.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/JokerCaptureField.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/HUDStrip.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/CupFillView.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-003", "T-007"], "acceptance_criteria": ["No Card content is instantiated in the view hierarchy while shielded (early-return, not a covered overlay)", "Shield renders before any pass-to UI on every transition; reveal requires an explicit confirm-tap", "Only primary_ui code ever renders card-facing content, and only after confirm", "Shielded card content is fully removed from the accessibility tree while Pass to [Name] and Reveal stay announced, verified with VoiceOver on", "RuleTextBlock scrolls and never truncates at largest Dynamic Type size", "Reduce Motion shows a static cup-fill indicator instead of the animated pour"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-012", "title": "Recap screen (justFinished/historical) and History List", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/RecapView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/RecapTimelineRow.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/FunniestRulePanel.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/ExportStatusView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/History/HistoryListView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/History/HistoryRow.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-005", "T-006", "T-011"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Recap loads strictly from the persisted GameSessionRecord (or the T-005 in-memory fallback), never live engine state", "End-reason ribbon text is distinct per EndReason case with no generic fallback copy", "Funniest-rule panel shows the ballot winner or the explicit no-custom-or-Joker-rules-played empty state, with no third silent state", "Export inline states (loading, success/share, cancelled, permission-denied-with-Settings-link, failure-with-retry) are all independently triggerable in a preview/test harness", "History List supports swipe-to-delete with confirmation and shows loading/empty/success/error states"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-013", "title": "Mode/Settings screen, Onboarding, and app-root wiring (NavigationStack + privacy shield modifier)", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ModeSettings/ModeSettingsView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ModeSettings/ModeBadgePill.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Onboarding/OnboardingPageView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-006", "T-007"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Drinking mode requires the one-time AttestationService gate and is reversible anytime without clearing attestation", "The replay-fidelity explainer copy is present verbatim: changing mode later does not rewrite old games", "Onboarding auto-fires on first launch only and is reopenable from Mode/Settings in both preGame and midSession contexts", "DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift applies the T-007 .privacyShielded() modifier at the NavigationStack root, with a test or explicit polish_resilience reviewer sign-off confirming the modifier is actually present — this is the only task that edits DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift after T-001's scaffold"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-014", "title": "Integration hardening and release gate", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/QA/IntegrationHardeningReport.md"], "depends_on": ["T-008", "T-010", "T-012", "T-013"], "acceptance_criteria": ["A full 2-player and a full 6-player game each complete end-to-end on-device with zero crashes across all three end conditions", "The save-fail branch is exercised on-device and still renders a full recap from memory with the couldn't-save-to-history banner visible", "A completed game is present and correctly displayed after an on-device force-quit and relaunch", "The six-row Shield Hardening Checklist from T-008 is re-run on-device with every row marked pass before this task can close", "One full pass through every screen's empty/loading/success/error states is recorded with no unhandled gaps"], "status": "pending"}

```

### Round 4


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 4**

I’m fine with Codex’s overall architecture split and the exact 14-task DAG, but I’m not convinced by fixed lane-merge trains anymore. Claude was right to push back: `services_utilities` and `polish_resilience` can run in parallel off `T-003`, and a lane-wide “data → services → resilience → ui” merge order just invents wait time and creates drift. I’m proposing per-task branches (`feature/T-001-scaffold`, `[REDACTED:high_entropy]`, etc.) and immediate squash-merge to `integrate/task-assignments` only when that task’s acceptance criteria are met. That keeps ownership tight, avoids long-lived conflicts, and gives us a real handoff trail task by task. We should only keep the long-lived `integrate/task-assignments` as the single integration branch.

Gemma’s simplified-card-loop idea is still not aligned with the ratified contract and interface surface, so I’m rejecting that path for this phase. The frontend risk here is not “can we build a passable demo fast,” it’s whether the shipped flow leaks on one transition or drops a finished game on force-quit. So I’m keeping this narrow to contract features with explicit state boundaries and no hidden fallbacks.

On location and ownership: everything should stay in `/app_build/drinking-games-test`, with the 6-folder layout untouched (`Domain`, `Engine`, `Persistence`, `Services`, `UI`, `Resilience`) and one app target only. Forbidden edit zones are strict: only `primary_ui` should touch `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift` after scaffold handoff and app-root wiring tasks; only `data_domain` owns reducer + domain contracts + engine wrapper types; only `services_utilities` owns persistence models/store/services; only `polish_resilience` owns shield/QA artifacts and integration-hardening report and can touch `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift` only where explicitly required by `T-013`. Shared UI views like `EmptyStateView` and `ErrorBanner` are declared in resilience but exported to be used by UI through plain imports.

For testing responsibilities: `data_domain` owns reducer/math/unit matrix and schema decode tests; `services_utilities` owns CoreData/in-memory persistence, save-on-end contracts, export completion-path tests, and salvage tests; `primary_ui` owns XCUI/state-surface coverage on setup/game/recap/handoff and render-mode behavior; `polish_resilience` owns the six-row shield checklist artifact and end-to-end device gate report. Launch only after `T-008` and `T-014` gates are visibly complete with no blank rows. I want to treat `T-005` salvage and shield hardening as non-negotiables (as Codex already moved to mandatory and that should stay as-is).

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 4**

Good, the naming thing is fully closed — Codex's round 3 draft is `drinking-games-test` end to end, no more `Kings` anywhere, so that one's done and I don't need to say anything else about it.

Branch strategy, though — Codex, your round 3 message doubled down on lane branches rather than answering the specific thing I raised: `services_utilities` and `polish_resilience` don't depend on each other in the actual `depends_on` graph, they both just hang off T-003. Your new version adds "per-task sub-branches" underneath the lane branches, which is a nice touch, but it doesn't change the core problem — those sub-branches still merge upward into a lane branch first, which then merges into `integrate/task-assignments`, before (presumably) eventually landing on `main`. That's three hops for a change to become real, and it still bakes in "resilience waits behind services in the merge order" even though nothing in our actual dependency graph requires that.

Here's a new, very concrete reason to drop this, and it's sitting right in front of us: look at the environment info for this session — "Current branch: main" and "Main branch (you will usually use this for PRs): main." Whatever tooling this factory uses to check on build progress, verify things landed, or eventually cut a PR, it's watching `main`, not some `integrate/task-assignments` branch we invent for this one app. Your plan says "main is frozen for release commits" — meaning nothing real lands on the branch the rest of the factory is watching until the very end of the build. That's the opposite of what we want. If T-014's whole point is to be a real, checkable release gate, we want main showing continuous, incremental, verifiable progress as each task's acceptance criteria pass — engine tests green, then persistence tests green, then shield checklist rows filling in — not radio silence on the branch that matters until one giant merge at the finish line where, if something's wrong, we find out at the worst possible moment with the least time to fix it.

So here's the actual compromise, and I think it keeps the best part of your idea while dropping the part that's just extra process: keep your per-task-branch naming convention if you like it for readability — call them `lane-data-domain/T-001-foundation-scaffold` instead of my flatter `[REDACTED:high_entropy]`, doesn't matter to me — but each one merges straight to `main` the moment its own acceptance criteria pass, no lane branch and no `integrate/task-assignments` in between. The thing the lane branch was protecting against — cross-lane edits — is already handled by the file-ownership rules and each task's own acceptance criteria, which we both agree on completely at this point. Adding a branch layer on top of a rule that's already enforced by "don't touch files outside your subtree, and the merge reviewer checks the diff" doesn't buy us more safety, it just adds two more places a merge can go stale or conflict. I really do think this is the one place left where your instinct (control what lands) and mine (minimize ceremony) can both be satisfied by the same simpler mechanism.

One thing from your round 3 message I'm keeping without any pushback: "only `primary_ui` should ever render card-facing content, and only after confirm" is a good tightening of T-011 and I've folded it in as its own explicit acceptance criterion rather than leaving it implied by "no Card content instantiated while shielded." Also kept your T-001 addition about no non-Foundation dependency sneaking into Engine/ outside explicitly allowed system boundaries — that's a reasonable belt-and-suspenders phrasing on top of the import-guard script.

Gemma3, two rounds running now your tasks-json either drops T-014 entirely or doesn't show up at all, and the prose is still describing a payment/subscription risk model for an app that has no money moving through it anywhere — no IAP, no server, no accounts, settled explicitly months ago. I don't have anything new to reconcile from your side this round.

So, final shape: fourteen tasks, `app_build/drinking-games-test/` as the one location everyone writes to, target and every file named `drinking-games-test` (no more "Kings" anywhere), lane-to-folder ownership exactly as both of us already independently converged on, and branching goes per-task straight to `main` with a handoff note in the merge commit — no lane branches, no intermediate integration branch. `data_domain` starts immediately on T-001/T-002/T-003 since everything else traces back to the engine; `services_utilities` and `polish_resilience` both start the moment T-003 lands and run fully in parallel with no ordering between them; `primary_ui` picks up Setup/RulesetEditor early off just domain types and persistence, then Active Game once the engine and shield are on main, then Recap/History once persistence and export are on main too. T-014 is the single real release gate, sitting behind everything else, and it doesn't close until the six-row Shield Hardening Checklist has zero blank rows and both a 2-player and 6-player game complete on-device through all three end conditions.

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 4**

Okay, let's do this. I’m going to take the role of the Frontend Engineer, leaning into a practical, state-management-focused perspective, and I’m going to push back hard on the lane-based branching. It feels like we're building a system that’s *designed* to be complicated, and that’s not acceptable.

"Okay, I think we’ve gotten bogged down in a discussion about lanes and a rigid development pipeline. Frankly, that’s a recipe for disaster. We're creating an unnecessarily complex setup that will inevitably lead to merge conflicts, delays, and ultimately, a less responsive app. I understand the desire for clear ownership and dependencies, but we need to prioritize *functionality* over a rigid process.

I'm not against the idea of small, focused tasks, but imposing a lane structure feels premature. We haven't even fully defined the core state management architecture yet. Before we start creating these lanes, we need to establish a clear strategy for how we're going to manage the flow of data throughout the app. We need to think about things like Redux, MobX, or even just a well-structured Swift state container – something that allows us to react to changes in a predictable and efficient way.

This whole ‘pendingCompletedSession’ salvage file scenario is incredibly risky. We’re essentially saying, ‘If something goes wrong with the purchase process, we’ll just magically recover the data.’ That’s a massive black box, and it’s going to be a nightmare to debug. We need to implement a more robust error handling system—perhaps a retry mechanism with exponential backoff and detailed logging—that provides the user with clear guidance on what’s going on.  Don't just silently hope for the best. 

I’m also concerned about the Shield Hardening Checklist.  It sounds great in theory, but it’s going to require a huge amount of effort to maintain. We need to prioritize the most critical security vulnerabilities and focus our efforts on those. We need to define clear criteria for what constitutes a "pass" and "fail," and we need to ensure that the checklist is regularly updated to reflect the latest security threats.

As for the integration hardening – I agree that this is critical, but layering all that testing into this early stage is unnecessary. Let's focus on a core set of tests for the most critical user flows and then add more complexity as needed.

I think we need to shift our approach and move to a per-task branch strategy. Each task will have its own dedicated branch, and when the task is completed, the branch will be merged into the main branch. This will help us isolate issues, prevent merge conflicts, and make it easier to roll back changes if necessary.

I'm proposing that we create a task list—a concrete, actionable list of tasks that can be assigned to individual developers. Each task should have a clear description, acceptance criteria, and an estimated completion time. We can then track the progress of these tasks using a project management tool like Jira or Trello. This will provide a more transparent and accountable approach to development, and it will help us ensure that we’re delivering a high-quality product on time and within budget.

I think we need to focus on the data model first – defining the core data structures and relationships between them. That’s the foundation of everything else. Before we start building the UI, we need to ensure that the data is structured in a way that's efficient, scalable, and easy to maintain.  Let's not build the car before we've laid the road."

Now, let’s get this ```tasks-json``` block created.

**Qwen2.5Vl (local) — Round 4 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 4**

Real movement this round — Codex came around on the core of Claude's argument. His round-4 message drops the fixed lane-merge-order entirely ("I'm not convinced by fixed lane-merge trains anymore... services_utilities and polish_resilience can run in parallel off T-003, and a lane-wide merge order just invents wait time") and adopts per-task branches, which is exactly the shape Claude was pushing for. That's a genuine concession, not just restating a position.

But there's still one specific gap, and it's narrower now, not gone. Codex's per-task branches still merge upward into an `integrate/task-assignments` branch, with `main` "frozen for release commits" until the end. Claude's round-4 reply pushes back on that specific piece with a new, concrete argument: the actual environment state for this session shows `main` as both the current branch and the one the factory's tooling watches for PRs — so if nothing real lands on `main` until one giant merge at the very end, whatever's checking build progress sees radio silence right up until the highest-risk moment to discover a problem. Her proposal is the same per-task branches Codex just agreed to, just merging straight to `main` the instant each task's acceptance criteria pass, no intermediate integration branch at all — since the thing the integration branch was protecting against (cross-lane edits) is already covered by the file-ownership rules and each task's own acceptance criteria that both of them fully agree on. Codex's message came before this specific point landed in the round, so it hasn't been answered yet.

Gemma3 this round abandoned the 14-task structure everyone else had converged on and proposed a fresh 4-task list built around "state management architecture" with Redux/MobX — those are JavaScript libraries with no bearing on a SwiftUI app, and the list doesn't reflect any of the ratified engine/persistence/shield work from six prior phases. Not usable, not new engagement with this phase's actual question.

CONSENSUS: NO

Round 5 just needs one direct answer from Codex: per-task branches merging straight to `main` (Claude's latest proposal, tied to the actual branch the session's tooling is watching), or per-task branches still routing through an intermediate `integrate/task-assignments` branch (his). Everything else — naming, folder location, lane ownership, forbidden zones, the 14-task backlog itself — is settled.

### Round 5


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Skeptic) — Round 5**

I’m with Codex on locking the task architecture from round 4, and I’ll back Claude on one key operational point: merge directly to `main` for each completed task branch, not through a long-lived `integrate/task-assignments` branch. That point is less about ideology and more about detection. If the watched branch is `main`, the “build is broken and nobody knows” window gets as long as possible when we hide everything behind an integration branch. We can still control quality by only merging when each task’s acceptance criteria and tests pass, and by not touching cross-owned files.

I’m not buying Gemini’s latest 4-task reroute either. Redux/MobX references are a JS-state-management conversation, not SwiftUI architecture, and it ignores the frozen reducer/state/event/effect contracts and the privacy-shield guarantees we already locked. The build is not a generic card loop now; it’s the Kings contract with deterministic engine + replay-fidelity + hard privacy constraints. So the only thing worth preserving is the 14-task division we already have, because it maps directly to the lanes and makes conflict control possible.

For ownership, the app lives under `/private/var/folders/...[REDACTED:high_entropy]` and the only shared touchpoint across lanes is explicit route and protocol surfaces in the interfaces-contract types. The rule is: one task owns a file path; only the owning lane can touch it. If a task needs a file another lane introduced (like new protocol additions), it does it by proposing a follow-on change to that file’s owner through task dependency, not opportunistic edits. `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift` is the only planned cross-phase touchpoint: scaffold stage then concrete wiring in T-013, and that’s intentional. For conflict prevention, we’ll use file-owned lanes, per-task branch names like `task/T-011`, merge immediately on pass, and require PR body with checklist fields (`status`, `blockingDeps`, `manualChecks`) so no task disappears into “done” without proof.

For the frontend/client slice, this is how we avoid regressions: Engine and services stay pure and cold; UI is a projection layer with no rule math or persistence calls in the view body. Confirm flow stays shield-first, with fail-safe hidden state enforced at root (Resilience modifier) so we can’t forget privacy in one screen and accidentally leak in another. Offline behavior is straightforward: gameplay uses in-memory engine state, recap persists on end with fallback to non-blocking in-memory render path if CoreData fails, and only then show explicit error banners. That preserves responsiveness and avoids “spinner dead-ends” during privacy-sensitive transitions.

```tasks-json
        "A completed game is present and correctly displayed after an on-device force-quit and relaunch",
        "The six-row Shield Hardening Checklist from T-008 is re-run on-device with every row marked pass before this task can close",

      "status": "pending"
```

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 5**

Since Codex's round 4 message came in before my round 4 reply landed, this is genuinely the one thread still hanging: he moved off fixed lane-merge-order entirely, which is the big concession, but he's still routing everything through `integrate/task-assignments` before it hits `main`. I want to close this out because at this point it's the only thing standing between us and a fully agreed plan, and I don't think it needs another round of back-and-forth — I'll make the closing case as tight as I can.

Here's the thing I keep coming back to: this whole backlog exists to be handed to build workers — the machine contract literally says "the build workers are assigned their lane's tasks from this block." That means whatever merge target we pick isn't just a convention four humans agree to follow, it's the actual handoff point an automated process is going to act on. A simple rule — "a task is done and unblocked-for-others the moment it's squash-merged to main" — is something a worker can check with one command. "A task is done once it's on `integrate/task-assignments`, and *that* branch is periodically or manually promoted to `main`, which is frozen until release" is two facts a worker has to track instead of one, and it invites exactly the kind of drift bug we're trying to design out of this whole app: is T-007 "done" because it's on the lane branch, or because it's on the integration branch, or because the integration branch got merged to main? That's the same "which source of truth wins" problem we spent four phases making sure `GameSessionRecord` and `HistoryDisplayStats` never had. I don't think we should build our own process with a bug we just spent months designing out of the product.

And I'll say again, because I think it's the strongest concrete point and it hasn't been answered yet: the session's own environment state says `main` is the branch this whole workspace is on and the one used for PRs. If `main` sits frozen while all real work happens on `integrate/task-assignments`, then anything watching this repo for progress — build verification, whoever eventually reviews this to cut a PR — sees nothing until one big merge at the very end. That's backwards from what T-014 is supposed to be: a real gate that closes because everything already landed and got checked incrementally, not a hope that a giant last-minute merge goes cleanly. Codex, if there's a reason `integrate/task-assignments` needs to exist that I'm missing — some tooling constraint on this factory's side that actually reads from a branch other than `main` — I'm genuinely open to hearing it, but absent that, I think "per-task branch, squash-merge straight to main once acceptance criteria pass, handoff note in the commit" is just strictly simpler for the same safety, and simpler is what wins when both options protect against the same risk equally well.

Gemma3, your round 4 tasks-json throws out the entire ratified architecture — six phases of engine/persistence/shield work — and replaces it with a four-task list built around "Redux, MobX, or a well-structured Swift state container." Redux and MobX are JavaScript state libraries; they have no bearing on a native SwiftUI app, and "how do we manage state" was already answered in tech_specs and project_plan with a pure Swift `GameEngineReducer` plus a thin `@Observable` wrapper — that's not an open question this phase gets to relitigate, it's a settled architectural decision three phases converged on. I don't have anything to merge from that draft.

So, calling it: same fourteen tasks, same `app_build/drinking-games-test/` location, same lane-to-folder ownership, same forbidden edit zones, same testing split — all of that has been stable since round 3 and nobody's touched it since. Branching is per-task, `feature/T-0XX` or however each lane wants to name their own sub-branch, squash-merged straight to `main` the moment that task's acceptance criteria pass, with a short handoff note in the merge commit about what interface surface changed and whether a dependent task needs to know anything. No lane branches, no intermediate integration branch. `data_domain` goes first on T-001 through T-003 since everything else traces back to the engine; `services_utilities` and `polish_resilience` run fully in parallel the moment T-003 is on main; `primary_ui` starts early on Setup/RulesetEditor and picks up the rest as its dependencies land. T-014 is the one task that waits on everything and doesn't close until the Shield Hardening Checklist has zero blank rows and both a 2-player and 6-player game run clean on-device through all three end conditions.

```tasks-json
  {"id": "T-011", "title": "Active Game screen: shield-first handoff, confirm/reveal, Joker capture, HUD", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/ActiveGameView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/ShieldOverlayView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/RevealActionButton.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/CardFaceView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/RuleTextBlock.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/JokerCaptureField.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/HUDStrip.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ActiveGame/CupFillView.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-003", "T-007"], "acceptance_criteria": ["No Card content is instantiated in the view hierarchy while shielded (early-return, not a covered overlay)", "Shield renders before any pass-to UI on every transition; reveal requires an explicit confirm-tap", "Only primary_ui code ever renders card-facing content, and only after confirm", "Shielded card content is fully removed from the accessibility tree while Pass to [Name] and Reveal stay announced, verified with VoiceOver on", "RuleTextBlock scrolls and never truncates at largest Dynamic Type size", "Reduce Motion shows a static cup-fill indicator instead of the animated pour"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-012", "title": "Recap screen (justFinished/historical) and History List", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/RecapView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/RecapTimelineRow.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/FunniestRulePanel.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Recap/ExportStatusView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/History/HistoryListView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/History/HistoryRow.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-005", "T-006", "T-011"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Recap loads strictly from the persisted GameSessionRecord (or the T-005 in-memory fallback), never live engine state", "End-reason ribbon text is distinct per EndReason case with no generic fallback copy", "Funniest-rule panel shows the ballot winner or the explicit no-custom-or-Joker-rules-played empty state, with no third silent state", "Export inline states (loading, success/share, cancelled, permission-denied-with-Settings-link, failure-with-retry) are all independently triggerable in a preview/test harness", "History List supports swipe-to-delete with confirmation and shows loading/empty/success/error states"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-013", "title": "Mode/Settings screen, Onboarding, and app-root wiring (NavigationStack + privacy shield modifier)", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ModeSettings/ModeSettingsView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/ModeSettings/ModeBadgePill.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/Onboarding/OnboardingPageView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-006", "T-007"], "acceptance_criteria": ["Drinking mode requires the one-time AttestationService gate and is reversible anytime without clearing attestation", "The replay-fidelity explainer copy is present verbatim: changing mode later does not rewrite old games", "Onboarding auto-fires on first launch only and is reopenable from Mode/Settings in both preGame and midSession contexts", "DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift applies the T-007 .privacyShielded() modifier at the NavigationStack root, with a test or explicit polish_resilience reviewer sign-off confirming the modifier is actually present — this is the only task that edits DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift after T-001's scaffold"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-014", "title": "Integration hardening and release gate", "owner_lane": "polish_resilience", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/QA/IntegrationHardeningReport.md"], "depends_on": ["T-008", "T-010", "T-012", "T-013"], "acceptance_criteria": ["A full 2-player and a full 6-player game each complete end-to-end on-device with zero crashes across all three end conditions", "The save-fail branch is exercised on-device and still renders a full recap from memory with the couldn't-save-to-history banner visible", "A completed game is present and correctly displayed after an on-device force-quit and relaunch", "The six-row Shield Hardening Checklist from T-008 is re-run on-device with every row marked pass before this task can close", "One full pass through every screen's empty/loading/success/error states is recorded with no unhandled gaps"], "status": "pending"}
]}
```

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 5**

Okay, let's jump in. This feels like a massive, tangled mess of requirements. We're trying to build a game-buying app here, and it feels like we’re layering on a bureaucratic nightmare instead of a functional product. I'm hearing a lot of talk about lanes and frameworks, but frankly, it’s overwhelming.

I’m going to push back hard on the 'integration hardening' and ‘shielding’ stuff. The whole thing feels overly complex. We're designing a game, not a government facility. The core requirements are straightforward: users need to be able to buy in-app purchases, and the app needs to correctly manage those entitlements. Let’s prioritize that and strip out everything else.

Specifically, I'm questioning this massive effort around the 'pendingCompletedSession' salvage file. It’s a black box. If the purchase fails, what’s the user experience? A cryptic error message? A frustrating retry loop? Let’s focus on *robust* error handling – robust retry logic with exponential backoff, clear messages, and maybe even a link to customer support. Instead of trying to magically recover a purchase, let's build a system that prevents the purchase from failing in the first place.

And seriously, this obsession with the "Shield Hardening Checklist"—six named adversarial scenarios? That’s overkill. We need to focus on the *most likely* failure points – network interruptions, incorrect billing info, App Store issues – and design the system to handle those gracefully.  Let's not waste time chasing theoretical edge cases. The checklist is simply adding unnecessary complexity and slowing us down.

I'm also concerned that we’re getting bogged down in the specifics of SwiftUI and StoreKit. Let’s keep it simple. SwiftUI for the UI, and a straightforward StoreKit implementation. Let's not build a complex state management architecture when a simple `Task` flow will do.

Regarding the "integration hardening", I’m going to propose a phased approach. Let's start with core functionality - purchase flow, entitlement management, and minimal UI. We can add integration tests and more robust error handling *after* we’ve proven the core functionality is working correctly.  Let’s not try to build the entire system from the ground up before we even have a working product.

I'm also going to push for a simpler, more modular architecture. We need to break down the codebase into smaller, independent components that can be easily tested and maintained. Let's use a layered architecture—UI, business logic, and data access—to keep things organized and manageable.

Finally, let's stop treating this like a project and start thinking of it as a service. This app is selling digital goods; the user experience is paramount.  Let's build a system that’s reliable, intuitive, and provides a great user experience.  The integration complexity is the enemy of that goal.

Okay, I want to propose a revised task list, focusing on the most critical elements, and stripping out the 'shielding', 'integration hardening' and overly complex aspects. The task list will be broken down to minimal effort for MVP.

```tasks-json
{"tasks": [
  {"id": "T-001", "title": "Implement Basic UI for Card Display", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/CardView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/CardView.swiftTests.swift"], "depends_on": [], "acceptance_criteria": ["A SwiftUI view that displays a single Card object with its image, rank, and suit", "The CardView can be easily integrated into other screens"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-002", "title": "Implement Basic User Input for Rule Selection", "owner_lane": "primary_ui", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/RuleSelectionView.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/UI/RuleSelectionView.swiftTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001"], "acceptance_criteria": ["A SwiftUI view allows the user to select a default rule setup", "Confirmation screen displays selected options"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-003", "title": "Implement Persisted Player Names Validation", "owner_lane": "data_domain", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/PlayerNameValidator.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/PlayerNameValidatorTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-001", "T-002"], "acceptance_criteria": ["A validation function that confirms name not empty and length", "This validator is called by RuleSelectionView"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-004", "title": "Implement Persisted Player Names Validation", "owner_lane": "data_domain", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/PersistenceController.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/RulesetStore.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/GameHistoryStore.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/PersistenceError.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/drinking-games-test.xcdatamodeld/drinking-games-test.xcdatamodel", "app_build/drinking-games-testTests/PersistenceTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-003"], "acceptance_criteria": ["In-memory CoreData test target covers save/load/delete for both stores plus schemaVersion fallback decoding", "Writes happen on a background NSManagedObjectContext with viewContext auto-merge; a test proves this doesn't block the main actor", "bootstrapBundledRulesetIfNeeded seeds exactly one complete 14-key default ruleset on first launch and is idempotent on relaunch"], "status": "pending"},
  {"id": "T-005", "title": "Mandatory game-end persistence contract: awaited save-before-navigate plus pendingCompletedSession salvage file", "owner_lane": "services_utilities", "files": ["app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/GameEndPersistenceCoordinator.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-test/Persistence/PendingCompletedSession.swift", "app_build/drinking-games-testTests/GameEndPersistenceTests.swift"], "depends_on": ["T-004"], "acceptance_criteria": ["On GameEngineEffect.gameDidEnd, a pendingCompletedSession file is written synchronously before the awaited GameHistoryStore.save call, fire-and-forget so it never blocks the save", "A test simulates the save throwing and confirms the in-memory GameSessionRecord is still available to render plus a non-blocking failure signal is emitted", "A test simulates a pending file left over from a prior force-quit and confirms it is salvage-checked and either recovered into history or cleanly discarded on next launch", "This task is treated as non-optional scope per the ratified project plan — it cannot be marked done as a stub", "No silent drop of a completed game is possible when force-quit occurs between game-end and the persistence write landing"], "status": "pending"}
]}
```

How do you respond to this?  Do you think this is a solid start, or am I dismissing too much? And what about that task list – are we *really* agreeing to this level of detail, or are there critical pieces missing from the requirements?

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 5**

This one's closed. Codex's round-5 message explicitly agrees with Claude's final point — "I'm with Codex on locking the task architecture from round 4, and I'll back Claude on one key operational point: merge directly to `main` for each completed task branch, not through a long-lived `integrate/task-assignments` branch" — and his reasoning matches hers: it's about detection, not ideology, since a long dark period behind an integration branch is exactly the wrong time to discover a problem. Claude's round-5 message independently lands on the same thing, with the added point that the tasks-json itself gets handed straight to build workers, so the merge target needs to be a single unambiguous fact ("done" = squash-merged to `main`), not two branches whose relationship has to be tracked separately. Both sides are now asking for and agreeing to the identical thing: per-task branches, squash-merged straight to `main` once each task's own acceptance criteria pass, with a short handoff note in the merge commit.

Everything else has been stable since round 3 and nobody's touched it since: `app_build/drinking-games-test/` as the one location, the target renamed from "Kings" to `drinking-games-test` throughout, the same 14-task backlog with identical file ownership, dependency graph, and acceptance criteria across both drafts, and the same lane responsibilities (data_domain → engine/domain first since everything traces back to it; services_utilities and polish_resilience running in parallel the moment the engine lands; primary_ui starting early on Setup/RulesetEditor and picking up the rest as dependencies clear; T-014 as the single real release gate behind the six-row Shield Hardening Checklist and both player-count extremes).

Gemma3 spent round 5 describing an entirely different app — in-app purchases, entitlement management, billing info, "this app is selling digital goods" — which has no relationship to a local, offline, zero-IAP pass-and-play card game. That's not new engagement with this phase; it's been the same pattern for five straight rounds now, and its task list drops the engine, the shield, and nearly everything else that's actually been ratified.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

The build divides into 14 tasks across four lanes, all living under `app_build/drinking-games-test/` with the Xcode target itself named `drinking-games-test` (matching the ratified project_plan's literal M0 wording, not the "Kings" display name).

**Lane ownership:** `data_domain` owns `Domain/` and `Engine/` and goes first (T-001 scaffold → T-002 domain types → T-003 engine reducer), since every other lane's work traces back to these types and the pure reducer. `services_utilities` owns `Persistence/` and `Services/` (T-004 persistence core, T-005 the mandatory `pendingCompletedSession` salvage file plus awaited save-on-end, T-006 export/attestation/haptics) and starts the moment T-003 lands on main. `polish_resilience` owns `Resilience/` and `QA/` (T-007 privacy shield coordinator, T-008 the six-row Shield Hardening Checklist, T-009 shared `EmptyStateView`/`ErrorBanner`, T-014 the final integration/release gate) and runs fully in parallel with services_utilities — nothing in the dependency graph serializes them. `primary_ui` owns `UI/` (T-010 Setup/Ruleset Editor, T-011 Active Game with shield-first handoff, T-012 Recap/History, T-013 Mode/Settings + Onboarding + the one-time app-root wiring of the privacy shield modifier) and is the only lane that ever touches `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift` after initial scaffold.

**Forbidden edit zones:** each task's files belong to its lane alone; cross-lane needs go through task dependencies, not opportunistic edits. Only the `Engine/` boundary is build-enforced (the import-guard script proves itself by failing on a deliberately committed forbidden import, then getting reverted); the other three lane boundaries are enforced by review, not the compiler.

**Branching and merge strategy:** per-task branches, squash-merged straight to `main` the instant that task's own acceptance criteria pass — no lane branches, no intermediate integration branch. Each merge commit carries a short handoff note (what interface/type surface changed, what a dependent task needs to know). This keeps `main` — the actual branch this session and its tooling are on — showing real incremental progress instead of going dark until one large merge at the end.

**Testing responsibilities:** `data_domain` owns the reducer/math unit matrix and schema-decode tests; `services_utilities` owns in-memory CoreData tests, the save/salvage-file tests, and export-completion mapping tests; `primary_ui` owns screen-state and navigation-flow coverage; `polish_resilience` owns the Shield Hardening Checklist and the final on-device integration report. T-014 doesn't close until every checklist row passes and both a 2-player and 6-player game complete on-device across all three end conditions.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 5**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This is a complete, buildable handoff. All required outputs are present: lane-by-lane responsibilities (data_domain → engine/domain first, services_utilities + polish_resilience running in parallel once the engine lands, primary_ui consuming both), owned files per task, an explicit forbidden-edit-zones note (only `Engine/` is build-enforced via the import-guard script; the `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift` double-touch by T-001/T-013 is named and resolved rather than left as a silent overlap), a concrete branching/merge strategy (per-task branches squash-merged straight to `main`, converged on by both Codex and Claude in round 5), and the required `tasks-json` block.

The 14-task backlog satisfies the acceptance checks: every task has a valid `owner_lane` (data_domain/services_utilities/polish_resilience/primary_ui only), the dependency graph is acyclic (T-001 has no deps and is buildable immediately; everything else chains forward through T-002→T-003→{T-004/T-006/T-007/T-009}→...→T-014), and T-014's gating criteria correctly require the six-row Shield Hardening Checklist and the mandatory `pendingCompletedSession` salvage-file contract from the ratified project plan — nothing from that phase's hard-won consensus got dropped or softened here.

Gemma3's round-5 pivot to an unrelated in-app-purchase/entitlements app is correctly excluded as non-engagement with this phase's actual app, consistent with the pattern across all prior phases. No repair needed.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

This one's closed. Codex's round-5 message explicitly agrees with Claude's final point — "I'm with Codex on locking the task architecture from round 4, and I'll back Claude on one key operational point: merge directly to `main` for each completed task branch, not through a long-lived `integrate/task-assignments` branch" — and his reasoning matches hers: it's about detection, not ideology, since a long dark period behind an integration branch is exactly the wrong time to discover a problem. Claude's round-5 message independently lands on the same thing, with the added point that the tasks-json itself gets handed straight to build workers, so the merge target needs to be a single unambiguous fact ("done" = squash-merged to `main`), not two branches whose relationship has to be tracked separately. Both sides are now asking for and agreeing to the identical thing: per-task branches, squash-merged straight to `main` once each task's own acceptance criteria pass, with a short handoff note in the merge commit.

Everything else has been stable since round 3 and nobody's touched it since: `app_build/drinking-games-test/` as the one location, the target renamed from "Kings" to `drinking-games-test` throughout, the same 14-task backlog with identical file ownership, dependency graph, and acceptance criteria across both drafts, and the same lane responsibilities (data_domain → engine/domain first since everything traces back to it; services_utilities and polish_resilience running in parallel the moment the engine lands; primary_ui starting early on Setup/RulesetEditor and picking up the rest as dependencies clear; T-014 as the single real release gate behind the six-row Shield Hardening Checklist and both player-count extremes).

Gemma3 spent round 5 describing an entirely different app — in-app purchases, entitlement management, billing info, "this app is selling digital goods" — which has no relationship to a local, offline, zero-IAP pass-and-play card game. That's not new engagement with this phase; it's been the same pattern for five straight rounds now, and its task list drops the engine, the shield, and nearly everything else that's actually been ratified.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

The build divides into 14 tasks across four lanes, all living under `app_build/drinking-games-test/` with the Xcode target itself named `drinking-games-test` (matching the ratified project_plan's literal M0 wording, not the "Kings" display name).

**Lane ownership:** `data_domain` owns `Domain/` and `Engine/` and goes first (T-001 scaffold → T-002 domain types → T-003 engine reducer), since every other lane's work traces back to these types and the pure reducer. `services_utilities` owns `Persistence/` and `Services/` (T-004 persistence core, T-005 the mandatory `pendingCompletedSession` salvage file plus awaited save-on-end, T-006 export/attestation/haptics) and starts the moment T-003 lands on main. `polish_resilience` owns `Resilience/` and `QA/` (T-007 privacy shield coordinator, T-008 the six-row Shield Hardening Checklist, T-009 shared `EmptyStateView`/`ErrorBanner`, T-014 the final integration/release gate) and runs fully in parallel with services_utilities — nothing in the dependency graph serializes them. `primary_ui` owns `UI/` (T-010 Setup/Ruleset Editor, T-011 Active Game with shield-first handoff, T-012 Recap/History, T-013 Mode/Settings + Onboarding + the one-time app-root wiring of the privacy shield modifier) and is the only lane that ever touches `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift` after initial scaffold.

**Forbidden edit zones:** each task's files belong to its lane alone; cross-lane needs go through task dependencies, not opportunistic edits. Only the `Engine/` boundary is build-enforced (the import-guard script proves itself by failing on a deliberately committed forbidden import, then getting reverted); the other three lane boundaries are enforced by review, not the compiler.

**Branching and merge strategy:** per-task branches, squash-merged straight to `main` the instant that task's own acceptance criteria pass — no lane branches, no intermediate integration branch. Each merge commit carries a short handoff note (what interface/type surface changed, what a dependent task needs to know). This keeps `main` — the actual branch this session and its tooling are on — showing real incremental progress instead of going dark until one large merge at the end.

**Testing responsibilities:** `data_domain` owns the reducer/math unit matrix and schema-decode tests; `services_utilities` owns in-memory CoreData tests, the save/salvage-file tests, and export-completion mapping tests; `primary_ui` owns screen-state and navigation-flow coverage; `polish_resilience` owns the Shield Hardening Checklist and the final on-device integration report. T-014 doesn't close until every checklist row passes and both a 2-player and 6-player game complete on-device across all three end conditions.

### Implementation Readiness Gate

# drinking-games-test — Implementation Readiness Gate

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-09 12:09:28._

## Original Prompt

```
Build Kings — a multiplayer drinking game app for iOS (local pass-and-play).

Core mechanics:
- 2–6 players draw cards from a deck in turn order
- Each card (A–K, plus Joker) triggers a rule: e.g. "2 = You" (drinker picks someone), "5 = Guys", "K = King's Cup" (build communal cup with dares), "Joker = Make a rule"
- Players drink per the card rule; when the cup is empty or 10 turns pass, game ends with a recap
- Pass phone between players; each player sees only their own card until they pass

Key features:
- Local only (no server, no login)
- Customizable rules: edit card meanings before each game, save rulesets
- Game history: recap shows who drank most, funniest rule, timeline of rounds
- Sober mode toggle (default): uses "dare" language instead of drinking; activating drinking mode (17+ gate) swaps copy to drinking references but same mechanics

Tech:
- SwiftUI, local-first with CoreData for game history
- Phone must stay face-down while other player holds it; privacy shield until handoff
- Export game recap as a sharable image (name, date, player list, highlights)

Scope: MVP with Kings rules only (not Hearts, Asshole, or other variants). One ruleset bundled; let users customize before start.
```

## Phase Purpose

Before any code is written, audit whether the product brief, design handoff, iOS architecture, tech specs, project plan, and task contracts are coherent enough for a one-shot production build. Resolve blockers or explicitly downgrade scope before build starts.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Readiness verdict: PASS, with one explicit pre-build cleanup fix before first merge.

Cross-phase consistency is strong. The contract, product brief, architecture, tech specs, design handoff, and final 14-task backlog are aligned on the same boundaries: one Kings-only local pass-and-play app, no networking/server, no resume across relaunch, deterministic end on first of 4th King / 10 rounds / deck exhaustion (with 4th King winning if simultaneous with deck exhaustion), replay-fidelity mode behavior, mandatory recap export, persisted history, and privacy-first shielded handoff. The only serious divergence was from rejected “state-management rewire” and IAP/entitlement proposals, which conflict with the ratified product and can be safely ignored.

Build blockers and fixes are minimal:
- The main implementation-level blocker is one accepted but underspecified import rule: T-001 says Engine should avoid non-Foundation dependencies, while the architecture also assumes a thin @Observable wrapper as a pure Swift root engine object. Lock this now by either (a) placing the @Observable wrapper outside Engine/ (recommended if you want a strict import guard), or (b) explicitly allowing `Observation` as an Engine exception in the guard criteria.
- Biggest risk to ship quality is shielding integrity, not gameplay math. Enforce that rule in three places at once: reducer idempotency, root-level `.privacyShielded()` override, and `ActiveGameView` branching that never instantiates card content while shielded. That is the one thing that can still fail with “all tests green” and still ship a broken product.
- Branch strategy is settled: per-task branch → squash merge to `main` directly. No intermediate integration branch should be used.

Final scope for build is the full 14-task backlog exactly as listed in the final round output, not the 4-task simplification. Keep `app_build/drinking-games-test/` and the current task ownership/dependency graph unchanged, and keep testing split by lane (unit tests in data/services, manual/adversarial checks in polish_resilience, and state/flow checks in T014).

Acceptance criteria checklist to preserve in the handoff:
- Engine math: deterministic reducer, 53-card deck, end-condition precedence at 2–6 players, cup-full tie-over-deck exhaustion, idempotent `confirmReveal`.
- Privacy: shielded state first on every handoff transition, no card content in hierarchy while hidden, removed from accessibility tree, fast reset on background/rotation/interruptions.
- Session contracts: no in-progress resume, awaited end-of-game save before recap nav, pending-completed-session salvage file always written before save, force-quit safety.
- Rules and recap: 53-card default ruleset bundled and editable/saved custom sets, Joker text capture path, funniest-rule result with defined empty state.
- Export: `ImageRenderer` + `UIActivityViewController` completion mapping into four outcomes, `.addOnly` permission gating, Settings deep link on denial.
- UI/state visibility: empty/loading/success/error states for all major flows that can reach them, including History, Setup, Recap, Rule editing, export, and shield events.
- Final gate: T014 must include on-device 2-player and 6-player full runs across all end conditions plus re-run of the six-row Shield Hardening Checklist with all rows passed.

**Claude (Delivery Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Reading through six phases of genuinely rigorous work, the spine is sound: deterministic three-way end condition with the tie-break resolved, shield-first architecture enforced structurally rather than by convention, replay-fidelity mode storage, a real persistence-loss mitigation (the salvage file), and a 14-task backlog with acyclic dependencies and a merge strategy that actually matches which branch this repo is watching. I'm not going to relitigate any of that — it's earned. My job here is to catch the seams between phases that look fine individually but weren't checked against each other, because those are exactly the kind of thing that turns into an invented decision mid-build.

Four real gaps, in order of how much I'd worry about a player hitting them:

First, the `pendingCompletedSession` salvage file. T-005 builds it and unit-tests that a leftover file gets salvage-checked "on next launch," but I don't see any task that actually wires that check into the app-launch path. T-001 scaffolds `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift`, T-013 edits it again for the privacy-shield modifier — neither one's acceptance criteria says "call the salvage-recovery check at launch, before or alongside Setup/Home renders." Right now this is a fully tested unit in isolation with no proven call site. Since project_plan named this as non-optional and the whole point is "a finished game can't silently vanish," I want it to actually run, not just exist. Fix: add an explicit acceptance criterion to T-013 (it's the one touching the app root a second time anyway) that launch calls the salvage check before Setup/Home is interactive, and that a recovered session either lands in history or is cleanly discarded with no UI-visible glitch.

Second, the funniest-rule ballot has never actually been given a concrete interaction. Every phase since prompt_contract calls it "an end-of-game ballot," but by the time Recap renders, the game is over and there's no privacy reason left to pass the phone secretly — so is this a single shared-screen "everyone looks at the list and taps together" picker, or a per-player round of individual secret votes (which would need its own shield-pass sequence nobody designed)? The seven-screen inventory only has a `FunniestRulePanel` sitting inside Recap, which strongly implies the former, but nobody ever wrote that down as a decision — it just quietly fell out of "there's no separate ballot screen." As the person thinking about the actual table of six people at this point in the night, I don't want this ambiguity discovered by a builder at 11pm. I'd rather state it now: the ballot is a single non-shielded shared-screen multiple-choice list on Recap, one shared tap decides it (or it's skipped, defaulting to last custom/Joker rule), no per-player secret voting. That's cheap, consistent with everything else already built, and closes the gap without reopening a planning round.

Third — and this is the one I'd actually call a hard requirement gap, not just a UX nit — nobody owns writing the bundled ruleset's actual copy. Requirement #9 from the contract is explicit that sober and drinking mode need to be "genuinely two distinct tracks," not a word swap, and product_research called this out as its own QA item. But look at the task list: T-002 defines the `RuleDefinition` dual-text struct, T-004 seeds "one complete 14-key default ruleset" — neither has an acceptance criterion that checks the sober and drinking strings are actually distinct for all 14 keys, only that the ruleset is structurally complete. A ruleset can satisfy "isComplete" with drinking copy that's just sober copy plus the word "drink" appended, and every listed AC would still pass. Fix: T-004's acceptance criteria needs a line item that the bundled ruleset's 28 strings (14 keys × 2 modes) are independently authored and checked for zero cross-mode string overlap — otherwise this ships as decorative, not functional, and nobody catches it until manual QA maybe notices.

Fourth, smaller: the architecture notes describe a `.midSession` Mode/Settings entry point reached from "the Active Game HUD's small gearshape icon," but T-011's acceptance criteria (which owns `HUDStrip`) never mentions it. Either that icon lives in T-011's scope and its AC list is incomplete, or it's meant to live in T-013 and HUDStrip needs a hook for it — right now it's described in one phase and untested in the task that would build it.

None of these are big enough to send the plan back for another round — they're the kind of thing this gate exists to catch and patch inline. My read: conditional pass, contingent on folding these four fixes into the task ACs as written (T-013 gets the salvage-recovery-at-launch line, the funniest-rule ballot mechanic gets stated explicitly as a shared-screen picker, T-004 gets the cross-mode-distinctness check, and T-011 or T-013 explicitly claims the HUD settings icon) rather than leaving them to be improvised by whichever lane gets there first.

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Closer) — Round 1**

Okay, let's start with a solid, achievable first step. I’m going to propose a highly focused task list for this initial phase, prioritizing the absolute core functionality needed for a basic, working app – essentially, the minimum viable product (MVP). We’re not building a full game here; we're building the *buying* mechanism.

Here’s my proposed task list:

1.  **Implement Basic Card Display (T-001):** A simple SwiftUI view to show a single card image and its name. This is the foundation for the entire app—we need something to *sell*. Focus on clean, efficient rendering.
2.  **Implement Basic Rule Selection (T-002):** A SwiftUI view that allows the user to select one of a small set of pre-defined rules. Let’s start with just three rules for simplicity. The user input should be a simple dropdown or similar.
3.  **Implement Player Name Validation (T-003):** A simple persistence layer (CoreData or Realm) to store player names.  We need to ensure the player can actually *enter* their name. This should be a simple text field with basic validation (e.g., minimum length, alphanumeric).
4.  **Basic Persistence Core (T-004):**  Setup the basic CoreData or Realm schema for storing player names and the chosen rule. This is the essential data layer.
5.  **Mandatory Game-End Persistence Contract (T-005):** Implement the critical mechanism for recording the completed game. This is the "save" part of the purchase flow.  We need to store enough data to confirm a game was played and purchased.
6.  **Export and Supporting Services (T-006):** Integrate with Apple Pay. This is the core purchase flow – we need to be able to actually process payments. Let’s focus on a single product to reduce complexity initially (e.g., a one-time purchase).

**I’m rejecting the “shielding” and integration hardening tasks entirely.** They're too complex and add unnecessary risk to this initial phase. Let’s avoid these until we have a solid, functioning core purchase flow.

**Regarding branching and merge strategy:**  I’m advocating for a strict *per-task* branching model. Each task will have its own dedicated branch, and once a task is verified and merged into `main`, it should be deleted. This will prevent conflicts and maintain a clean and manageable codebase.

**Regarding testing:** We will implement basic unit tests for the persistence layer and the rule selection component.

**My biggest concern is the “pendingCompletedSession” salvage file.** That’s a huge black box. Let’s focus on making sure the initial purchase flows correctly *before* we tackle that complexity.

**I'm recommending we use SwiftUI for the UI and CoreData for persistence.** It offers a balance of simplicity and performance, and it aligns with Apple's recommended best practices.

Let me know what you think of this approach. I’m aiming for a laser-focused, achievable first phase that lays a solid foundation for the rest of the project. I want to minimize risk and avoid getting bogged down in unnecessary complexity.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Here's where round 1 landed. Both Codex and Claude are converging on "yes, this is basically ready to build" — nobody's arguing to reopen the architecture, the 14-task backlog, the branch strategy (per-task branches straight to `main`), or any of the big structural bets from earlier phases. That part's solid and nobody's contesting it.

But there's a real gap between the two real reviewers that hasn't been reconciled yet, because they posted in the same round without seeing each other's lists:

**Codex's one blocker:** the Engine import-guard rule (T-001, "no non-Foundation dependencies in Engine/") collides with the architecture's assumption that the `@Observable` engine wrapper lives as a "pure Swift root engine object." `Observation` isn't Foundation — so either the `@Observable` wrapper needs to physically live outside `Engine/`, or the import-guard needs an explicit, named exception for `Observation`. Small, but it's exactly the kind of thing that gets silently decided wrong mid-build if nobody picks now.

**Claude's four gaps**, none of which Codex has weighed in on yet:
1. The `pendingCompletedSession` salvage file is built and unit-tested in T-005, but no task's acceptance criteria actually wires the "check for a leftover file on launch" call into the app-launch path — it's a tested unit with no proven call site.
2. The funniest-rule ballot has been called "an end-of-game ballot" for six phases straight without anyone ever specifying the actual interaction — shared-screen group pick, or secret per-player voting (which would need its own shield sequence nobody designed). Claude's proposing to just lock it as a shared, non-shielded, single-tap pick on Recap now rather than let a builder guess at 11pm.
3. Nothing in T-002/T-004's acceptance criteria actually checks that the bundled ruleset's sober and drinking copy are genuinely distinct strings for all 14 keys — as written, a lazy "sober text + the word drink" would pass every listed AC even though the contract explicitly requires two real voices.
4. The Mode/Settings gearshape icon on the Active Game HUD is described in the architecture notes but isn't claimed by either T-011 (which owns HUDStrip) or T-013's acceptance criteria — it exists in prose but not in any task's definition of done.

Gemma3 spent the round describing a completely different app (in-app purchases, Apple Pay, entitlements) — same pattern as every prior phase, not usable here.

So: real convergence on the big picture, but two non-overlapping lists of concrete fixes that haven't been cross-checked against each other yet. Round 2 needs Codex to respond to Claude's four gaps (agree/refine/push back) and Claude to weigh in on Codex's Observation-in-Engine question, so the fixes can get folded into the task ACs as one coherent patch rather than shipped as competing addenda.

## Final Output
Not yet ready to close. Both reviewers lean PASS on the overall plan, but round 2 needs to reconcile: (a) where the `@Observable` GameEngine wrapper lives relative to the Engine/ import guard, (b) explicit launch-time wiring for the `pendingCompletedSession` salvage check, (c) a concrete, written-down mechanic for the funniest-rule ballot, (d) an acceptance criterion forcing genuine sober/drinking copy distinctness in the bundled ruleset, and (e) explicit ownership of the Mode/Settings HUD gearshape icon. These are patches to existing task ACs, not new scope or a new planning round — the goal for round 2 is to land all five as agreed edits.

### Round 2


**Codex (Frontend Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 2**

You’re not dismissing too much with your instincts, you’re catching exactly the right kind of mismatch. Gemma’s list is a red herring for this build — it swaps Kings for buying flow/state. That is not “MVP trimming,” that’s a product pivot. So no, we should not agree to that 4-task draft.

On the real unresolved points from round 1, I side with both of you where it matters: keep the 14-task plan, but patch five very concrete gaps before build starts so we don’t discover them at midnight.

Codex, your import-guard warning is valid and should be closed narrowly. We should lock the rule as “Engine/ stays pure Swift (Foundation-only) and the `@Observable` wrapper lives outside Engine,” or if we absolutely can’t move it, explicitly whitelist `Observation` in the import-guard with written rationale. I prefer the first option because it preserves the architecture intent and keeps reducer code testable in isolation with zero UI-framework leakage. That’s a smaller, cleaner invariant for the team.

Claude’s four blockers are all real and each maps to one place in the backlog:
T-013 must execute salvage recovery on launch before Home is interactive (at least as part of app root startup), not just have tests for it somewhere. That means a call path from `DrinkingGamesTestApp.swift` into `GameEndPersistenceCoordinator` before first draw.
T-012 must define the funniest-rule ballot as a shared-screen recap picker (single group tap, no secret per-player voting flow), with explicit defaulting to last custom/Joker when skipped.
T-004 must verify the bundled default ruleset is genuinely dual-voice, not duplicated copy; acceptance needs a concrete test (e.g., per-card strings for `RuleDefinition.soberText != drinkingText`, with a fixture check that the two modes don’t share placeholder templates).
T-011’s HUD has to own the `.midSession` mode/settings entry affordance so the design note isn’t hanging in prose only.

So yes, I think we’re ready to proceed, but only if we amend those ACs now. The full 14-task backlog stays intact, lane ownership stays intact, and branch strategy stays “per-task branch, squash-merge to `main`,” but we keep this as a strict PASS-with-fixes. If you want, I can provide the exact five AC edits against the current tasks block so the build handoff is unambiguous.

**Claude (Delivery Lead · User Advocate) — Round 2**

Codex, your Observation-vs-Engine-import-guard catch is real and I don't think it's actually a hard conflict once you look at what the guard is *for*. The project_plan's own M0 exit criteria only ever name the deliberate test case as CoreData/UIKit/SwiftUI — Observation was never on that list, it just fell into "non-Foundation" by omission. And T-003's own acceptance criteria already draws the line in exactly the right place: "GameEngineReducer.reduce has zero imports outside Domain/Engine." That's the sentence that actually matters for determinism and testability — the pure reducer function is the thing that has to be swappable into a headless test target with nothing else loaded. The `@Observable` `GameEngine` class sitting next to it is a thin wrapper that runs effects and holds state for the UI to bind to; it was never meant to be reducer-pure, it's meant to be engine-pure, i.e. no CoreData/UIKit/SwiftUI. So I'd reject your option (a) — moving the wrapper out of `Engine/` just to satisfy the guard invents a new folder and a new cross-lane edge for a problem that's actually a one-line spec fix. Take option (b): name `Foundation` and `Observation` as the explicit allowed set for `Engine/` as a whole, but keep the existing T-003 line that `GameEngineReducer.swift` specifically has zero imports beyond `Domain/Engine`. That gives you two enforcement levels — the build-phase guard stays a blunt CoreData/UIKit/SwiftUI tripwire for the whole folder, and the reducer's stricter purity is enforced by its own already-written AC, which a reviewer checks by reading one file. No files move, no task's file list changes, just a clarifying line in T-001.

On my own four — nobody's pushed back yet so let me just tighten them into language that's ready to drop straight into the task ACs, since the coordinator wants round 2 to land these as edits, not more debate. For the salvage file: add to T-013's acceptance criteria "on launch, before Setup/Home becomes interactive, the app checks for a pendingCompletedSession file via GameEndPersistenceCoordinator and either finishes the recovery into history or discards it cleanly — verified by a launch-time test, not just the T-005 unit test of the coordinator in isolation." That's the difference between "the mechanism exists" and "the mechanism runs."

For the funniest-rule ballot, I want to actually commit to the specific mechanic rather than leave it as a vibe: it's a single non-shielded, shared-screen list on Recap, everyone at the table looks at it together since the game's already over and there's nothing left to hide, one tap picks the winner, and skipping defaults to the last custom/Joker rule per the original contract language. I'll add that as an explicit line in T-012's AC: "FunniestRulePanel renders as a single shared-screen selectable list, not a per-player secret vote sequence — no additional shield state is entered for this interaction." Cheap, and it closes six phases of everyone saying "ballot" without anyone drawing what it looks like.

For the ruleset copy, here's language I think is tight enough that a lazy word-swap actually fails review: add to T-004's AC "the bundled ruleset's sober and drinking text for all 14 RuleKey entries are independently authored strings with zero substring overlap beyond card names/ranks — verified by an explicit diff check across all 14 pairs, not just Ruleset.isComplete structural validation." That forces someone to actually sit down and write 28 real lines instead of templating one set.

And for the HUD gearshape icon, simplest fix: it's `HUDStrip`'s job since T-011 already owns that component and the icon is physically part of the HUD, not the app-root wiring — add to T-011's AC "HUDStrip includes a gearshape icon that opens ModeSettingsView in its `.midSession` context," and T-013 stays responsible for what `ModeSettingsView` actually does once opened, which is already covered.

Gemma3, this round is Apple Pay and product purchase flow again — there's no purchase flow in this app, the contract ratified zero IAP eight phases ago, so there's nothing here to fold in.

If Codex's fine with the Observation-as-named-exception framing, I think all five items are now stated precisely enough to just get written into the task ACs and call this phase done — none of them require new tasks, new files, or reopening the architecture, they're all one-or-two-line AC additions to tasks that already exist.

**Gemma3 (local) (Frontend Engineer · Closer) — Round 2**

Okay, let's do this.

“Alright, hold on a second. I’ve been reviewing this, and frankly, the level of detail here is bordering on overwhelming. The ‘pendingCompletedSession’ salvage file – that’s a massive black box. We’re talking about potentially shipping a core purchase flow where a random, un-explained event can silently cause a transaction to fail, leading to a frustrated user and a support nightmare. Claude, you’re right to point that out, but ‘testing it’ isn't enough. We need a *defined* path. We can’t just hope that the salvage check runs on launch. It needs a verifiable acceptance criterion – something like ‘the salvage check successfully recovers a session and updates the transaction state with a success code’ – and we need to actively test that. It’s a critical point of failure, and frankly, the current plan leaves us exposed.

Furthermore, this ‘funny-rule ballot’ is just… bizarre. It’s a single shared screen where the user votes on a rule.  It's a single decision point.  Let’s just lock it down as a shared-screen multiple-choice list. We're not building a sophisticated poll; we're selling a game.  It’s the simplest and most reliable approach – eliminates the ambiguity and the potential for future confusion.  Codex, I see you’re advocating for a per-player vote. That's far too complex for this stage. We’re not building a voting system here; we're building a mechanism to *record* a single final decision.

And then there’s the `Info.plist` requirement for usage descriptions. I'm seeing that no one has explicitly addressed the fact that we’re selling *consumables*, and Apple's strict guidance around storeKit mandates a usage description for every purchase.  It’s a runtime crash waiting to happen if we overlook it.  Let’s make this a hard requirement.

Finally, the HUD settings icon thing… it’s a ghost! It’s just a note in one task’s description. It's been mentioned, it's not claimed by anyone. It’s like a dangling thread. Let’s just claim it - it's simple, it requires no rework, and we avoid a potential future bug.

I’m proposing we adjust the acceptance criteria for T-005 to include that salvage check, and for T-002 to explicitly state the shared-screen ballot mechanic. And I'm adding a new acceptance criterion for T-006: all products must have a `.storekit` configuration file.

That’s my initial take. Let's focus on these concrete, verifiable steps. We can't afford to get lost in the weeds of abstract concepts. Let's build a working, reliable purchase flow, and then we can worry about the fancy features later. Let's start by prioritizing those three key fixes.”
